## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The original irreducibility wording is ambiguous, and the author explicitly adopts the standard graph-product interpretation: \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) is connected. That is a reasonable faithful interpretation.

The DKEP normal-bidual criterion is invoked only after factoriality is later checked, which is the correct order: DKEP Lemma 8.5 requires the trivial action to be ergodic on the center, i.e. the algebra must be a factor in this application. DKEP’s Lemma 8.5 indeed gives the normal-bidual criterion in terms of absence of central states on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

### 2. Boundary support calculus

This remains the fatal part.

#### 2.1 Confusion between \(X_{\mathcal H}\) and \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\)

The support projection \(q_X\) used in DKEP Lemma 8.5 is the support of the normal corner associated to \(\mathbb K_X(M)\), not merely the ordinary support of the boundary piece \(X\). DKEP defines \(\mathbb K_X(M)\) separately from \(X\), via the left ideal \(\mathbb K_X^L\), and only then uses \(\mathbb K_X(M)\) in the normal-bidual construction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

In the revised proof, the key argument says “the ordinary support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\)” is the right support of the rectangular module generated by \(T_\xi\), and then uses coefficient identities for \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). But the \(q_{\mathcal H}\) appearing later is \(q_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), i.e. the support of \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\). The draft never proves that the ordinary support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\) agrees with the ordinary support of \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\), nor that coefficient-generator computations for \(X_{\mathcal H}\) control the larger DKEP compact algebra \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(N)\).

This object mismatch is essential.

#### 2.2 The support-sandwich inclusion is still unproved

The proof asserts the crucial inclusion

\[
q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A_Nq_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
\subset q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}
\mathcal A_N
q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\tag{1}
\]

The generator identity

\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})(T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
=
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{*}
T_{\xi_4\otimes\overline{\xi_3}\otimes\eta_2}
\]

is plausible and follows from DKEP’s fusion formula \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). DKEP records that formula in its correspondence section. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

But this identity only handles products of three elementary coefficient operators. It does **not** prove the support-corner inclusion (1). To get (1), one needs to control arbitrary elements of the hereditary algebras and of the weak-* support corners. The proof says this follows from “hereditary closure and weak-* limiting steps” in DKEP Proposition 5.9 and Theorem 5.10, but those cited results do not state such a support-sandwich theorem. Proposition 5.9 characterizes a single rectangular operator \(T\) by the conditions \(T^*T\in \mathbb K_Y\) and \(TT^*\in \mathbb K_X\); Theorem 5.10 gives equivalent definitions of mixing for correspondences relative to boundary pieces. Neither supplies the asserted weak-* support-corner multiplication result. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This is not a minor citation gap: the entire graph-product intersection lemma depends on (1).

#### 2.3 Hereditary closure is not controlled by the displayed triple coefficient identity

DKEP Example 5.11 defines \(X_{\mathcal H}\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) A hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by these coefficients contains norm limits of much more complicated expressions than single coefficients. Products of coefficients of \(\mathcal H\) already naturally lead to higher fusion powers, not just back to \(\mathcal H\).

Therefore the sentence

> “elements of the corner \(e_{\mathcal H}(e_{\mathcal K}A^{**}e_{\mathcal K})e_{\mathcal H}\) are weak-* limits of finite sums of products controlled by (2)”

is a substantial unproved assertion. A rigorous proof would need an explicit lemma showing that the support corner generated by \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}\), and \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\) is dominated by the support for the triple-fusion correspondence. The draft does not prove this, and DKEP does not state it in the cited locations.

#### 2.4 The direct-sum support assertion is still underjustified

The draft now says that if \(\mathcal H=\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\), then \(q_{\mathcal H}\le \bigvee_j q_{\mathcal H_j}\), citing DKEP Theorem 5.10. But Theorem 5.10 is formulated for concrete boundary pieces \(X,Y\), not arbitrary joins of normal-bidual support projections. To use it as written, one must construct a boundary piece whose DKEP support is the join and prove the finite-support vectors are mixing relative to that concrete boundary piece. This is not done.

For finite direct sums, the assertion is plausible via \(2\times2\) positivity; for arbitrary multiplicities in CDHJKN’s decomposition, the infinite direct-sum passage still needs a real proof.

([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But since the support-sandwich inclusion and direct-sum support statement remain unproved, Lemma `intersection of graph-product boundary corners` is not rigorously established. This is fatal: the later palindromic compact-corner inclusion relies exactly on this lemma.

### 6. Final graph-product application

This section is mostly correct, conditional on the boundary intersection lemma.

The amalgamated free-product decomposition over \(M_{\operatorname{Lk}(v)}\), the construction of \(w_1,w_2\), and the vanishing of expectations are all plausible by reduced-word arguments.

The factoriality and nonamenability checks are supported by CDHJKN. Main Theorem 0.5 gives the stated factoriality obstruction, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability characterization for graph products. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices excludes both a universal vertex and an isolated exceptional complement edge.

Thus the final graph-theoretic part is fine **if** Lemma `intersection` is available. But that lemma remains unproved.

---

## Final assessment

The revised proof has improved presentation and fixes several earlier formal issues, but it is still not a complete rigorous solution. The essential missing piece is the boundary support-sandwich calculus. The cited DKEP results define coefficient boundary pieces, characterize relative compactness, and characterize mixing, but they do not provide the asserted support-corner inclusion. The proof also still conflates or at least fails to bridge the gap between the coefficient boundary \(X_{\mathcal H}\) and the DKEP compact algebra \(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\) whose normal support is actually used.

Because the graph-product boundary intersection lemma depends on this unproved support calculus, `answer.tex` is not answer-ready.

## Detailed mathematical audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation of irreducibility as “not a nontrivial join,” equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected, is reasonable and should be explicitly stated because the problem statement’s wording omits some standard qualifiers such as nonempty/proper subgraphs. This part is acceptable.

The statement that DKEP Lemma 8.5 specializes to finite factors with the trivial group action is essentially correct: DKEP Lemma 8.5 assumes a separable finite von Neumann algebra with an action whose restriction to the center is ergodic; for a factor and the trivial action, the center condition is automatic. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 2. Normal support identification \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X\)

This is a **major gap**.

The proof uses the identification
\[
q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X,
\]
where \(e_X\) is the ordinary open support of the boundary piece \(X\subset B(L^2N)\), while \(q_X\) is the support of \((\mathbb K_X(N)^\sharp_J)^*\) in the DKEP normal bidual. DKEP does discuss \(p_{\rm nor}\), ordinary support projections of \(C^*\)-subalgebras, and the resulting normal-bidual corner inclusions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` needs to prove, not merely assert, that the support of \(X\) and the support of \(\mathbb K_X(N)\) agree after cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\).

The given argument is incomplete for several reasons:

- It does not precisely define \(A^\sharp_J\) or show why annihilation by all functionals in \(A^\sharp_J\) characterizes the normal support after cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\).
- It invokes “DKEP’s strong-closure proposition for normal \(N,JNJ\)-bimodular functionals” without a precise theorem number or a statement sufficient to imply the desired annihilator equality.
- It uses convergence in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\), but does not prove that every relevant \(\psi\in A^\sharp_J\) is continuous for the topology in which the closure is taken.
- It does not carefully handle passage from products \(S^*R\) to all of \(\mathbb K_X=(\mathbb K_X^L)^*\mathbb K_X^L\) and then to the normal-bidual support.

This support identification is foundational for all later \(q_U=p_{\rm nor}s_U\) computations. As written, it is not a complete proof.

### 3. The coefficient algebra \(\mathcal B_U\)

The paragraph defining \(\mathcal B_U\) contains several unproved assertions.

The author defines \(\mathcal B_U\) as the norm-closed linear span of coefficients coming from nonempty finite Connes fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\), \(Z\subset U\), and then says DKEP’s identities imply this is a \(C^*\)-algebra. DKEP does have the identities \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\) and \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But to conclude that the closed linear span is a \(C^*\)-algebra, one must check products of coefficients from possibly different fusions and show the resulting operators are again in the prescribed span. This is not fully written.

The statement that \(\mathcal B_U\) has the same ordinary support as \(X_{M_U}\) is also under-justified. The author uses the fact that \(P_L\in P_U B(L^2M)P_U\) when \(L\subset U\), but does not carefully prove that this implies \(X_{M_L}\subset X_{M_U}\) in DKEP’s boundary-piece sense. DKEP’s \(X_B\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by operators of the form \(xJyJe_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The inclusion is plausible, but the proof in `answer.tex` is only a sketch.

### 4. Lemma: intersection of graph-product boundary corners

This is the **central fatal gap**.

The lemma claims
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
The proof reduces this to the product-support inclusion
\[
\mathcal B_W\mathcal B_U\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_U.
\]

The key unsupported step is the sentence:

> Therefore the first factor on the right of (6) is supported by \(s_I\), while \(T_{\eta_2}\) is supported by \(s_U\), and the first inclusion in (5) follows.

This is not automatic. From knowing that a vector belongs to a correspondence whose coefficient boundary has support \(s_I\), one only immediately controls coefficient operators such as \(T_\alpha^*T_\beta\). To conclude that a **rectangular operator** \(T_\alpha^*\) lies in \(s_I A^{**}\), or that \(T_{\eta_2}\) lies in \(A^{**}s_U\), one needs a rectangular support lemma: for example, a proof that both \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) and \(T_\alpha T_\alpha^*\) are controlled by the relevant support, and that this implies the desired one-sided support relation. No such lemma is stated or proved.

This is not a minor omission: the entire support-sandwich argument depends on this rectangular support step.

The use of CDHJKN fusion rules is also too compressed. CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 give precise decompositions of \(M_{V_1}\)-\(M_{V_2}\) bimodules and their fusions. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) The author’s repeated-iteration use for arbitrary finite fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z\) and contragredients is plausible, but it is not written carefully enough to cover arbitrary bounded vectors in direct sums, off-diagonal coefficients, or infinite multiplicities.

Finally, the cut by \(p_{\rm nor}\) uses the assertion that all supports \(s_U\) commute with \(p_{\rm nor}\). DKEP gives such commutation for ordinary supports of appropriate \(C^*\)-subalgebras satisfying the normal multiplier hypotheses. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` does not verify those hypotheses for the newly defined \(\mathcal B_U\), especially simultaneous \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-bimodularity and faithful multiplier actions.

### 5. Finite palindromic boundary criterion

This lemma is one of the more convincing parts, but it still has some missing details.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}q_i^\perp\subset\widetilde S(N)\) is plausible because \(q_i\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\). The GNS argument with the palindromic product is also plausible.

However, the final hypertrace step relies on a u.c.p. map
\[
\Theta:B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 does produce natural u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` should explicitly state the exact DKEP construction being used and why it is \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular in the required sense. This is likely fixable, but as written it is still a cited black box with insufficient precision.

### 6. Relative Bass–Serre paradox proposition

The proposition is plausible but not rigorous enough as written.

The reduced-word commutator formulas
\[
[p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0
\]
are asserted by “direct inspection.” For a self-contained proof at this level, one must check them on all relevant reduced-word summands in the amalgamated-free-product \(L^2\)-decomposition, including the one-letter and \(B\)-summand cases. The proof also needs to explicitly show that these commutators lie in the relative compact boundary piece \(X_B\) or its \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure, so that DKEP Lemma 6.1 applies. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is about when commutator conditions on a dense generating set imply proper proximality of a point. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) `answer.tex` does not spell out the membership in the correct \(X_B\)-mixing/compact space.

The inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
also need a careful \(B\)-valued inner-product proof. Orthogonality of the two images is asserted from \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\), but the proof should verify this on arbitrary \(Q\)-initial reduced-word vectors.

The “\(B\)-Haar” step for \(h=w_1a\) is plausible, but the proof should explicitly cover negative powers and show that the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal by computing \(E_B(h^{m-n})=0\).

These are not merely stylistic omissions: Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\) is the only source of relative proper proximality for the graph-product links, so it must be completely rigorous.

### 7. Application to graph products

The graph-theoretic part is mostly sound.

I cross-checked the elementary combinatorial implications by brute force for graphs with \(3\le |V|\le 6\): complement connectedness implies every vertex has a non-neighbor; in the singleton \(C(v)\) case there is an \(s\in\operatorname{Lk}(v)\) nonadjacent to the unique \(r\in C(v)\); the intersection of all links is empty; and connected complement with at least three vertices has no isolated complement vertex or isolated complement edge. This supports the author’s graph-combinatorial reductions.

The use of CDHJKN’s factoriality criterion is valid in spirit. CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 says factoriality fails only through universal vertices with nonfactor vertex algebra or through an exceptional nonedge pair with both dimensions \(2\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) In a connected complement graph with at least three vertices, those obstructions are indeed impossible.

The use of CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 for nonamenability is also valid in spirit: amenability would force every nonedge pair to be a two-dimensional exceptional pair adjacent to all other vertices, which is incompatible with connected complement on at least three vertices. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

However, because the earlier relative-proximality and boundary-intersection steps are not fully proved, the final application cannot be certified.

---

## Summary of blocking issues

The solution is **not answer-ready** because of the following essential unresolved gaps:

1. The identification \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X\) for arbitrary boundary pieces is not rigorously proved.
2. The construction and support equality for \(\mathcal B_U\) are under-justified.
3. The boundary-corner intersection lemma relies on an unproved rectangular-support principle.
4. The use of CDHJKN fusion rules in the support argument is not detailed enough to handle arbitrary bounded vectors/direct sums/off-diagonal coefficients.
5. The relative Bass–Serre proposition is an essential new lemma but is proved only by sketches of reduced-word and orthogonality arguments.
6. Therefore the palindromic criterion cannot be applied to the graph-product boundary pieces as claimed.

The theorem may well be true, and parts of the strategy look promising, but `answer.tex` does not yet constitute a complete rigorous proof.

## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This part is acceptable. The author explicitly interprets irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This is the standard graph-product interpretation and resolves the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. DKEP normal-bidual setup

The general definitions are broadly consistent with DKEP. The use of Lemma 8.5 for a separable finite factor with trivial action is acceptable, since the center is trivial and hence the ergodic-center hypothesis is automatic. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

But the support notation is still too compressed. The proof needs a precise justification of the passage from ordinary open support projections in \(B(L^2N)^{**}\) to the support projections \(q_X\) in DKEP’s normal bidual. This is not merely notational; it is used crucially in Lemma \(\ref{lem:intersection}\).

### 3. Boundary support calculus

The new support lemma (2) is mathematically reasonable: if \(BC\subset eA^{**}s\) and \(CB\subset sA^{**}e\), the approximate-unit argument does give
\[
r(sA^{**}s)r\subset eA^{**}e.
\]
The added rectangular lemma is also basically correct: if \(T^*T\in D\), then \(T=Td\) for the support \(d\) of \(D\).

However, the first support assertion remains problematic. The paper states:

\[
(B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_BA_N^{**}s_Bp_{\rm nor}.
\tag{1}
\]

This is not proved in `answer.tex`, and the cited DKEP Proposition 2.10 does not visibly state this formula. It may be derivable with extra work, but that derivation is essential and absent. Without it, the equality
\[
q_U=p_{\rm nor}s_U
\]
is not justified.

### 4. The definition and identification of \(\mathcal B_U\)

This is still a major gap. The proof claims:

\[
\mathcal B_U=K_{X_{M_U}}(M).
\]

This is an essential lemma, but it is not proved. The argument says it follows from the definitions, the \(T\)-operator identities, and CDHJKN fusion. That is not enough.

Specific problems:

- DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_{M_U}\) using all operators in the corner \(e_{M_U}B(L^2M)e_{M_U}\), not only rank-one-type coefficient operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) One must prove that the finite-fusion coefficient algebra described by the author really recovers the DKEP hereditary algebra \(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).
- DKEP Example 5.11 gives a coefficient-boundary construction for a correspondence, but it does not automatically identify \(K_X\) for \(X=X_{M_U}\) with the norm-closed algebra generated by all finite Connes fusions of \(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- The proof does not handle off-diagonal coefficients arising from direct-sum decompositions carefully.
- The proof does not justify that coefficients of arbitrary bounded vectors in possibly infinite direct sums are norm limits of coefficients of finite-support vectors.

The last point is particularly serious. The sentence

> “Finite-support vectors in these direct sums are dense, and the coefficient maps are norm continuous”

is not valid in the form needed. Finite-support vectors are dense in Hilbert norm, but \(T_\xi\) is controlled by the bounded-vector/right-bounded norm, not merely by Hilbert norm. In infinite direct sums, truncations of a bounded vector need not converge in the operator norm of the associated \(T_\xi\). Thus the passage from finite-support vectors to arbitrary bounded vectors is not justified.

### 5. Intersection lemma

The revision addresses part of my previous objection by adding the rectangular support lemma. That helps with the formal step
\[
T_\alpha^*T_{\eta_2}\in s_IA^{**}s_U
\]
provided one already knows
\[
T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\in\mathcal B_I,\qquad T_{\eta_2}^*T_{\eta_2}\in\mathcal B_U.
\]

But the proof of \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\in\mathcal B_I\) still depends on the unresolved direct-sum/bounded-vector issue above. CDHJKN gives decomposition and fusion rules at the bimodule level, but the proof must still show that the corresponding coefficient supports behave correctly under arbitrary bounded vectors and infinite multiplicity direct sums. The current draft does not.

Therefore Lemma \(\ref{lem:intersection}\), the central boundary-corner inclusion
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W},
\]
remains unproved.

### 6. Finite palindromic criterion

This part is substantially improved and is likely correct modulo standard DKEP facts.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\) is plausible. The GNS argument with the palindromic product is also sound. The final hypertrace step is supported by DKEP’s Lemma 8.5 proof, where a natural map from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual \(K_X\)-corner is used. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Still, the proof should explicitly justify normality of the compressed restriction to \(N\), for example by domination by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\). This is fixable and not the main obstruction.

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre proposition

The revised argument is much clearer than before. The reduced-word commutator formulas, the inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q,
\]
and the \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) are now plausible.

However, the proof is still somewhat compressed. In particular, the claim that DKEP Lemma 6.1 directly gives \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\) requires a precise identification of the relevant \(X_B\)-mixing space with the commutator target. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is stated for dual normal \(M\)-bimodules and \(X\)-mixing/proximal points, not directly as the simple criterion used here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This is probably repairable, but as written it is still a black-box application.

That said, the Bass–Serre proposition is less problematic than the boundary-corner intersection lemma.

### 8. Application to graph products

The graph-theoretic part is sound. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from complement-connectedness is correct. The AFP splitting over the link is standard.

The final factor and nonamenability checks are also essentially correct. CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives the relevant factoriality obstruction, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability characterization; connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices rules out the exceptional isolated complement vertex/edge cases. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But because the preceding relative-proximality and boundary-intersection machinery is not rigorously established, the final conclusion does not yet follow.

## Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The author explicitly adopts the standard graph-product interpretation of irreducibility as nontrivial join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. That resolves the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. DKEP normal-bidual setup

The general notation is mostly consistent with DKEP. However, the revised draft uses \(p_{\rm nor}\) later without defining it in the introduction. Since \(p_{\rm nor}\) is essential to the claimed formula \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}s_X\), it should be explicitly defined in the paper, not merely invoked.

The use of DKEP Lemma 8.5 for a separable finite factor is appropriate: the trivial action has ergodic center because the algebra is a factor. DKEP’s Lemma 8.5 indeed gives the normal-bidual criterion in terms of absence of a central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 3. Support formula \(q_X=q(X)\)

This is still a major weak point.

The proof states that for a boundary piece \(X\), Proposition 3.6 implies that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure, and since \(\sharp_J\)-functionals are continuous for this closure, they have the same annihilator in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\). This is plausible but not fully justified as written.

DKEP Proposition 3.6 says that \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) is both the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(X\) and of \(K_X\), and identifies it as a strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule. But `answer.tex` still needs to explain precisely why this implies equality of annihilators in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\), and hence equality of normal-bidual support projections. This is an essential bridge, not a harmless notational point. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Similarly, the formula
\[
(B^{\sharp_J})^*=q(B)\mathcal A_N q(B),\qquad q(B)=p_{\rm nor}s_B,
\]
is still not proved in sufficient detail. DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives inclusions/intersections of normal biduals for simultaneous normal categories, but the author’s precise ordinary-open-support cutdown formula requires a derivation. The revised text says it follows from the paragraph before DKEP Remark 2.8 plus Proposition 2.10, but it does not spell this out. This remains a gap because the entire boundary-corner intersection lemma depends on replacing \(q_U\) by \(p_{\rm nor}s_U\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 4. Coefficient model for \(X_{M_U}\)

The revision avoids the earlier assertion \(\mathcal B_U=K_{X_{M_U}}\), which is good. However, it replaces it with another nontrivial assertion:

> DKEP Examples 3.4 and 5.11 identify \(X_{M_U}\) with the hereditary algebra generated by coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of bounded vectors in Hilbert amplifications of \(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).

This is plausible, but it is not literally stated by the cited examples in this form. DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_B\) using the corner \(e_BB(L^2M)e_B\), and Example 5.11 defines coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences; it also notes that a certain correspondence model recovers Example 3.4. But the present proof needs a precise proof that the basic-construction correspondence \(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), with Hilbert amplifications, gives exactly the DKEP \(X_{M_U}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The sentence “the amplifications account for the whole corner \(e_{M_U}A_Me_{M_U}\)” is an important lemma, not a self-evident fact. It should be proved or cited precisely. Without it, the subsequent reduction to products of coefficient operators is not justified.

### 5. Infinite direct sums

The new direct-sum paragraph is an improvement over the prior draft. The use of finite-coordinate truncations and strong-* convergence of the coefficient operators is a plausible way to avoid the earlier incorrect norm-convergence claim.

However, this still needs more detail. In particular:

- One must verify that coordinate projections in the direct sum preserve bounded vectors and that the associated \(T_{\xi_F}\) satisfy \(T_{\xi_F}\to T_\xi\) strong-*.
- The off-diagonal coefficient support argument uses the rectangular support lemma but should explicitly identify the hereditary subalgebras to which the diagonal coefficients belong.
- The proof states the direct-sum fact for “coefficient algebras supported by \(s\),” but later uses it to conclude membership in \(sA^{**}s\), then applies a rectangular lemma originally stated for membership in concrete hereditary algebras. This mismatch should be fixed.

This is likely repairable, but as written it is still too compressed for a central lemma.

### 6. Boundary-corner intersection lemma

This remains the central unresolved issue.

The proof aims to establish
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]

The CDHJKN fusion input is appropriate in spirit: \(\mathcal H_W\otimes_M\overline{\mathcal H_W}\otimes_M\mathcal H_U\) should decompose into summands \(\mathcal H_Z\) with \(Z\subset U\cap W\), by repeated use of the fusion rule. CDHJKN Proposition 5.5 indeed gives this type of rule. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But the proof still has several gaps:

1. **Coefficient density in \(X_{M_U}\)** is not established. The proof says it is enough by norm closure to multiply two amplified coefficient operators, but that requires the amplified coefficients to be norm-linearly dense in \(X_{M_U}\). This is not proved.

2. **The product identity is used beyond its stated domain.** The identity
   \[
   (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
   =
   T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}
   \]
   is standard for bounded vectors, but the proof must explicitly handle Hilbert amplifications and the relevant boundedness of the fused vector.

3. **The rectangular support lemma is applied in a stronger form than stated.** The lemma is stated for \(T^*T\in D\) and \(S^*S\in C\), where \(D,C\) are hereditary subalgebras. In the proof, the author only obtains
   \[
   T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\in s_IA^{**}s_I,\qquad
   T_{\eta_2}^*T_{\eta_2}\in s_UA^{**}s_U.
   \]
   To apply the stated lemma, one must either show these concrete operators lie in \(X_{M_I}\) and \(X_{M_U}\), respectively, or restate/prove the lemma in the stronger support-corner form. This is currently missing.

4. **The cut by \(p_{\rm nor}\)** depends on the unresolved support formula \(q_U=p_{\rm nor}s_U\). Thus even if the ordinary-envelope inclusion were accepted, the normal-bidual inclusion is not yet rigorously obtained.

Because Lemma \(\ref{lem:intersection}\) is essential for the finite palindromic criterion, this gap prevents certification of the whole proof.

### 7. Finite boundary criterion

This section is now mostly convincing.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\) is sound in outline, and the author has fixed the normality point by domination by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\). The GNS argument with the palindromic product is also valid.

The final hypertrace step still cites “DKEP’s natural normal-bidual map” without giving the exact construction. DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 does produce natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the relevant normal-bidual corner, so this is probably acceptable if cited more precisely. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This part is not the main obstruction.

### 8. Relative Bass–Serre proposition

This part is improved, but still somewhat compressed.

The reduced-word commutator formulas are plausible and match the free-product argument used in DKEP’s free-product section. DKEP Lemma 6.1 supports the general principle that checking commutators on an ultraweakly dense generating algebra suffices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the proof still does not fully spell out:

- why the displayed commutators are \(X_B\)-mixing in exactly the DKEP sense, rather than merely lying in \(X_B\);
- why the images \(w_1qL^2N\) and \(w_2qL^2N\) are orthogonal for all reduced \(Q\)-initial vectors;
- why \(apa^*\le q\) in the full AFP decomposition, including all word-length cases.

These are standard reduced-word verifications and probably repairable, but they are not written at full referee-level rigor.

### 9. Application to graph products

The graph-product application is mostly correct, assuming the previous machinery.

The AFP splitting over the link is standard. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) is correct. The expectation computations onto \(M_{L_v}\) are plausible by graph-product reduced-word calculus.

The factoriality and nonamenability checks using CDHJKN are correct in spirit. Main Theorem 0.5 rules out factoriality failures except universal vertices or exceptional nonedge pairs, and Proposition 6.3 characterizes amenability by vertex amenability plus the isolated two-dimensional nonedge condition. Connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices rules these out. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

---

## Previous concerns: addressed vs remaining

### Addressed or substantially improved

- The author no longer asserts \(\mathcal B_U=K_{X_{M_U}}\) as directly as before.
- The infinite-direct-sum issue is addressed more intelligently using strong-* convergence rather than operator-norm convergence.
- The finite palindromic criterion now includes the normality domination argument.
- The Bass–Serre argument has more reduced-word detail.
- The final CDHJKN factor/nonamenability argument is clear and essentially correct.

### Still unresolved

- The equality \(q_X=q(X)\), i.e. computing the DKEP normal-bidual support from the original boundary piece \(X\), remains underproved.
- The formula \(q(B)=p_{\rm nor}s_B\) is still not derived carefully enough from DKEP.
- The coefficient model \(X_{M_U}=\) “amplified coefficient boundary of \(\mathcal H_U\)” is asserted but not fully proved.
- The boundary-corner intersection lemma still depends on unproved coefficient-density and support-corner arguments.
- The rectangular support lemma is applied in a stronger form than stated.

### New or reintroduced issues

- \(p_{\rm nor}\) is used but not explicitly defined in the revised `answer.tex`.
- The proof now relies heavily on “Hilbert amplifications account for the whole corner \(e_{M_U}A_Me_{M_U}\),” but this is not proved.
- The support lemma (2) and its later use are not perfectly aligned: the lemma assumes membership in hereditary subalgebras, while the proof obtains only support-corner membership.

---

## Final assessment

The revised draft is closer to a viable proof, and much of the strategy is plausible. But as a strict referee, I cannot regard the central boundary-support intersection lemma as fully proved. Since that lemma is essential to pass from relative proper proximality over the vertex links to ordinary proper proximality, the solution is **not yet answer-ready**.

## Referee report

### Overall verdict

`answer.tex` **does not yet constitute a complete rigorous solution**. It compiles and satisfies the formal LaTeX contract, and the high-level strategy is plausible. However, the proof has several essential gaps in the operator-algebraic support calculus underlying Lemma 1, especially the identification of the DKEP normal support \(q_X\) with the ordinary support of the original boundary piece \(X\), and the use of DKEP Proposition 2.6 / Proposition 3.6 for an annihilator statement they do not state. Since Lemma 1 is central to the finite-boundary reduction, these are not cosmetic issues.

---

## 1. LaTeX contract audit

I reconstructed `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex` twice. The document compiled successfully to a **5-page PDF**, with no unresolved references after the second run.

Contract checks:

- Uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- Uses `fullpage`, which is permitted.
- No `geometry`, `a4wide`, `typearea`, manual margin changes, line-spacing changes, or in-document font-size changes.
- PDF is at most 12 pages.

So there is **no LaTeX-contract failure**.

## 2. Literature validation

The two cited papers exist and are relevant.

- DKEP’s paper *Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras* is the intended source for the normal-bidual formulation, boundary pieces, \(S_X(M)\), and Lemma 8.5. DKEP Lemma 8.5 states, under separability and ergodicity of the action on the center, that proper proximality relative to \(X\) is equivalent to absence of an \(M\)-central invariant state on the normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). For the trivial action on a factor, the author’s specialized use is broadly consistent. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN’s graph-product paper is correctly identified as containing the relevant graph-product structure results. It states the fusion decomposition for the bimodules \(H_U(V_1,V_2)\) and gives the fusion rule over \(W\subset U_1\cap U_2\). ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf)) It also states the factoriality criterion in Main Theorem 0.5 and the amenability criterion in Proposition 6.3. ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf))

However, several **specific uses** of DKEP in `answer.tex` are not justified by the cited results as written.

---

## 3. Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The author explicitly adopts the standard interpretation of irreducibility as “not a nontrivial join,” equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected. This is reasonable and necessary because the original problem statement is imprecise about nonempty/proper subgraphs. This part is acceptable.

The assumption that traces are faithful normal traces is also standard for graph products of tracial von Neumann algebras.

### DKEP normal-bidual setup

The statement of DKEP Lemma 8.5 is broadly acceptable after specializing to the trivial group action and a factor. DKEP’s actual Lemma 8.5 includes a group action and assumes ergodicity on the center; for a finite factor and the trivial action this condition is satisfied. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No fatal issue here, though the paper should explicitly say “we apply Lemma 8.5 to the trivial group action; ergodicity on \(Z(N)\) follows because \(N\) is a factor.”

---

## 4. Major issue: the support-calculus paragraph is not rigorous

This is the most serious problem.

### 4.1 Incorrect statement that \(p_{\rm nor}\) is central

`answer.tex` says:

> Let \(p_{\rm nor}\in A_N^{**}\) be the central projection implementing the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal representation...

This is not correct as stated. DKEP define \(p_{\rm nor}\) as the projection associated to states whose restriction to \(M\) is normal, and they identify \((A^\sharp)^*\) with \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\). But DKEP explicitly warn that the canonical embedding \(A\to (A^\sharp)^*\) is not generally a \(*\)-homomorphism because \(p_{\rm nor}\) need not be central. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This matters because the proof repeatedly cuts ordinary \(A^{**}\)-support inclusions by \(p_{\rm nor}\). Such cutdowns can be valid if one proves the needed commutation with the particular support projections, but the text’s blanket “central projection” statement is false and cannot be used as a shortcut.

### 4.2 The formula \(q(B)=p_{\rm nor}s_B\) in the simultaneous \(N,JNJ\)-normal setting is not proved

The author claims that DKEP’s paragraph before Remark 2.8 plus Proposition 2.10 give

\[
(B^{\sharp_J})^*=q(B)\mathcal A_Nq(B),\qquad q(B)=p_{\rm nor}s_B.
\]

DKEP’s one-normal discussion does say that for an \(M\)-\(C^*\)-subalgebra \(B\), the support \(q_B\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\) and yields a corner inside \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But DKEP Proposition 2.10, in the simultaneous category, is stated as an inclusion/intersection result for the two normal structures; it does not, by itself, explicitly give the full support formula claimed in `answer.tex`. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This may be derivable, but `answer.tex` does not give the derivation. Since the later proof depends on cutting ordinary support inclusions by \(p_{\rm nor}\), this is an essential missing lemma.

### 4.3 Misuse of DKEP Proposition 2.6 and Proposition 3.6

The text says:

> Proposition 3.6 and the strong-bimodule annihilator criterion of Proposition 2.6 imply that every \(\sharp_J\)-functional vanishing on \(X\) vanishes on \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), hence on \(K_X(N)\).

This is not supported by the cited statements.

- DKEP Proposition 2.6 is Magajna’s bidual identification \(X^{\natural\natural}\cong (X^\sharp)^*\). It is not stated as a “strong-bimodule annihilator criterion.” ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Proposition 3.6 says \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) is the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(K_X\) and is a closed strong bimodule. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) It does not by itself say that every \(\sharp_J\)-functional vanishing on \(X\) vanishes on \(K_X\).

The conclusion that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same annihilator in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\) is essential because DKEP’s support \(q_X\) is the support of \((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\), whereas the proof later computes supports using the original coefficient boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\). This identification is not proved.

A valid proof would need a precise statement such as:

\[
\operatorname{Ann}_{A^{\sharp_J}}(X)=\operatorname{Ann}_{A^{\sharp_J}}(K_X(N)),
\]

with a proof using the exact topology defining \(A^{\sharp_J}\) and DKEP’s relative compactness results. The current citations do not provide that.

This is a **fatal gap** for the present solution.

---

## 5. Coefficient boundary \(X_{M_U}=X_{\mathcal H_U}\)

The identification of \(X_{M_U}\) with the DKEP coefficient boundary of

\[
\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M
\]

is plausible. DKEP Example 3.4 defines the boundary piece associated to a subalgebra \(B\subset M\) using the Jones projection \(e_B\), and Example 5.11 defines the coefficient boundary \(X_H\) of a correspondence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the proof in `answer.tex` is compressed. In particular, the identity \(T_{\Omega_U}=e_{M_U}\) is nontrivial and should be computed explicitly from DKEP’s definition of \(T_\xi\). This is probably repairable, but as written it is more of a sketch than a rigorous proof.

---

## 6. Special approximate units and finite-fusion coefficients

The claim that \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive approximate unit whose elements are norm limits of finite sums of coefficients of finite fusions of \(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\) is plausible but insufficiently justified.

The argument requires several nontrivial facts:

1. \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by positive coefficient generators.
2. Functional calculus of finite positive sums can be approximated by polynomials without constant term.
3. Products of coefficient operators are again coefficient operators of finite Connes fusions, using \(T_{\bar \xi}=T_\xi^*\) and \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\).
4. CDHJKN’s fusion rules apply exactly to all such finite fusions in the form needed.

DKEP does give the formula \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) CDHJKN gives the needed type of fusion rule. ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf)) But the passage from arbitrary approximate units in the hereditary algebra to the specific finite-fusion coefficient form is not written with enough detail for a strict proof.

This issue is not necessarily fatal by itself, but it compounds the support-identification gap above.

---

## 7. Lemma “boundary-corner intersection”

The intended statement

\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_M q_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}
\]

is the core technical engine of the proof. The high-level strategy is sensible: multiply finite-fusion coefficients, use the coefficient calculus, apply CDHJKN fusion, and pass to supports.

But the proof depends on the unproved support equality

\[
q_{X_{M_U}}=p_{\rm nor}s_U
\]

where \(s_U\) is the ordinary support of the original boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\). As explained above, this is not established.

There is also an additional technical issue: the proof says that the vector

\[
\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1
\]

decomposes into summands \(\mathcal H_Z\) with \(Z\subset U\cap W\). This is plausible by repeated use of CDHJKN Proposition 5.5, but the proof does not spell out the induction or the exact specialization of CDHJKN’s \(H_U(V_1,V_2)\) notation to the full \(M\)-\(M\) correspondences being used. CDHJKN’s fusion rule does indeed decompose over \(W\subset U_1\cap U_2\), but the author needs to explicitly identify their \(\mathcal H_U\) with the \(H_U(V,V)\) of CDHJKN. ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf))

Because Lemma 1 is used to force the palindromic product into the compact corner, this gap is fatal unless repaired.

---

## 8. Finite boundary criterion

This lemma is mostly sound conditional on the DKEP normal-bidual framework.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\) is plausible because DKEP states that the support projection of \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\) commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The GNS argument using the palindromic positive contraction is also a good way to avoid non-normal meet problems.

One point should be expanded: the final hypertrace construction invokes “the u.c.p. map from \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\) to \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) used in DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5.” This is probably correct, but a standalone proof should state the map and its properties, rather than relying on an implicit object inside another proof.

This is a minor-to-moderate exposition gap, not the main fatal issue.

---

## 9. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The AFP argument is plausible. The projections \(p,q\) onto reduced words with first \(P\)- or \(Q\)-letter are standard, and the commutator formulas with right multiplication are credible. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is indeed the type of result allowing one to verify \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\) from commutator conditions on an ultraweakly dense \(*\)-subalgebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, this proposition is written as a compressed proof. A strict proof should include:

- A precise AFP Fock-space decomposition.
- Verification of the commutator formulas on each reduced-word sector.
- A proof that \(q w_1^*w_2 q=0\) follows from \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\).
- A proof that \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar for all positive and negative powers.

These details are likely repairable. I would not reject the proof solely for this section, but it is not fully detailed.

The final invocation of DKEP Theorem 6.2 is acceptable in spirit: DKEP Theorem 6.2 gives equivalent formulations involving \(B\)-central states on \(S_X(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

## 10. Application to graph products

The graph-product application is mostly correct, assuming the earlier lemmas.

The AFP splitting

\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]

is standard for graph products. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from complement-neighbors of \(v\) is combinatorially correct.

The argument that \(\bigcap_v L_v=\emptyset\) is correct because no vertex lies in its own link.

The identification \(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\) is correct in spirit: \(M_\emptyset=\mathbb C\), so the Jones projection is rank one and its \(M,JMJ\)-translates generate the compact operators.

The factoriality and nonamenability arguments are supported by CDHJKN:

- Main Theorem 0.5 says factoriality can fail only through universal vertices whose vertex algebra is not a factor, or through exceptional two-dimensional non-edge pairs adjacent to all other vertices. ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf))
- Proposition 6.3 characterizes amenability by vertex amenability plus the condition that every non-edge pair is an isolated two-dimensional exceptional pair. ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf))

Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected with at least three vertices, an isolated complement vertex or isolated complement edge is impossible. This part is sound.

---

## 11. Summary of essential gaps

The answer is not ready because the following essential points remain unproved or incorrectly cited:

1. **False statement:** \(p_{\rm nor}\) is called central, but DKEP explicitly warn that \(p_{\rm nor}\) need not be central. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))  
2. **Unproved support cutdown formula:** The claimed simultaneous formula
   \[
   (B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_B\,\mathcal A\,p_{\rm nor}s_B
   \]
   is not directly stated by DKEP Proposition 2.10 and is not proved.
3. **Mis-cited annihilator equality:** DKEP Proposition 2.6 and Proposition 3.6 do not establish that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same annihilator in \(A^{\sharp_J}\). This is essential for computing \(q_X\) from the original boundary piece.
4. **Core Lemma 1 depends on that unsupported support identification.** Therefore the palindromic compact-corner inclusion is not rigorously established.
5. Several technically nontrivial coefficient/fusion and approximate-unit assertions are sketched but not fully proved.

Because these gaps affect the central mechanism of the proof, `answer.tex` is not a complete rigorous solution.

## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Verdict

The revision fixes several earlier problems, especially the incorrect “central \(p_{\rm nor}\)” shortcut and the misuse of DKEP Proposition 2.6. However, `answer.tex` is **still not answer-ready**. The main remaining fatal gap is the unproved identification
\[
X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U},\qquad \mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M,
\]
and the subsequent use of this identification to construct approximate units in \(X_{M_U}\) from finite Connes-fusion coefficients. This is essential for Lemma 1, the boundary-corner intersection lemma, and therefore for the entire finite-boundary reduction.

The document uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses only the permitted `fullpage` package for layout, and does not use forbidden font-size, spacing, or margin changes. The LaTeX contract is satisfied.

## 2. Literature cross-check

The cited sources are real and relevant.

- DKEP’s paper defines boundary pieces, \(K_X(M)\), \(\widetilde S_X(M)\), and Lemma 8.5 in essentially the form used here. Lemma 8.5 applies to a separable finite von Neumann algebra with a trace-preserving action ergodic on the center; for the trivial action on a factor, the specialization used in `answer.tex` is legitimate. DKEP also state that \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\) is a corner whose support projection commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Proposition 2.10 does identify simultaneous normal biduals as intersections of one-normal biduals, and it gives nonunital inclusions for suitable subalgebras. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.6 supports the revised annihilator argument much better than the previous citation: condition (4) says that functionals vanishing on \(X B(L^2N,L^2M)Y\) vanish on \(K_{X,Y}^{\infty,1}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 give the relevant graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule over \(W\subset U_1\cap U_2\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 and Proposition 6.3 support the final factoriality and nonamenability arguments. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

## 3. Previous concerns addressed

### 3.1 Incorrect centrality of \(p_{\rm nor}\)

This has been fixed. The revised proof no longer calls the normal-support projection central. This is important because DKEP explicitly note that the canonical embedding into the normal bidual is not generally multiplicative precisely because \(p_{\rm nor}\) need not be central. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 3.2 Annihilator equality \(X^\perp=K_X^\perp\)

The revision substantially improves this part by invoking DKEP Theorem 5.6. The argument is now plausible: if \(\phi\in A^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X\), then since \(X\) is hereditary, \(\phi\) vanishes on \(XAX\), so Theorem 5.6 gives \(\phi(T)=0\) for \(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}\), hence for \(T\in K_X\). This missing intermediate phrase, “because \(XAX\subset X\) and is norm dense in \(X\),” should be written explicitly, but the underlying citation is now appropriate.

### 3.3 Finite boundary criterion

The palindromic criterion is now basically sound. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\), the GNS argument for the palindromic product, and the final hypertrace contradiction are credible. This section is not the main obstruction.

---

## 4. Remaining fatal issue: \(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\) is not proved

The proof says:

> Thus DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the coefficient boundary of \(\mathcal H_U\) with the subalgebra boundary \(X_{M_U}\) of Example 3.4.

This is too fast and, as written, unsupported.

DKEP Example 5.11 defines \(X_H\) for an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence \(H\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by coefficient operators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\). It then notes that if \(B\subset M\) and one considers \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence, then the resulting boundary piece is the subalgebra boundary of Example 3.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` uses instead the induced **\(M\)-\(M\)** correspondence
\[
\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M.
\]
The equality between the coefficient boundary of this induced correspondence and the Example 3.4 boundary \(X_{M_U}\) is an additional lemma. It may be true, but it is not stated by DKEP Example 5.11 in the form cited, and the proof in `answer.tex` only checks \(T_{\Omega_U}=e_{M_U}\). That proves one inclusion at best, not equality of hereditary coefficient algebras.

The missing direction is essential: one must show that coefficients of **all bounded vectors** in \(L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) lie in \(X_{M_U}\), or at least have the same normal support as \(X_{M_U}\). Without this, the later claim that \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by positive coefficients of \(\mathcal H_U\) is unproved.

---

## 5. Consequence: approximate-unit construction is not justified

Lemma 1 relies on the assertion:

> The boundary \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive approximate unit whose elements are norm limits of finite sums of coefficients of finite Connes fusions of \(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\).

This follows only if \(X_{M_U}\) is indeed the coefficient boundary of \(\mathcal H_U\), or if a separate support-level replacement is proved. Since the equality \(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\) is not established, this approximate-unit construction remains unsupported.

This is not cosmetic: those approximate units are used to prove
\[
s_W(s_UA_M^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_{U\cap W}A_M^{**}s_{U\cap W},
\]
which is then used to prove the crucial normal-bidual corner inclusion
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
Thus the central boundary-intersection lemma still rests on an unproved essential lemma.

---

## 6. Additional technical gap in Lemma 1

Even granting the coefficient-boundary equality, the proof should explicitly record two further facts:

### 3.2 Annihilator equality \(X^\perp=K_X^\perp\)

The use of DKEP Theorem 5.6 is now much more appropriate. If a \(\sharp_J\)-functional vanishes on \(X\), it vanishes on \(XAX\), and Theorem 5.6 implies vanishing on \(K_X^{\infty,1}\), hence on \(K_X\). This addresses the earlier mis-citation problem.

## 4. Remaining fatal issue: \(\mathcal C_U\) is not proved to be an algebra

In Lemma 2, the author defines \(\mathcal C_U\) as:

> the linear span of coefficients of all finite Connes fusions of copies of \(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\).

Then the proof says:

> The identities \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\) and \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) make \(\mathcal C_U\) a self-adjoint algebra...

This is **not justified as stated**.

If
\[
a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2},\qquad b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}
\]
are coefficients of two finite fusions, then the product is written as
\[
ab
=
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]
But the two vectors here generally lie in **different correspondences**: the left vector lies in a larger fusion, while \(\eta_2\) lies in the original \(U\)-fusion. This is a rectangular coefficient between different fusions, not a coefficient of a single finite Connes fusion in the sense of DKEP Example 5.11.

Thus the displayed identity does not prove that the product remains in the linear span of coefficients of finite fusions as defined. At minimum, the author would need to redefine \(\mathcal C_U\) to include all rectangular coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) where \(\xi,\eta\) lie in possibly different finite \(U\)-labelled fusions, and then prove that this class has the claimed closure and support properties.

This matters because the approximate-unit construction depends on this algebra claim. The proof says that a contractive approximate unit for \(X_{M_U}\) can be chosen as norm limits of elements of \(\mathcal C_U\). That conclusion is not established unless the polynomial functional calculus of finite positive coefficient sums stays in the claimed finite-fusion coefficient class. As written, this step is not rigorous.

This is a central gap: the approximate units \(h_\alpha^U,h_\alpha^W\) are then used to prove
\[
s_W(s_UA_M^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_{U\cap W}A_M^{**}s_{U\cap W},
\]
which is the heart of the boundary-corner intersection lemma.

---

## 5. Related support-bookkeeping issue

The proof also uses the statement:

> If a correspondence is a direct sum of correspondences \(\mathcal K_j\) with \(X_{\mathcal K_j}\subset X\), then all its coefficients lie in \(s_XA^{**}s_X\).

This is plausible, and the author gives a one-sentence explanation. But because CDHJKN’s fusion theorem gives decompositions as direct sums, and the proof relies on support containment for arbitrary bounded vectors in those direct sums, this deserves a fully stated lemma. In particular, one should prove carefully that finite coordinate cutdowns of bounded vectors remain uniformly bounded and that their coefficient operators converge weak-* to the desired coefficient. The current statement is probably correct but too compressed for such a central use.

## 6. Finite-boundary criterion: small missing normality argument

In Lemma 3, the compression argument says that the restriction of the compressed state to \(N\) is normal. This is true, but the proof no longer includes the domination argument from the earlier version. The missing line is:
\[
0\le \omega(q^\perp xq^\perp)\le \omega(x)=\tau(x),\qquad x\in N_+,
\]
because \(q\) commutes with \(N\). Hence the compressed functional is dominated by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\), so it is normal. This is a minor repairable omission, not the main fatal flaw.

## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem interpretation

The answer explicitly adopts the standard interpretation that “irreducible” means not a nontrivial graph join, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected. This is a reasonable interpretation of the problem statement, which is otherwise slightly ambiguous about whether the two subgraphs are required to be nonempty and disjoint.

No major issue here.

---

### 2. Normal support calculus

The formula

\[
(D^{\sharp_J})^*=\mathcal A_N\cap s_DA^{**}s_D
\tag{1}
\]

is plausible for hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebras with faithful multiplier actions of both \(N\) and \(JNJ\), but the answer states it rather generally. DKEP’s support discussion before Remark 2.8 requires normal \(M\)-\(C^*\)-algebra hypotheses and faithful multiplier action; the simultaneous-normal version is then obtained through Proposition 2.10. The answer should explicitly verify these hypotheses for every hereditary algebra to which it applies. This is not just cosmetic, because general DKEP boundary pieces need not have all of \(N\) and \(JNJ\) in their multiplier algebras; DKEP later replaces \(X\) by \(K_X(M)\) precisely to get better multiplier behavior.

The claim \(q_X=q(X)\) for an arbitrary DKEP boundary piece is also overgeneralized. For the specific subalgebra boundary pieces \(X_{M_U}\), this may be repairable, because Example 3.4 gives good multiplier invariance. But as written, the statement is not fully justified for arbitrary boundary pieces.

This issue is serious but probably repairable. It is not the main fatal flaw.

---

### 3. Lemma “induced subalgebra boundary”

The statement \(X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B\) is plausible and probably true, but the proof has clarity and convention problems.

In particular, the sentence

> “for the vector \(\widehat1\) the coefficient \(T_{\widehat1}T_{\widehat1}^{*}\) (for \(H\)) and \(T_{\widehat1}^{*}T_{\widehat1}\) (for \(K\)) is \(1_{L^2B}\)”

is at least ambiguous and appears reversed under the convention later stated in the paper, \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\). With that convention, for \(H={}_NL^2N_B\), \(T_{\widehat1}:L^2B\to L^2N\) is the inclusion, so \(T_{\widehat1}^*T_{\widehat1}=1_{L^2B}\), while \(T_{\widehat1}T_{\widehat1}^*=e_B\). The analogous identity for \(K={}_BL^2N_N\) is reversed. This may only be a notational slip, but in a proof built around coefficient operators, the direction matters.

The minimality argument using DKEP Example 5.11 also needs more detail. Example 5.11 gives minimal boundary pieces for a given correspondence, but the answer only sketches why \(\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_BK\) is mixing relative to \(X_B\times B(L^2N)\). A direct computation from the cyclic vector \(1\otimes_B1\), whose coefficient is \(e_B\), would be cleaner.

This is a gap/ambiguity, but not by itself fatal if corrected.

---

### 4. Lemma “direct sums and finite coefficient algebras” — fatal flaw

This is the main mathematical failure.

The first assertion attempts to prove that if each summand \(\mathcal K_j\) has coefficient boundary supported by \(s_X\), then every coefficient of \(\bigoplus_j\mathcal K_j\) lies in \(s_XA^{**}s_X\). The proof uses finite-coordinate projections \(p_F\) and states:

> “the strong convergence, with uniform bounds, gives  
> \(T_{\xi_F}^*T_{\eta_F}\to T_\xi^*T_\eta\) weak-* in \(A^{**}\).”

This implication is false.

Here \(A=B(L^2N)\) is being treated as a \(C^*\)-algebra and \(A^{**}\) is its universal enveloping von Neumann algebra. Strong-operator convergence in the standard representation does **not** imply weak-* convergence in \(A^{**}\), i.e. convergence against all functionals in \(A^*\). It only gives convergence against normal functionals of the standard representation.

A concrete standard counterexample: in \(B(\ell^2\mathbb N)\), let \(P_n\) be the projection onto \(\overline{\mathrm{span}}\{e_k:k\ge n\}\). Then \(P_n\to0\) strongly, but a singular state obtained as an ultralimit of diagonal vector states satisfies \(\varphi(P_n)=1\) for all \(n\). Thus \(P_n\) does not converge to \(0\) in the \(\sigma(B(\ell^2),B(\ell^2)^*)\) topology, hence not weak-* in \(B(\ell^2)^{**}\).

This invalidates the proof that infinite direct-sum coefficients are supported by \(s_X\). Since CDHJKN fusion decompositions may have infinite multiplicity, this direct-sum support step is essential. The answer does not provide an alternative argument using DKEP’s \(\sharp_J\)-normal topology, Magajna strong-module closure, or a valid annihilator argument.

Therefore Lemma `\ref{lem:dsupport}` is not proved.

The second part of the lemma, constructing approximate units from finite-fusion coefficients, is more plausible, but it depends on the preceding coefficient-boundary identification and does not fix the failed support argument.

---

### 5. Boundary-corner intersection lemma

The boundary-corner intersection

\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}
\tag{5}
\]

is the heart of the proof. Its proof depends directly on the flawed direct-sum support lemma.

The use of CDHJKN fusion rules is conceptually correct: fusing \(H_W\)-blocks with \(H_U\)-blocks should produce summands indexed by subsets of \(W\cap U\). CDHJKN’s results support this kind of statement. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) But after obtaining a direct sum of correspondences, the answer again needs a valid argument that coefficients of arbitrary bounded vectors in the direct sum are supported by the appropriate boundary support. That is precisely the part left unproved because of the invalid weak-* convergence step above.

Consequently, the proof of (5) is incomplete. Since the palindromic compact-corner inclusion depends on (5), the later finite-boundary criterion cannot be applied.

This is a fatal gap.

---

### 6. Finite boundary criterion

The finite boundary criterion is largely plausible. The compression argument by \(q_i^\perp\) is reasonable because DKEP states that the support projection of \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\) commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The GNS argument for the palindromic positive contraction is also plausible.

However, one step should be cited or expanded:

> “DKEP's canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map  
> \(\Theta:B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) …”

DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 indeed discusses natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps into \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\), but the answer should explicitly connect that construction to the compact case \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) and verify that \(\omega\circ\Theta\) is a Connes hypertrace. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This is a missing detail, but likely repairable.

The finite criterion is not the main obstruction.

---

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

This proposition is plausible but terse.

The commutator formulas for the first-letter projections \(p,q\) are asserted by “reduced-word inspection.” This is acceptable as a sketch, but for a standalone proof the author should verify the cases where right multiplication by a centered letter cancels the final letter of a reduced word and may shorten the word to the \(B\)-summand. The projection inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]

are also plausible, and the \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) is standard. But again, the proof is compressed.

I would not reject solely on this section, but it would need expansion in a rigorous final proof.

---

### 8. Graph-product application

The graph-product splitting

\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]

is standard. The combinatorial selection of \(w_1,w_2\) from non-neighbors of \(v\) is correct; I also brute-force checked the relevant finite-graph combinatorial implications for small vertex numbers. The final use of CDHJKN’s factoriality and amenability criteria is also essentially correct, provided one notes explicitly that in the tracial setting a trace-zero unitary lies in the centralizer and implies \(\dim M_v\ge2\). CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 and Proposition 6.3 support these uses. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But this final step relies on the unproved boundary-corner intersection lemma, so the proof as a whole does not go through.

---

## Final assessment

The document is well organized and many components are plausible, but the proof is not complete. The fatal issue is the unsupported passage from strong convergence of finite-coordinate cutdowns to weak-* convergence in \(A^{**}\), which invalidates the direct-sum support lemma and therefore the boundary-corner intersection lemma. Since that intersection lemma is indispensable for producing the compact-corner palindromic product, the final conclusion is not rigorously established.

### Remaining / new fatal issue

The central new problem is that the proof now works “inside \(\mathcal A_M\)” while using concrete multiplication formulas from \(B(L^2M)\). This is not justified.

DKEP Remark 2.8 states that although the canonical embedding
\[
A\to (A^\sharp)^*
\]
is a complete order isomorphism onto its range, it is **not a \(*\)-homomorphism in general**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) Thus, for concrete operators \(a,b\in B(L^2M)\), the product of their images in \(\mathcal A_M\) need not equal the image of the concrete product \(ab\).

## Detailed audit by section

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

The adopted interpretation of irreducibility as “not a nontrivial join,” equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected, is reasonable and explicitly recorded. No issue here.

---

### 2. Normal supports and graph-product boundary corners

The attempt to identify the weak-* closure of \(X\) in \(\mathcal A_N\) with the DKEP support corner is plausible for the relevant \(X_{M_U}\), using DKEP Theorem 5.6 and the equality of annihilators. But the section immediately introduces the fatal “rectangular fact”:

\[
R^*R\in r\mathcal A_Nr,\quad S^*S\in s\mathcal A_Ns
\Longrightarrow R^*S\in r\mathcal A_Ns.
\tag{2}
\]

This statement is true **inside a von Neumann algebra** if \(R,S\) are elements of that von Neumann algebra and \(R^*R,S^*S\) are computed using that von Neumann algebra’s multiplication. But in the proof, \(R,S\) are concrete operators in \(B(L^2N)\), and \(R^*R\), \(R^*S\) are concrete products. Their images in \(\mathcal A_N\) need not satisfy the same multiplicative identities.

So the sentence

> “Indeed \(R(1-r)=0\) and \(S(1-s)=0\).”

is unjustified. From the fact that the **image of the concrete operator** \(R^*R\) lies in \(r\mathcal A_Nr\), one cannot conclude that the image of \(R\) satisfies \(R(1-r)=0\) in \(\mathcal A_N\).

This is a fatal support-calculus error.

---

### 3. Lemma `\ref{lem:coeff}`: induced subalgebra boundary

This is substantially improved. The coefficient directions are now consistent with DKEP’s convention \(T_\xi\in B(L^2M,L^2N)\) for an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence. The argument \(X_{\mathcal H_B}\subset X_B\) via fusion and minimality, and \(X_B\subset X_{\mathcal H_B}\) via \(\xi\otimes\widehat1\), is plausible.

This part no longer appears to contain a fatal issue, though it remains somewhat terse.

---

### 4. Lemma `\ref{lem:dsupport}`: direct sums and finite coefficient algebras

The replacement of the previous invalid strong-to-universal-bidual argument by a DKEP mixing-closure argument is a real improvement. DKEP’s proof of Theorem 5.10 does use exactly the kind of projection-cutdown closure argument the draft invokes. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the lemma still culminates in:

> “every coefficient of \(\mathcal K\) belongs, after the canonical embedding \(B(L^2N)\subset\mathcal A_N\), to \(q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X\).”

This support conclusion is acceptable only as a statement about the **image of the concrete coefficient operator**. It does not justify later multiplying such images as if the concrete coefficient-product identities remained valid in \(\mathcal A_N\).

Thus, the direct-sum repair itself may be salvageable, but it does not support the later boundary-intersection proof as written.

---

### 5. Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}`: boundary-corner intersection

This is the central failure.

The proof wants to show

\[
h_\alpha^W h_\beta^U\in q_I\mathcal A_M q_U.
\tag{4}
\]

It reduces by norm closure to concrete coefficients
\[
a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2}\in\mathcal C_W,\qquad
b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}\in\mathcal C_U
\]
and then uses the concrete identity
\[
ab
=
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\tag{5}
\]

This identity is valid in the concrete algebra \(B(L^2M)\), by DKEP’s Connes-fusion formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But the proof needs an identity for the product of the **images of \(a\) and \(b\) in \(\mathcal A_M\)**. Since the canonical map into \(\mathcal A_M\) is not multiplicative in general, the image of \(ab\) need not equal the product of the images of \(a\) and \(b\). DKEP explicitly warns about this nonmultiplicativity. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Therefore:

- The reduction from \(h_\alpha^W h_\beta^U\) to concrete coefficient products is invalid.
- The use of the rectangular support fact (2) is invalid.
- The inclusion \(q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}\) is not proved.
- The palindromic compact-corner inclusion is not proved.

This is fatal because the final theorem depends on this lemma.

---

### 6. Finite-boundary criterion

This section is mostly plausible. The compression argument by \(q_i^\perp\) is sound in spirit because DKEP states that the relevant support projection commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The GNS argument for the palindromic positive contraction is also reasonable.

The notation
\[
\Theta(T)=q_0Tq_0
\]
must be interpreted as DKEP’s u.c.p. compression, not as a multiplicative embedding of \(B(L^2N)\) into \(\mathcal A_N\). With that interpretation, this section is likely repairable. It is not the main obstruction.

---

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The argument remains terse but plausible. The reduced-word commutator formulas and projection inequalities should be expanded in a complete proof, but I did not find a definite mathematical contradiction here.

This section is not the fatal problem.

---

### 8. Application to graph products

The graph-combinatorial part is correct. I also brute-force checked the finite-graph implications for small vertex numbers: connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with \(|V|\ge3\) rules out universal vertices and isolated complement edges, and gives the required Bass–Serre unitaries.

The use of CDHJKN for factoriality and nonamenability is appropriate: Main Theorem 0.5 gives the factor criterion, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability criterion. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

However, this final application depends on Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}`, which is not proved.

---

## Summary of mathematical status

The revision fixes the previous direct-sum topology problem in a plausible way, but the proof still fails at the most important point: the boundary-corner intersection argument uses concrete coefficient multiplication inside a normal bidual where the canonical embedding is not multiplicative.

To make this proof rigorous, the author would need to return to a support calculation in an ordinary enveloping von Neumann algebra \(A^{**}\), or develop a genuine rectangular normal-bidual calculus showing that the relevant products in \(\mathcal A_M\) are controlled by DKEP’s \(K_{X,Y}\)-corners. The current text does neither.

Accordingly, the proof is not complete.

## Review of previous concerns

### 1. Nonmultiplicativity of the normal bidual — addressed

The previous draft incorrectly multiplied concrete coefficient operators after embedding them into \(\mathcal A_M=(B(L^2M)^{\sharp_J})^*\). The revised proof explicitly avoids this. In the new boundary-support intersection lemma, all coefficient products \(hkh\) are computed concretely in \(B(L^2M)\), then limits are taken in the ordinary bidual \(A_M^{**}\), and only afterward is the result compressed by the DKEP projection \(p_M\).

This resolves the earlier fatal objection.

### 2. Strong-operator convergence versus weak-* convergence in \(A^{**}\) — addressed

The old proof used strong convergence of finite-coordinate cutdowns to infer weak-* convergence in the universal bidual, which was invalid. The revised direct-sum lemma instead uses the DKEP mixing-closure argument from the proof of Theorem 5.10. This is the right mechanism and avoids the earlier topology error.

### 3. Induced subalgebra boundary conventions — addressed

The revised proof of \(X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B\) fixes the earlier directional confusion for the coefficient maps \(T_{\widehat 1}\). It now correctly identifies \(T_{\widehat1}:L^2N\to L^2B\) for \({}_NL^2N_B\) and the inclusion \(T_{\widehat1}:L^2B\to L^2N\) for \({}_BL^2N_N\). This section is now acceptable.

### 4. Hypertrace step in the finite-boundary criterion — addressed

The finite-boundary criterion now treats \(\Theta(T)=q_0Tq_0\) as DKEP’s u.c.p. compression into the normal compact corner, not as a multiplicative embedding. The construction of the resulting Connes hypertrace is sufficiently clear.
