## Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### Problem interpretation

The interpretation of irreducibility as nontrivial join-irreducibility, equivalently connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\), is explicitly stated. This resolves the ambiguity in the original problem statement.

### Normal supports and coefficient boundary pieces

The normal-support paragraph is now correctly framed around the ordinary bidual \(A^{**}\) and the support projection \(p_N\). The proof should be read for the boundary pieces actually used later, namely \(X_{M_U}\), \(X_B\), and \(\mathbb K(L^2M)\), for which the necessary multiplier hypotheses hold; DKEP Example 3.4 explicitly gives \(M\) and \(JMJ\) in the multiplier algebra for subalgebra boundary pieces. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### Lemma `\ref{lem:coeff}`

The proof of \(X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B\) is now coherent. The fusion argument gives \(X_{\mathcal H_B}\subset X_B\), while the vectors \(\xi\otimes\widehat1\) show that every generator of \(X_B\) appears as a coefficient for \(\mathcal H_B\). The self-conjugacy map in the tracial case is standard.

### Lemma `\ref{lem:dsupport}`

The direct-sum support argument now uses DKEP’s strong-module closure machinery rather than invalid universal-bidual weak-* convergence. The conclusion that coefficients of the direct sum lie in \(K_X(N)\) follows from mixing relative to \(X\times B(L^2N)\), Theorem 5.10, and Proposition 5.9.

The construction of \(\mathcal C_U\) and the approximate units is also valid: coefficient products are computed concretely using \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), and functional calculus with functions vanishing at zero gives contractive approximate units inside the required norm closure.

### Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}`

This is the critical repair, and it now works.

For positive finite-fusion coefficients \(h=T_\xi^*T_\xi\) and \(k=T_\eta^*T_\eta\), the identity

\[
hkh=T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}^*
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}
\]

is used only in the concrete algebra \(B(L^2M)\). CDHJKN’s fusion rule then decomposes the correspondence containing \(\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta\) over subsets of \(U\cap W\). The direct-sum lemma places this coefficient in \(K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Passing to approximate-unit limits in \(A^{**}\), then using annihilators in \(A^{\sharp_J}\), proves

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]

The induction producing the palindromic support inclusion is also correct.

### Finite boundary criterion

The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) is valid because the DKEP support projections commute with both \(N\) and \(JNJ\). The GNS argument with the positive palindromic product is correct. The construction of the hypertrace from \(\omega(q_0)=1\) is now adequately justified.

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The reduced-word arguments are concise but mathematically standard. The projections \(p,q,e_B\) lie in \(S_{X_B}(N)\), the inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]

follow from the AFP reduced-word decomposition, and the \(B\)-Haar unitary \(h=w_1a\) gives pairwise orthogonal translates of \(e_B\). This proves relative proper proximality via DKEP Theorem 6.2.

### Application to graph products

The graph-product AFP splitting is standard. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from the complement graph is correct in both cases \(|C(v)|\ge2\) and \(|C(v)|=1\). The intersection of all links is empty because no vertex lies in its own link. The boundary-intersection lemma then places the palindromic product in the compact corner.

The final use of CDHJKN to obtain factoriality and nonamenability is valid: connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices rules out universal vertices and isolated exceptional complement edges, and Proposition 6.3 rules out amenability under the same condition.

## Remaining serious issues

### 1. The normal-bidual support paragraph is still not fully rigorous

The proof’s support paragraph remains an essential bottleneck. It states that for a boundary piece \(X\),
\[
p_N\bar q_X=\bar q_Xp_N,\qquad
(X^{\sharp_J})^*=q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X,\quad q_X=p_N\bar q_X,
\]
where earlier \(q_X\) was defined as the support of \((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\), not of \((X^{\sharp_J})^*\). This creates a notational and logical conflation: one must distinguish the support of \(X\) from the support of \(K_X(N)\), then prove they agree.

DKEP provides the ingredients: \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) is the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of both \(X\) and \(K_X\), and Theorem 5.6 gives annihilator characterizations involving \(XB(L^2N)X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` does not carefully prove the equality of the normal-bidual supports. It asserts it in the same paragraph where it is needed. This is not merely expository: Lemma 3 later uses approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) to converge to the support projection \(q_{X_{M_U}}\) that was defined via \(K_{X_{M_U}}\).

A rigorous proof should introduce two projections, say \(q(X)\) and \(q(K_X)\), prove \(q(X)=q(K_X)\) via Theorem 5.6, and only then use approximate units of \(X\) to approximate the DKEP support projection.

### 2. Lemma `direct sums and finite fusions` remains underproved

The direct-sum part now cites the closure argument from DKEP Theorem 5.10. DKEP Theorem 5.10 does contain exactly the kind of closure argument involving bounded vectors, projection cutdowns, and strong-module closedness. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) However, the answer still does not spell out enough details to make the lemma standalone.

Specific missing points:

- The proof does not explicitly specify the right boundary piece being used, presumably \(Y=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), when applying DKEP Theorem 5.10.
- The proof treats finite-coordinate truncations as a sequence \(\xi_n\), but the direct sum is indexed by an arbitrary set \(J\), so one should use a net over finite subsets unless countability is justified.
- It says finite-coordinate truncations of a bounded vector are bounded and converge to the vector. This is true, but the proof should explicitly check both left- and right-boundedness and the uniform operator-norm bounds.
- The conversion from “all bounded vectors are \(X\)-mixing” to \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\in K_X(N)\) requires the exact orientation of DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9. Proposition 5.9 distinguishes \(K_{X,Y}\), \(K_X\), and \(K_X^{\infty,1}\), so this should not be left implicit. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This lemma is essential for the support-intersection argument, so these are not harmless omissions.

### 3. The boundary-support intersection lemma is still the central unresolved gap

The revised Lemma `intersection of boundary supports` is much better than the previous version, but it is still not a complete proof.

The coefficient identity
\[
hkh
=
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}^{*}
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}
\]
is correct. The use of CDHJKN fusion rules is also plausible: their Proposition 5.5 gives fusion decompositions indexed by subsets of intersections. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) However, the answer applies this to arbitrary finite fusions of \(\mathcal H_W\), \(\overline{\mathcal H_W}\), \(\mathcal H_U\), and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\) in a single sentence. A real proof must induct over the number of fusion factors and track how each label changes under repeated applications of CDHJKN Proposition 5.5.

More importantly, the passage
\[
\phi(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)=0
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
q_Wq_Uq_W\in q_I\mathcal A_Mq_I
\]
still depends on the unresolved normal-support identification discussed above. The proof says that approximate units converge to ordinary open supports in \(A_M^{**}\), then compresses by \(p_M\), and then invokes an annihilator description. This is exactly the delicate nonmultiplicative normal-bidual point that needed a fully explicit proof. DKEP does provide support-corner machinery, but the answer does not prove the required compatibility of ordinary supports, \(p_M\), \(X\), and \(K_X(M)\) in sufficient detail. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Thus Lemma `intersection` remains insufficiently justified. Since this lemma is the bridge from relative proper proximality of the link-boundary pieces to ordinary proper proximality, this is a fatal gap.

### 4. The finite-boundary criterion is improved but still slightly compressed

The replacement of \(q_0Tq_0\) by DKEP’s canonical u.c.p. map is an improvement. DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 indeed constructs natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps from \(\mathbb B(L^2M)\) into the normal compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the proof should still explicitly verify:

- that \(\Theta_0(x)=xq_0\) for \(x\in N\), or at least that \(\omega(\Theta_0(x))=\tau(x)\);
- that \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\subset \widetilde S(N)\), so \(\omega\) is actually defined on the range of \(\Theta_0\);
- that \(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is \(N\)-central using the \(N\)-bimodularity of \(\Theta_0\) and centrality of \(\omega\).

These are probably repairable, but the argument is still not fully written.

### 5. The Bass–Serre proposition is plausible but not fully proved

The revised Bass–Serre argument gives more detail, but it still relies on “reduced-word inspection” for several essential facts:

- \([p,JxJ]\) and \([q,JxJ]\) are finite sums of \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-translates of \(e_B\);
- these commutators satisfy the hypotheses of DKEP Lemma 6.1 for an ultraweakly dense generating set;
- \(q w_1^*w_2 q=0\);
- \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar and the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal.

All of these are believable, but they are not fully demonstrated. DKEP Theorem 6.2 characterizes proper proximality through the nonexistence of central states on \(S_X(M)\), so this proposition is a key input, not a side calculation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) A complete proof should include the AFP normal-form decomposition and check these operator equalities on each reduced-word summand.

### 6. Final graph-product application is mostly correct, conditional on earlier lemmas

The combinatorial construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from complement connectedness is correct. The AFP decomposition
\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]
is standard, though it should be cited or proved. The final factoriality and nonamenability argument is supported by CDHJKN Theorem E / Theorem 2.4 and Proposition 6.3. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

But this final section depends critically on Lemma `intersection` and Lemma `finite`, so the proof is not complete unless those are fully rigorous.

The revision makes meaningful progress and resolves some presentation and citation issues. Nevertheless, the proof still has essential unresolved technical gaps, especially in the normal-bidual support calculus and the boundary-support intersection lemma. These are not optional details; they are the core mechanism by which the proof passes from relative proper proximality to ordinary proper proximality.

Therefore the revised `answer.tex` is **not answer-ready**.

## Section-by-section audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The proof explicitly records the adopted interpretation of irreducibility as “not a non-trivial join,” equivalently connected complement graph. This is the standard graph-product interpretation, and it matches the use of CDHJKN’s join-irreducible results later in the proof. The problem statement ambiguity is therefore handled.

### Normal support bookkeeping

The new Lemma `normal support bookkeeping` addresses the previous main objection. It now separates the ordinary open support of \(X\) from that of \(K_X(N)\), proves that their compressed normal supports agree, and formulates the annihilator characterization explicitly.

This is supported by DKEP’s normal-bidual construction: DKEP describe \((A^\sharp)^*\) as the normal-support corner \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and for a subalgebra \(B\subset A\) whose multiplier action is faithful they identify \((B^\sharp)^*\) with the corresponding compressed support corner. The simultaneous \(N,JNJ\)-normal version is exactly the role of Proposition 2.10. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The equality of the supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is now justified via DKEP Theorem 5.6 and Proposition 3.6: DKEP identify \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) as the relevant \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure and characterize it by annihilation against \(\sharp_J\)-normal functionals vanishing on \(X B(L^2N) X\); since \(X\) is hereditary, vanishing on \(X\) gives vanishing on that product. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This resolves the previous support-calculus objection.

### Subalgebra coefficients

The proof that the coefficient boundary of
\[
{}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N
\]
is \(X_B\) is now acceptable. It uses DKEP Example 5.11, which defines coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences and identifies the subalgebra boundary in the case of \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The argument that \(Z\subset U\Rightarrow X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\) via \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\) is correct.

### Direct sums and finite fusions

The direct-sum lemma now contains the missing finite-subset net argument, boundedness control for coordinate truncations, and the correct appeal to DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9. DKEP Theorem 5.10 gives the equivalence between mixing of correspondences and relative compactness of all bounded-vector coefficient operators; Proposition 5.9 converts rectangular relative compactness into \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\in K_X(N)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The finite-fusion algebra argument is also now adequate. The coefficient product formula
\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
=
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}
\]
is consistent with DKEP’s Connes-fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The construction of a contractive approximate unit from functions \(g_\varepsilon(s)=s(s+\varepsilon)^{-1}\), and the polynomial approximation argument, are standard and sufficient.

### Intersection of boundary supports

This was the central gap in the prior draft. The revised proof now gives a sufficiently explicit argument.

The proof uses CDHJKN’s identification of \(H_R(V,V)\) with
\[
L^2M\otimes_{M_R}L^2M
\]
and their fusion rule
\[
H_{U_1}(V_1,V_2)\otimes_{M_{V_2}}H_{U_2}(V_2,V_3)
\cong
\bigoplus_{W\subset U_1\cap U_2}H_W(V_1,V_3)^{\oplus k}.
\]
This directly supports the induction that a finite fusion with labels \(A_1,\dots,A_n\) decomposes over subsets of \(\cap_iA_i\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

The passage from coefficient products to support projections is also now justified: positive finite-fusion products land in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), norm limits land in \(K_{X_{M_I}}^{\infty,1}(M)\), and the normal-support lemma then converts annihilation against \(A_M^{\sharp_J}\)-functionals into membership in the normal-bidual corner \(q_I\mathcal A_Mq_I\). This resolves the earlier nonmultiplicativity concern.

The induction for palindromic products is correct.

### Finite-boundary criterion

The finite-boundary criterion is now sound. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) gives a central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\) if \(\omega(q_i^\perp)>0\), and the factor assumption correctly forces its normal restriction to \(N\) to be the trace.

The previous issue with the formal expression \(q_0Tq_0\) has been fixed. DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 explicitly uses natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal compact corner, and the revised proof invokes that map instead of pretending that the concrete inclusion is multiplicative. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The final Connes-hypertrace contradiction is standard and valid.

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The revised proof gives enough AFP normal-form detail. The projections \(p,q\) onto first-letter \(P\)- and \(Q\)-subspaces are shown to lie in \(S_{X_B}(N)\) using commutator computations and DKEP Lemma 6.1. DKEP’s definition of \(S_X(M)\), Lemma 6.1, and Theorem 6.2 justify this route to relative proper proximality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
are correctly justified by reduced-word orthogonality. The \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) is also correct: \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for all \(n\ne0\), so the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and centrality forces their common state value to be zero.

### Graph-product application

The graph-product AFP decomposition over \(M_{\Lk(v)}\) is standard and is used correctly. The construction of the unitaries \(w_1,w_2\) from connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) is correct in both cases \(C(v)\) has at least two vertices and \(C(v)=\{r\}\). The expectation computations follow from graph-product reduced-word calculus.

The final use of CDHJKN is now correctly stated. CDHJKN Theorem E gives the factoriality criterion, and their Theorem 2.4 gives the join-irreducible \(|V|\ge3\) factor case directly. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability criterion, and connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices excludes the exceptional isolated nonedge condition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

Thus \(M\) is a separable non-amenable finite factor, and the finite-boundary criterion applies.

## Final assessment

All previously fatal objections have been addressed:

- normal-support calculus: addressed;
- equality of supports for \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\): addressed;
- direct-sum coefficient control: addressed;
- CDHJKN fusion-label induction: addressed;
- nonmultiplicative normal-bidual compression issue: addressed;
- hypertrace step: addressed;
- Bass–Serre reduced-word details: addressed;
- final factoriality/nonamenability citations: addressed.

I find no remaining open gap or missing assumption affecting the proof.

## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Summary verdict

The revision improves several local arguments, especially the subalgebra-coefficient discussion, the finite-boundary compression argument, and the Bass–Serre paradox. However, the proof is still **not answer-ready**. A central support-intersection step remains invalid as written because it multiplies concrete operators after passing to DKEP’s normal bidual as though the canonical map
\[
A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\to (A^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
were multiplicative. DKEP explicitly warn that this canonical embedding is generally **not** a \(*\)-homomorphism. This directly invalidates the passage from
\[
\phi(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)=0
\]
for concrete products to
\[
\widetilde\phi(q_Wq_Uq_W)=0
\]
inside \(\mathcal A_M\). Since this is the load-bearing proof of the boundary-support intersection lemma, the final proper-proximality argument is incomplete.

No obvious LaTeX-contract violation appears in the displayed source: it uses `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, the permitted `fullpage` package, no forbidden layout/font/spacing commands, and appears standalone. The mathematical issue below is decisive.

## What the revision fixed or improved

### 1. Problem interpretation

The revised draft explicitly records the intended interpretation: irreducible means join-irreducible, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected. This is appropriate for finite simple graphs.

### 2. Literature alignment

The cited DKEP and CDHJKN results are real and relevant. DKEP Lemma 8.5 does characterize relative proper proximality using the normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives the simultaneous-normal-bidual inclusion framework, and DKEP explicitly define \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\) as a corner with support commuting with \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

CDHJKN’s graph-product bimodule and fusion rules are also accurately targeted: their Theorem 5.4 decomposes \(L^2(M)\) over subgraph algebras, and Proposition 5.5 gives the fusion rule over intersections. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Their factor and amenability criteria are also correctly relevant. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

### 3. Subalgebra coefficient lemma

The revised proof of the coefficient boundary of
\[
{}_N L^2N_B\otimes_B {}_B L^2N_N
\]
is substantially better. It now uses a linking-algebra formula
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^*T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
= T_\xi^*\,\theta_{\alpha,\beta}\,T_\eta,
\]
which is the right kind of argument. DKEP Example 5.11 indeed identifies coefficient boundary pieces of correspondences and notes that for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence one obtains the subalgebra boundary of Example 3.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Minor caveat: the proof still silently identifies \({}_B L^2N_N\) with \(\overline{{}_N L^2N_B}\). This is true in the tracial case, but the argument should explicitly say that this identification is being used before writing \(H\otimes_B\overline H\).

### 4. Bass–Serre proposition

The Bass–Serre argument is clearer than before. The reduced-word projections \(p,q\), the commutator formulas with right multiplication, the paradoxical inequalities
\[
2\varphi(q)\le \varphi(p)\le \varphi(q),
\]
and the \(B\)-Haar unitary argument are now mostly explicit. The use of DKEP Lemma 6.1/Theorem 6.2 is directionally correct: DKEP Theorem 6.2 defines relative proper proximality by absence of central states on \(S_X(M)\) with normal restriction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This section still has some compressed reduced-word verification, but it is no longer the main obstruction.

---

## Main remaining fatal issue: nonmultiplicativity in the normal bidual

The central problem is in Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}` and in the support bookkeeping surrounding it.

The revised proof argues as follows:

1. Choose c.a.u.’s \(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and \(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\).
2. Show that the **concrete product**
   \[
   h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha
   \]
   belongs to \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), where \(I=U\cap W\).
3. Hence every \(\phi\in A_M^{\sharp_J}\) vanishing on \(X_{M_I}\) satisfies
   \[
   \widetilde\phi(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)=0.
   \]
4. Since \(h_\alpha\to q_W\) and \(k_\beta\to q_U\) strongly* in \(\mathcal A_M\), conclude
   \[
   \widetilde\phi(q_Wq_Uq_W)=0.
   \]

The invalid step is the transition from concrete products to products inside \(\mathcal A_M\). In DKEP’s normal-bidual construction, the canonical map
\[
A_M=\mathbb B(L^2M)\longrightarrow \mathcal A_M=(A_M^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
is generally **not multiplicative**. DKEP say this explicitly: the canonical embedding is a complete order isomorphism onto its range, but “is not a \(*\)-homomorphism in general” because the normal support projection need not be central. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Thus the following two elements are not known to agree:
\[
\iota(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)
\quad\text{and}\quad
\iota(h_\alpha)\,\iota(k_\beta)\,\iota(h_\alpha)
\]
inside \(\mathcal A_M\). The coefficient/fusion argument proves vanishing for the first expression. Strong* convergence of \(h_\alpha,k_\beta\) in \(\mathcal A_M\) gives convergence of the second expression to \(q_Wq_Uq_W\). Without multiplicativity, the proof does not connect these two nets.

This is not a cosmetic gap. It is exactly the mechanism needed to prove
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Therefore Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}` is not proved.

A valid proof would need to work in the ordinary enveloping algebra \(A_M^{**}\), where concrete multiplication is multiplicative, prove an ordinary-support inclusion there, and then carefully compress by the simultaneous normal support projection; or else prove directly that the nonmultiplicative defect is annihilated by all relevant \(\sharp_J\)-normal functionals. The current `answer.tex` does neither.

---

## Normal-support bookkeeping remains overstated

The revised Lemma `\ref{lem:support}` is better than before, but still too sweeping.

It says: for a hereditary \(Y\subset A_N\),
\[
(Y^{\sharp_J})^*=s(Y)\mathcal A_Ns(Y),
\]
and “the canonical image of \(Y\)” is weak* dense in the corner, with c.a.u.’s converging strongly* to \(s(Y)\).

Some version of this is plausible, but the proof given treats the universal simultaneous normal representation as if it were a genuine \(*\)-representation of \(A_N\). DKEP’s actual construction is subtler: for an \(M\)-\(C^*\)-algebra \(A\), they describe \((A^\sharp)^*\) as a cut-down \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and again the canonical embedding of \(A\) need not be multiplicative. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The equality of annihilators of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is more plausibly justified now using DKEP Theorem 5.6 and Proposition 3.6. DKEP Theorem 5.6 indeed gives an annihilator characterization involving functionals vanishing on \(XB(L^2N,L^2M)Y\), and Proposition 3.6 identifies \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) as the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of both \(X\) and \(K_X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But the support lemma still does not adequately handle the nonmultiplicative normal-bidual embedding.

---

## Direct-sum/fusion lemma: mostly improved, but still compressed

The direct-sum lemma is more defensible than before. DKEP Theorem 5.10 does give equivalences between correspondence mixing and membership of all bounded-vector operators in relative compact classes. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The revised proof also correctly points to DKEP Proposition 5.9 for converting relative compactness of \(T_\xi\) into compactness of \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the proof still compresses several nontrivial facts:

- finite direct sums require control of off-diagonal coefficients \(T_{\xi_i}^*T_{\xi_j}\);
- infinite direct sums require a precise use of the closure step in DKEP Theorem 5.10;
- the phrase “finite direct sums have the same property” hides the hereditary-corner argument.

These are probably fixable, but the current version is still not fully self-contained at the level expected of a “strict proof.”

---

## Finite-boundary criterion

The revised finite-boundary lemma is mostly correct **assuming** the palindromic compact-corner hypothesis (4.1). The compression
\[
q^\perp \widetilde S_X(N)q^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
is now explicitly checked. The use of the GNS extension of the state is also now clearer.

The hypertrace step is plausible: DKEP Lemma 8.5 indeed uses natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But since the proof of (4.1) in the graph-product case depends on the invalid support-intersection lemma, this criterion cannot be applied as written.

---

## Graph-product application

The final application is broadly correct if all previous lemmas were available:

- The link AFP decomposition is standard, though a citation should be added.
- The choices of \(w_1,w_2\) from complement-connectedness are combinatorially sound.
- The intersection of all links is indeed empty.
- CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives factoriality/fullness for join-irreducible graphs with at least three vertices and state-zero unitaries. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability obstruction used in the final paragraph. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But the final deduction depends essentially on Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}`, which is not proved.

---

## Bottom line

The revision addresses several previous objections, but the proof still has a fatal gap: it uses multiplication in DKEP’s normal bidual as if the canonical embedding of \(\mathbb B(L^2M)\) were multiplicative. DKEP explicitly state that this is generally false. Consequently, the key inequality
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
is not established, and the proof of proper proximality is incomplete.

## Referee report on the revised `answer.tex`

### Overall verdict

The revision addresses the previous fatal objection. In particular, the boundary-support intersection is now carried out in the ordinary enveloping bidual \(A^{**}\), where multiplication is legitimate, and only then compressed by DKEP’s normal support projection. This avoids the earlier invalid use of the nonmultiplicative canonical map \(A\to(A^{\sharp_J})^*\); DKEP explicitly warn that this canonical embedding is not generally a \(*\)-homomorphism. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

I find the revised proof mathematically acceptable as a complete solution, modulo minor expository improvements noted below that do not affect the validity of the main theorem.

### LaTeX contract

The displayed source uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, only the permitted `fullpage` layout package, and no forbidden line-spacing, font-size, or margin commands. I see no LaTeX-contract violation in the source as provided; the author’s stated 6-page successful compile is within the 12-page limit.

### Literature validation

The main DKEP framework is cited appropriately. DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives the relevant simultaneous normal-bidual inclusion/corner framework; the preceding one-normal discussion identifies \((A^\sharp)^*\) with a cutdown \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and for subalgebras gives the corresponding support-cutdown picture. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Corollary 3.2 support the use of \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) as the strong \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-bimodule closure associated with \(X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.10, Proposition 5.9, and Example 5.11 support the correspondence/coefficient arguments. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The CDHJKN input is also correctly used: Theorem 2.4 gives diffuse full factor results for join-irreducible graph products with state-zero unitaries, and Proposition 5.5 gives the fusion rule over intersections of vertex subsets. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

### Previous concerns and current status

#### 1. Nonmultiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding — resolved

This was the previous fatal flaw. The revised Lemma `intersection of boundary supports` now works in \(A^{**}\). It uses ordinary supports \(r_U\), proves
\[
r_Wr_Ur_W\in r_{K_I}A^{**}r_{K_I},
\]
then compresses by the simultaneous normal support \(p\):
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p\le pr_{K_I}p=q_I.
\]
This is the correct repair. It no longer identifies \(\iota(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)\) with \(\iota(h_\alpha)\iota(k_\beta)\iota(h_\alpha)\) inside \(\mathcal A_M\).

#### 2. Normal-support bookkeeping — adequately repaired

The support lemma now distinguishes ordinary supports in \(A^{**}\) from compressed supports in \(\mathcal A_N=pA^{**}p\). The equality \(s(X)=s(K_X(N))\) is justified through the DKEP strong-closure/annihilator machinery. This was one of the earlier weak points; the present proof is acceptable.

#### 3. Subalgebra coefficient lemma — repaired

The revised proof now gives the correct linking-algebra identity
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
= T_\xi^{*}\theta_{\alpha,\beta}T_\eta,
\]
and explicitly uses the tracial identification
\[
{}_BL^2N_N\cong \overline{{}_NL^2N_B}.
\]
This resolves the earlier concern about passing from the one-sided \(N\)-\(B\) correspondence to the fused \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence.

#### 4. Direct sums and finite fusions — acceptable

The direct-sum argument is now tied to DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9. The proof is terse, but the logic is sound for the subalgebra boundary pieces used later.

Minor note: Lemma `direct sums and finite fusions` is stated for an arbitrary boundary piece \(X\), but the proof invokes DKEP Theorem 5.10, whose stated hypotheses include \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\). In the actual application \(X=X_{M_I}\), this hypothesis is satisfied by DKEP Example 3.4, so this does not affect the final theorem. For maximal precision, the lemma statement should add this hypothesis.

#### 5. Boundary-support intersection — now valid

The revised proof of
\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
is now the strongest part of the argument. The ordinary-bidual support computation, combined with CDHJKN’s fusion rule, gives the desired inclusion without relying on nonnormal lattice meets or on multiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding.

#### 6. Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary criterion — acceptable

The compression
\[
q^\perp\widetilde S_X(N)q^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
is now explicitly checked. The GNS argument using the palindromic product is sound. The final hypertrace step is supported by DKEP Lemma 8.5, which supplies the relevant normal-bidual u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) to the compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### 7. Bass–Serre paradox — acceptable

The AFP normal-form argument is still compressed, but the essential reduced-word checks are present. The commutator verification for \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\) is consistent with DKEP Lemma 6.1, which says that it suffices to check the commutator condition on an ultraweakly dense generating \(*\)-subalgebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The paradoxical inequalities and the \(B\)-Haar unitary argument are correct.

#### 8. Graph-product application — acceptable

The graph-combinatorial choice of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct. The link AFP decomposition is standard. The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives a diffuse full factor, hence a nonamenable finite factor, so the finite-boundary lemma applies.

### Minor recommendations

These are not validity-blocking, but would improve the presentation:

1. Add \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\) to Lemma `direct sums and finite fusions`, or say explicitly that only subalgebra boundaries are used.
2. Add a citation for the standard graph-product AFP decomposition
   \[
   M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\Lk(v)}}(M_{\Lk(v)}\bar\otimes M_v).
   \]
3. In the final paragraph, one could explicitly cite the standard fact that a diffuse amenable finite factor is not full, so a diffuse full factor is nonamenable.

These are editorial/clarificatory; they do not leave an essential mathematical gap.

## Section-by-section audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The draft explicitly adopts the standard interpretation of irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This addresses the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. Support bookkeeping

This section is improved relative to the previous version. The one-normal support formula is now closer to DKEP Section 2.3, and the simultaneous-normal version is at least plausibly obtained through Proposition 2.10.

However, the proof still compresses a nontrivial construction into a citation. In particular, the statement

\[
(Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y)
\]

requires checking that the ordinary open support \(r_Y\) in \(A^{**}\) is the same support appearing under the simultaneous normal representation, and that the two normal structures interact exactly as claimed. DKEP gives the one-normal formula and then states a simultaneous inclusion result, but the equality in this exact corner form is not written out in the draft. This may be repairable, but in a self-contained proof it remains an essential lemma rather than a transparent citation.

The last assertion, \(q_X=pr_X\) for a boundary piece \(X\), is also too brief. The proof says DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Proposition 2.4 imply that functionals vanishing on \(X\) are precisely those vanishing on \(K_X(N)\). This is plausible, but the draft does not verify all hypotheses needed to conclude that \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) is the **smallest** simultaneous strong bimodule generated by \(X\), nor does it spell out why this gives equality of the normal support projections of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\). Since the later intersection lemma depends directly on \(q_U=pr_U\), this is still an essential point.

### 3. Subalgebra coefficient boundary

The revision fixes one previous problem: it no longer relies on Hilbert-space rank-one operators generating all of \(B(L^2B)\). It correctly notes that DKEP Example 3.4 allows arbitrary \(S\in B(L^2B)\).

But the proof still does not establish the full lemma as stated. It verifies the formula

\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
=
T_\xi^*(T_{\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\bar\beta})T_\eta
\]

for bounded elementary vectors and concludes that “all coefficients” of \(\mathcal H_B\) lie in \(X_B\). This is a gap. The coefficient boundary of a correspondence is generated by coefficients of **all bounded vectors**, not only elementary bounded tensors. To pass from elementary tensors to arbitrary bounded vectors, one needs a density/closure argument with uniform boundedness of the associated \(T_\xi\)-operators, or an application of DKEP Theorem 5.10. The draft does not provide this.

This matters because the later proof uses this lemma to identify \(X_{M_U}\) with the coefficient boundary of \(L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), and then constructs approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) from finite-fusion coefficients. Without the full coefficient-boundary identification, the main support-intersection argument is not established.

### 4. Direct sums and finite fusions

The revision adds the missing multiplier hypothesis \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\). That addresses one prior objection.

However, the proof remains too compressed at a crucial point. It says that for a direct sum, finite-support bounded vectors are cyclic dense and then “applying Theorem 5.10 once more” gives the conclusion for all bounded vectors. DKEP Theorem 5.10 requires a cyclic set of bounded vectors satisfying a specific \(T_\xi\in YB(L^2M,L^2N)X\) condition, not merely that the summands are individually relatively compact. The draft does not verify the required cyclic set for the direct sum, nor the closure/uniform boundedness details needed to pass from finite-support vectors to arbitrary bounded vectors.

The finite-fusion coefficient algebra \(\mathcal C_U\) is also still asserted rather than proved. The statement that finite products of coefficients are finite sums of coefficients after inserting conjugate factors is standard, but in this context it is an essential lemma because it feeds directly into \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\). The draft should at least give a precise formula with conventions and explain how finite sums/direct sums are handled.

### 5. Boundary-support intersection lemma

This is the central technical lemma, and it still depends on unresolved inputs.

The CDHJKN fusion rule itself is valid and appropriately cited. The rule decomposes fusions over \(M_{V_2}\) as sums indexed by subsets of the intersection of the relevant labels. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The problem is the operator-algebraic passage from coefficient products to support inequalities. The line

\[
hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)
\]

rests on the direct-sum/finite-fusion lemma and on the coefficient-boundary identification above. Since those are not rigorously proved, this step is not secure.

The limiting argument in the ordinary bidual is clearer than before, and the use of strong-* convergence of approximate units is a reasonable route. But it still assumes the unproved support formula \(q_U=pr_U\) for the original boundary \(X_{M_U}\), and it assumes the existence of approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) lying in the specified norm-closed coefficient cones. Both assumptions trace back to earlier underproved lemmas.

Thus the claimed inequality

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]

is still not rigorously established.

### 6. Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This lemma is mostly plausible. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) is well motivated, and DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the normal-bidual central-state criterion. The use of the compact-corner u.c.p. map \(\Theta_0\) is also consistent with DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The main caveat is that this lemma depends on the preceding palindromic product being genuinely in \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\), which in this proof is supplied only by the still-unproved intersection lemma.

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

This section remains plausible but not fully rigorous.

The AFP reduced-word commutator formula

\[
[p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B)
\]

is asserted after a brief explanation. A strict proof should check it on the vacuum, one-letter words, and longer reduced words. Similarly, the orthogonality claim

\[
q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0
\]

from \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\) needs a complete reduced-word verification.

These are likely repairable gaps, not obviously false statements. But as written, the proof invokes several reduced-word facts without enough detail for a standalone rigorous solution.

### 8. Graph-product application

This part is much improved. The graph-product AFP decomposition is now cited, the graph combinatorics are correct, and the appeal to CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is appropriate. I also ran a small brute-force check of the graph combinatorics for graphs up to seven vertices; no counterexample was found to the local claims used in choosing \(w_1,w_2\).

The final nonamenability argument is acceptable: CDHJKN gives a diffuse full factor under the stated hypotheses, and a separable diffuse amenable finite factor would be the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor, which is not full. The CDHJKN full-factor statement is explicitly supported by Theorem 2.4. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But the final application depends essentially on the relative Bass–Serre proposition, the coefficient-boundary lemmas, and the boundary-support intersection lemma. Since those are not fully proved, the final conclusion is not established.

### Paragraph-by-paragraph audit

#### Problem statement and interpretation

The proof explicitly interprets irreducibility as nontrivial join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This is a faithful and standard interpretation of the problem statement. No issue remains here.

#### Support bookkeeping

The previous draft’s support bookkeeping was too abrupt. The current draft gives a clearer route:

1. Work with ordinary open supports \(r_Y\subset A^{**}\).
2. Use DKEP’s simultaneous-normal bidual construction to identify the normal corner.
3. Use Proposition 2.4, Corollary 3.2, and Proposition 3.6 to show that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same simultaneous-normal annihilator, hence the same compressed support.

This now sufficiently explains why approximate units from the original boundary \(X\) may be used to approximate the DKEP support \(q_X\). This was one of the central earlier gaps, and it is now addressed.

Minor note: the support formula is still written compactly, but the cited DKEP framework plus the argument supplied in the proof is enough for this level of solution.

#### Subalgebra fusion lemma

The previous draft did not adequately justify the coefficient-boundary identification for \(L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\). The revision fixes this by avoiding a naive elementary-tensor density argument and instead using DKEP Theorem 5.10.

The proof now correctly uses:

- \(H={}_NL^2N_B\),
- DKEP Example 5.11 for the left boundary \(X_B\),
- the full boundary \(B(L^2B)\) on the \(B\)-side,
- the fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\),
- DKEP Theorem 5.10 to pass from cyclic elementary tensors to all bounded vectors,
- DKEP Proposition 5.9 to obtain coefficient membership in \(K_{X_B}(N)\).

This addresses the earlier objection that arbitrary bounded coefficients were not controlled.

The statement that \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by positive coefficients of \(\mathcal H_B\) is also acceptable: \(e_B\) is the coefficient of the cyclic vector \(\widehat1\otimes_B\widehat1\), and DKEP Example 3.4 identifies \(X_B\) as the hereditary algebra generated by \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-translates of the Jones projection/corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### Finite fusion calculus

The previous direct-sum and finite-fusion lemma was underproved. The new version is much stronger and now explicitly uses condition (1) of DKEP Theorem 5.10. The direct-sum argument via the union of cyclic sets is valid, and the fusion argument using \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) is valid.

The product formula

\[
c_1c_2
=
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{*}T_\beta
\]

is correctly oriented under the DKEP convention \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). The use of a finite direct sum to place the two coefficient vectors in a common correspondence is also appropriate.

The approximate-unit construction for \(X_{M_U}\) from finite positive sums of coefficients and functional calculus is standard and now adequately justified.

#### Boundary-support intersection lemma

This was the central technical weakness in the earlier drafts. The revision now gives a coherent and rigorous proof.

The key chain is:

1. Finite-fusion coefficient products \(hkh\) decompose into coefficients of finite fusions whose labels include both \(W\) and \(U\).
2. CDHJKN’s fusion rule decomposes those fusions into direct sums of \(H_C\) with \(C\subset U\cap W\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
3. The direct-sum/fusion lemma puts those coefficients in \(K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}(M)\).
4. The limiting argument is performed in the ordinary bidual \(B(L^2M)^{**}\), not in the normal bidual, so the nonmultiplicativity issue is avoided.
5. Only after obtaining \(r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_I}\) is the result compressed by the simultaneous normal support \(p\).

This proves

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]

The induction to the palindromic product is then valid.

#### Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This section is now acceptable. The state-compression argument is correct, and the GNS argument using the palindromic positive contraction is careful about the fact that \(\widetilde S(N)\) is only an operator system. The use of DKEP Lemma 8.5’s u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner is supported by the cited source. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The AFP reduced-word argument is now detailed enough. The commutator formula with \(p\) and \(q\), the orthogonality \(q w_1^*w_2q=0\), the inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q,
\]

and the \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) are all valid.

The invocation of DKEP Lemma 6.1 to place \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\), and DKEP Theorem 6.2 to conclude relative proper proximality, is appropriate. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### Graph-product application

The graph-product AFP decomposition is standard from the reduced-word model. The local graph combinatorics are correct. I also brute-force checked the two combinatorial claims for all graphs with \(3\le |V|\le 6\): connected complement implies \(C(v)\neq\varnothing\), and if \(C(v)=\{r\}\), then there is \(s\in\operatorname{Lk}(v)\) with \(s\not\sim r\). No counterexample was found.

The reduced-word expectation computations for \(w_i\) and \(w_1^*w_2\) are valid.

The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is correct: for a join-irreducible graph with at least three vertices and trace-zero vertex unitaries, the graph product is a diffuse full factor. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Since the graph product is separable, diffuse amenability would imply hyperfiniteness, and the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor is not full; hence \(M\) is nonamenable.

## Fatal issue: the subalgebra-fusion lemma contains a false coefficient computation

In Lemma `subalgebra fusion`, the proof asserts:

\[
\text{“the positive coefficients of the vectors }x\zeta_By\text{ are }
xJyJ\,e_B\,Jy^*Jx^*.” 
\]

With DKEP’s convention \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\), this formula is not correct in general. DKEP explicitly defines \(T_\xi\) by \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

A simple check already disproves the displayed formula. Take \(B=\mathbb C\subset N\), with \(N\) a noncommutative finite factor, and take \(x,y\in\mathcal U(N)\). Then

\[
\mathcal H_B=L^2N\otimes L^2N,\qquad \zeta_B=1\otimes 1.
\]

For \(\xi=x\zeta_By=x\otimes y\), a direct computation gives

\[
T_\xi(\widehat b)
 = L_\xi^*R_\xi(\widehat b)
 = \tau(x^*bx)\widehat{y^*y}
 = \tau(b)\widehat 1,
\]

so \(T_\xi=e_{\mathbb C}\), hence \(T_\xi^*T_\xi=e_{\mathbb C}\). But the formula in the proof gives

\[
xJyJ\,e_{\mathbb C}\,Jy^*Jx^*,
\]

which is the rank-one projection onto the vector \(xy^*\) up to the usual identification. For \(xy^*\notin\mathbb C1\), this is not \(e_{\mathbb C}\). Thus the asserted coefficient identity is false.

This is not cosmetic. The proof uses that false identity to conclude that the hereditary algebra generated by positive coefficients of \(\mathcal H_B\) contains all generators \(xJyJe_B\) of \(X_B\). Without that step, the first assertion of Lemma `subalgebra fusion` is unproved.

### Consequences of this gap

The false coefficient computation propagates through the proof:

- Lemma `finite fusion calculus` uses Lemma `subalgebra fusion` to assert that \(X_{M_U}\) has an approximate unit built from positive coefficients of \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).
- Lemma `intersection of boundary supports` depends on those special approximate units.
- The final passage from relative proper proximality for the link boundaries \(X_{M_{L_v}}\) to proper proximality depends on Lemma `intersection`.

So the main technical bridge of the proof is still not rigorously established.

A correct route may exist using DKEP Example 5.11 and a linking/coefficient argument with \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), but the proof currently written does not provide it. DKEP Example 5.11 identifies \(X_H\) for \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) with the subalgebra boundary, but that is not the same as the false formula for positive coefficients of \(x\zeta_By\) in \(H\otimes_B\bar H\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

## Section-by-section audit

### Problem interpretation

The interpretation of irreducible as nontrivial join-irreducibility is appropriate, and the complement-connected equivalence is standard for finite simple graphs. The draft explicitly records this interpretation, satisfying the ambiguity requirement.

### Support bookkeeping lemma

This is much improved. The one-normal support statement is supported by DKEP Section 2.3, and the simultaneous-normal statement is plausibly obtained using DKEP Proposition 2.10. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) I no longer regard this as the primary fatal defect.

The equality \(pr_X=pr_{K_X(N)}=q_X\) is still compressed into a short annihilator argument, but it is plausible given DKEP Proposition 2.4 and Proposition 3.6. I would prefer more detail, but this is not the decisive obstruction.

### Lemma `subalgebra fusion`

This is the main failure. The proof’s central coefficient formula for \(x\zeta_By\) is false, as explained above. Since this lemma is used to build approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\), the rest of the proof cannot be certified.

The later use of DKEP Theorem 5.10 in the same lemma is plausible, but it does not repair the first assertion.

### Lemma `finite fusion calculus`

The shift from the previous coefficient \(*\)-algebra to a \(T_\xi\)-operator algebra is an improvement. DKEP indeed gives \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) and \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The claim that \(\mathcal T_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra is now much more credible.

However, the approximate-unit conclusion depends directly on the faulty Lemma `subalgebra fusion`. Therefore this lemma is not established as written.

### Lemma `intersection of boundary supports`

The use of CDHJKN’s fusion rule is appropriate in spirit. CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 decomposes the relevant graph-product bimodules, and Proposition 5.5 gives the fusion rule over intersections. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

But the proof relies on approximate units from the positive cones supplied by Lemma `finite fusion calculus`, which in turn relies on the false coefficient-generation argument. Thus the crucial inequality

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]

remains unproved in the submitted draft.

### Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This part is substantially improved and is likely correct conditional on the support framework. The compression argument, the GNS projection argument, and the use of the compact-corner u.c.p. map are now clearly stated. DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the bidual central-state criterion used here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### Bass–Serre proposition

This section is also improved. The reduced-word commutator formulas and paradoxical inequalities are plausible. The invocation of DKEP Lemma 6.1 is now better justified; DKEP Lemma 6.1 does allow checking the commutator condition on an ultraweakly dense generating \(*\)-subalgebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) I do not see a fatal issue here.

### Graph-product application

Conditional on the previous lemmas, the graph-product application is mostly correct. The AFP decomposition is standard in graph products, the choice of \(w_1,w_2\) is combinatorially sound, and CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 supports the diffuse full factor conclusion for join-irreducible graphs with \(|V|\ge3\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The final step still fails because the boundary-support intersection lemma is not proved.

---

## Overall verdict

The revision fixes several formal and expository issues, and the global strategy remains plausible. But `answer.tex` still does **not** constitute a complete rigorous proof. The revised Lemma `subalgebra fusion` contains a false coefficient identity, and that lemma is essential for constructing the approximate units used in the boundary-support intersection argument. Consequently the proof of the central inequality \(q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}\) is not valid as written.

### LaTeX contract

The revised file appears to satisfy the formal LaTeX contract: it uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, only `fullpage` for layout, no forbidden spacing/font-size commands, and is a standalone document. The author reports a clean two-pass `pdflatex` compile to 6 pages; I see no textual LaTeX-contract violation in the submitted source.

### Literature checks

The broad external citations are relevant. DKEP define \(T_\xi\) for a bounded vector by \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\), and they record the Connes-fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Example 5.11 indeed defines \(X_H\) as the hereditary algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and says that for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence this recovers the subalgebra boundary of Example 3.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.10 supports the mixing criterion used in the proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the bidual central-state criterion for proper proximality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 support the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule, and CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 supports the final full-factor claim. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

### What the revision fixed

The previous fatal translated-Jones-vector coefficient formula has been removed. The replacement correctly uses DKEP Example 5.11 for the elementary identity
\[
T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta.
\]
This addresses the exact counterexample I gave in the previous report.

The finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma and Bass–Serre argument remain substantially improved and are plausible conditional on the technical boundary-intersection lemma.

### Remaining fatal issue: \(T_\xi\) is still treated as if it were linear in \(\xi\)

The revised Lemma `subalgebra fusion` still has a serious gap. It defines \(\mathcal E_B\) as the linear span of the elementary operators \(T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}\), then says:

> “The reverse inclusion follows from the displayed identity, because \(T_\alpha\in\mathcal E_B\) for every bounded finite elementary sum \(\alpha\).”

This does **not** follow. DKEP’s operator is
\[
T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi,
\]
so it is quadratic in \(\xi\), not linear. In general,
\[
T_{\xi+\eta}
=
T_\xi+T_\eta+L_\xi^*R_\eta+L_\eta^*R_\xi,
\]
and \(T_{\lambda\xi}=|\lambda|^2T_\xi\), not \(\lambda T_\xi\). The fusion identity proves the formula for **elementary tensors**, but not for finite sums inside the same correspondence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Thus the proof does not establish that, for a bounded finite elementary sum
\[
\alpha=\sum_j \eta_j\otimes_B\bar\xi_j\in \mathcal H_B,
\]
one has \(T_\alpha\in\mathcal E_B\), nor that \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\in X_B\). This is exactly what is needed for the claimed reverse inclusion \(Y\subset X_B\) in Lemma `subalgebra fusion`.

This is not merely a wording problem: the later approximate-unit construction in Lemma `finite fusion calculus` depends on the assertion that \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by the stated positive \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\)’s. Since that assertion is still unproved, the boundary-support intersection lemma is not rigorously established.

### Downstream effect

Because Lemma `subalgebra fusion` remains incomplete, the following chain is still broken:

1. Lemma `subalgebra fusion` does not rigorously prove the required positive-generator description of \(X_B\).
2. Lemma `finite fusion calculus` uses that generator description to build approximate units in the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_U\}\).
3. Lemma `intersection of boundary supports` uses those approximate units to prove
   \[
   0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}.
   \]
4. The final proof of proper proximality depends on that intersection lemma.

The global strategy remains plausible, and this may be repairable by explicitly proving that \(T_\alpha\) for finite elementary sums belongs to \(X_B\), perhaps via the full \(e_B\mathbb B(L^2N)e_B\) corner in Example 3.4 or a linking-operator computation. But that proof is not present in `answer.tex`.

### Other comments

The support-bookkeeping lemma is much better motivated than in earlier versions, though still somewhat compressed. Since the proof already fails at the subalgebra-fusion/approximate-unit step, I do not need to decide whether the support bookkeeping is fully acceptable.

The graph-product application is correct only conditional on the unresolved intersection lemma. The cited CDHJKN results do support the intended bimodule and full-factor inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

### Verdict

The revision fixes the previous explicit false formula, but it introduces or leaves in place a related essential gap: it treats the DKEP coefficient operator \(T_\xi\) as if it were linear in finite sums of vectors. This invalidates the proof of the positive-generator description of \(X_B\), and hence the central boundary-support intersection argument remains incomplete.

## 3. Detailed mathematical audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation “irreducible = not a nontrivial graph join = connected complement graph” is standard and is explicitly recorded. This is good and addresses the ambiguity in the problem statement.

The assumption that all traces are faithful normal traces is also recorded. This is reasonable, since graph products of tracial von Neumann algebras are normally formulated with faithful normal traces.

No issue here.

---

### DKEP notation and \(\widetilde S_X(N)\)

The definition of \(\mathcal A_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*\), the support \(q_X\), and \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) is compatible with DKEP Lemma 8.5. The statement that for a separable finite factor, “normal restriction to \(N\)” is equivalent to restriction equal to the trace is acceptable: an \(N\)-central normal state on a finite factor is the unique trace.

No major issue here.

---

### Lemma “support bookkeeping”

This is the first serious problem.

The lemma asserts that for a hereditary \(N,JNJ\)-invariant subalgebra \(Y\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), with ordinary open support \(r_Y\in A^{**}\), one has
\[
r_Yp=pr_Y,\qquad (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y),
\]
where \(\mathcal A_N=pA^{**}p\).

This is an essential structural assertion. It is later used to identify the DKEP support \(q_X\) with the compression \(pr_X\), and then to turn an ordinary-bidual inequality
\[
r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_I}
\]
into the normal-bidual inequality
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I.
\]

The cited DKEP Proposition 2.10 does **not**, as stated, give this open-support formula. It records that the simultaneous normal bidual is the intersection of the two one-normal biduals and gives a non-unital inclusion for invariant subalgebras, but it does not identify the unit of \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) with the ordinary open support \(r_Y\) compressed by a projection \(p\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The proof sketch says that “the universal normal representation of \(B\) is the restriction of the universal normal representation of \(A\) to the reducing subspace with support \(r_B\).” This is a nontrivial statement for hereditary, non-ideal subalgebras in the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal category. It is not proved in the answer and is not directly quoted from DKEP.

This is not a cosmetic gap: the main boundary-intersection lemma depends on precisely this support identification. Without a rigorous proof of this support bookkeeping lemma, the proof of the theorem is incomplete.

---

### Equality of supports for \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\)

The second part of the support lemma claims
\[
pr_X=pr_{K_X(N)}=q_X.
\]

The annihilator argument using DKEP Proposition 2.4 and Proposition 3.6 is plausible: DKEP Proposition 3.6 indeed says that \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) is the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of both \(X\) and \(K_X(M)\), and DKEP Proposition 2.4 relates simultaneous strong bimodule closure to annihilation by simultaneous-normal functionals. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

But the conclusion again depends on the previous, insufficiently justified identification of normal-bidual support projections with compressed ordinary open supports. Equal annihilators in \(A^{\sharp_J}\) imply equality of the weak-* closures inside \(\mathcal A_N\), but the answer still needs a rigorous bridge from those weak-* closures to the specific projections \(pr_X\) and \(pr_{K_X}\). That bridge is exactly the missing support formula above.

So this part is not independently fatal, but it inherits the fatal gap in the support bookkeeping.

---

### Lemma “subalgebra fusion”

The first half, identifying \(X_B\) with the hereditary algebra generated by the coefficient operators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) for \(H={}_NL^2N_B\), is supported by DKEP Example 5.11. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The positive-generation argument using hereditary supports is reasonable and fixes a common pitfall involving the nonlinearity of \(\xi\mapsto T_\xi\).

The later claim that every coefficient operator \(T_\alpha\) of a bounded vector in any direct sum of copies of \(\mathcal H_B\) lies in \(K_{X_B}(N)\) is plausible via DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9, but the proof is compressed. In particular, it should explicitly justify that the elementary tensors used are cyclic in the sense of Theorem 5.10 and that their coefficient operators lie in the required \(X_B\mathbb B(L^2N)X_B\) class, not merely in \(X_B\). This is likely repairable, but as written it is a gap in the details.

Not independently fatal, but it requires expansion.

---

### Lemma “finite fusion calculus”

The stability of relative mixing under finite Connes fusion, conjugation, and direct sums is plausible from DKEP Theorem 5.10 and the identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But this is a nontrivial closure statement, and the proof is very terse.

The approximate-unit construction for \(X_{M_U}\) from the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_U\}\) is reasonable, provided the previous subalgebra-fusion lemma is fully justified.

This section is not the main obstruction, but it rests on earlier insufficiently detailed coefficient-boundary arguments.

---

### Lemma “intersection of boundary supports”

This is the central technical lemma, and it depends critically on the flawed support bookkeeping.

The CDHJKN input is valid: their Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 do provide the required decomposition and fusion rule for the graph-product bimodules. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

The algebraic part of the argument—expanding \(hkh\) into monomials in \(T_\xi\)’s and using the fusion rule to force all labels into \(U\cap W\)—is plausible, but several details are suppressed:

1. One must carefully handle that \(\xi\mapsto T_\xi\) is not linear. The proof uses linear spans of \(T_\xi\)’s, so finite sums are acceptable only because \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\) is a linear space, but this should be stated explicitly.

2. One must justify that the CDHJKN Hilbert-bimodule decomposition preserves bounded vectors and the \(T_\xi\)-operator calculus sufficiently to conclude membership in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).

3. The passage from norm-closure of the positive cones to the ordinary-bidual support inequality depends on the unproved formula \(q_X=pr_X\).

The final compression step
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W=pr_W\,pr_U\,pr_W=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p\le pr_{K_I}p=q_I
\]
is not justified unless the support bookkeeping lemma is fully proved. Since that lemma is not adequately established, this intersection lemma remains unproved.

This is a fatal gap because the finite-family-to-compact-boundary step needs precisely this intersection result.

---

### Lemma “from finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary”

This lemma is mostly sound in outline.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\) is plausible: \(q_i\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\) by the DKEP support construction, and the compressed state would contradict relative proper proximality via DKEP Lemma 8.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The GNS argument with an extension of \(\omega\) to \(\mathcal A_N\) is also plausible. The author correctly notes that the extension is only used to show \(\omega(R)=1\) and \(\omega(q_0)=1\), while centrality is used only on \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\subset \widetilde S(N)\).

The use of the canonical u.c.p. map
\[
\Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0
\]
is consistent with the map appearing in the proof of DKEP Lemma 8.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This lemma is acceptable, modulo the prior unproved fact that the palindromic product really lies in \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\).

---

### Proposition “relative Bass–Serre paradox”

The proof is a reasonable relative analogue of the free-product paradoxical-decomposition argument in DKEP. The projection computations with first-letter \(P\) and first-letter \(Q\) subspaces are plausible.

Some details are compressed but likely repairable:

- The proof should explicitly state that the commutator computations verify both \(x\) and \(x^*\) hypotheses needed to apply DKEP Lemma 6.1.
- It should justify that \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\) and that \(w_iqw_i^*, apa^*\) remain in the operator system where the state is evaluated.
- The pairwise orthogonality of \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) follows from \(B\)-Haarity, but the proof should spell out the calculation \(E_B(h^{-n}h^m)=0\) for \(m\ne n\).

I do not see a fatal error here, but the proof is terse.

---

### Application to graph products

The graph-product AFP decomposition
\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]
is standard, but the answer should cite CF17 or CDHJKN explicitly at this point.

The combinatorial construction of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct: if \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) force another complement-neighbor \(s\) of \(r\), and since \(C(v)=\{r\}\), this \(s\) lies in \(L_v\).

The expectation computations onto \(M_{L_v}\) are plausible by reduced-word calculus, though again a few lines of detail would improve rigor.

The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is valid: the cited theorem gives diffuse full factoriality for join-irreducible graph products with at least three vertices and trace/state-zero vertex unitaries. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) The argument that a diffuse amenable finite factor cannot be full is standard.

Thus the final application is sound assuming all previous technical lemmas, especially the boundary-support intersection lemma, are valid. But because the support-intersection lemma is not rigorously established, the final proof does not close.

---

## 4. Main blocking issues

The following issues prevent readiness:

1. **Unproved normal-bidual support formula.**  
   The identity
   \[
   (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y)
   \]
   and the commutation \(r_Yp=pr_Y\) are essential and are not supplied by the cited DKEP Proposition 2.10 as stated. This is the main fatal gap.

2. **Boundary-support intersection depends on that unsupported formula.**  
   The proof of
   \[
   0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
   \]
   collapses unless the ordinary-bidual support calculation can be rigorously transported into DKEP’s simultaneous normal bidual.

3. **Several coefficient/mixing closure arguments are too compressed.**  
   These are probably repairable, but the current text does not fully verify the hypotheses of DKEP Theorem 5.10 in the subalgebra-fusion and finite-fusion lemmas.

Because these are essential to the proof, `answer.tex` is not a complete rigorous solution.

## 2. Literature cross-check

The cited literature supports several broad ingredients:

- DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives the simultaneous-normal bidual framework and a **non-unital inclusion** for invariant subalgebras, but its stated content is more limited than what the revised proof uses in the support-bookkeeping lemma. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.10 gives the equivalence between cyclic bounded vectors with \(T_\xi\in YB(L^2M,L^2N)X\), mixing, and relative compactness; it also records \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Example 5.11 identifies \(X_H\) as the hereditary algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and for \(H={}_M L^2M_B\) recovers the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives the central-state criterion and explicitly works with u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN Proposition 5.5 gives the graph-product fusion rule with summands indexed by subsets of the intersection. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))
- CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 supports the final statement that a join-irreducible graph product with at least three vertices and state-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

Thus the broad cited framework is relevant. The problem is not the choice of references, but that the revised proof still uses several consequences not actually proved in the text or directly supplied by the cited results.

## 4. Section-by-section audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The adopted interpretation is faithful to the usual meaning of join-irreducibility, and the proof later uses precisely this interpretation.

### Normal support bookkeeping lemma

This is still the main technical obstruction.

The lemma claims that for a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra \(Y\subset A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\),
\[
(Y^{\sharp_J})^*
 =\overline Y^{\,\sigma(\mathcal A_N,A_N^{\sharp_J})}
 =q_Y\mathcal A_Nq_Y,
\]
and that every contractive approximate unit of \(Y\) converges ultraweakly in \(\mathcal A_N\) to \(q_Y\).

The restriction-map/annihilator part is plausible: DKEP Proposition 2.2 gives a Hahn–Banach extension theorem for simultaneous normal functionals, and DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives a non-unital inclusion of \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) into \(\mathcal A_N\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the proof then says:

> This weak* closure \(W\) is hereditary: \(YA_NY\subset Y\), \(A_N\) is weak* dense in \(\mathcal A_N\), and multiplication in \(\mathcal A_N\) is separately weak* continuous, so \(W\mathcal A_NW\subset W\).

This step is not justified. It implicitly treats the canonical map
\[
A_N\to \mathcal A_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
as multiplicative, at least when sandwiched by elements of \(Y\). But DKEP’s normal-bidual construction does not provide this multiplicativity in the form needed here. In Lemma 8.5, DKEP explicitly obtain u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into normal-bidual corners; u.c.p. is not enough to transfer concrete products \(yaz\) into products of their images in the von Neumann algebra \(\mathcal A_N\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This is not a cosmetic point. The proof needs
\[
\iota(y_1)\,\iota(a)\,\iota(y_2)=\iota(y_1ay_2)
\]
or at least
\[
\iota(Y)\,\iota(A_N)\,\iota(Y)\subset \overline{\iota(Y)}^{\,uw}
\]
inside \(\mathcal A_N\). Neither is proved. Proposition 2.10, as stated, gives a non-unital inclusion of biduals for invariant subalgebras; it does not by itself prove that the weak-* closure of \(Y\) inside \(\mathcal A_N\) is the hereditary corner \(q_Y\mathcal A_Nq_Y\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

So the revised support-bookkeeping lemma remains unproved in an essential place.

### Subalgebra fusion lemma

This section is improved and mostly plausible.

The identification of \(X_B\) with the hereditary algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) is supported by DKEP Example 5.11. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The positive-generation argument using \(s^*s\) and \(ss^*\) is sound.

The use of DKEP Theorem 5.10 to conclude mixing of \(\mathcal H_B\) relative to \(X_B\times X_B\) is terse but plausible: elementary tensors are cyclic, and their \(T\)-operators lie in \(X_B\subset \overline{X_BA_NX_B}\). Proposition 5.9 then supports passing from relative compactness to membership of \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) and \(T_\alpha T_\alpha^*\) in \(K_{X_B}(N)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

I do not regard this lemma as the fatal problem.

### Finite fusion calculus lemma

The stability of mixing under finite Connes fusion, conjugates, and direct sums is plausible from DKEP Theorem 5.10 and the formula \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The \(*\)-algebra property of \(\mathcal T_U\) is also plausible because the definition explicitly includes linear spans, finite direct sums, fusions, and conjugates.

This lemma is still compressed, but it is probably repairable. It is not the main obstruction.

### Intersection of boundary supports

This is the central fatal gap.

The algebraic fusion input is valid in broad outline: CDHJKN Proposition 5.5 gives the required fusion rule, with summands indexed by subsets of intersections. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) Thus the concrete coefficient calculation plausibly shows that the **concrete operator product**
\[
h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma
\]
belongs to \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), where \(I=U\cap W\).

But the proof then takes ultraweak limits **inside \(\mathcal A_M\)** and treats
\[
h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma
\]
as the product in \(\mathcal A_M\) of the images of \(h_\alpha,k_\beta,h_\gamma\). This is exactly where the proof needs multiplicativity of the canonical map
\[
\mathbb B(L^2M)\to \mathcal A_M.
\]
The concrete coefficient calculus proves membership of the concrete product in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\). It does **not** prove that the product of the three images in \(\mathcal A_M\) lies in the corner \(q_I\mathcal A_Mq_I\).

This invalidates the passage:
\[
h_\alpha\to q_W,\qquad k_\beta\to q_U
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\to q_Wq_Uq_W
\]
with all intermediate products lying in \(q_I\mathcal A_Mq_I\).

In short: the revised proof avoids the explicit ordinary-bidual formula \(q_X=pr_X\), but it replaces it with a direct normal-bidual multiplication argument that is not justified. The earlier ordinary-bidual strategy was designed precisely to avoid this nonmultiplicativity problem. The revised proof has not supplied a valid replacement.

Therefore the key inequality
\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
is still not rigorously proved.

### Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This lemma is largely sound assuming the palindromic product hypothesis. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) and the use of DKEP Lemma 8.5 are reasonable. DKEP Lemma 8.5 indeed gives the relevant central-state criterion and the u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, this lemma depends on the intersection lemma to verify the hypothesis
\[
R\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
Since the intersection lemma is not proved, this step cannot complete the theorem.

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

This section is improved and appears plausible. The reduced-word projection computations are standard, and the added calculation showing pairwise orthogonality of \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) is helpful.

I would still prefer an explicit citation for the AFP normal form and for the version of DKEP Theorem 6.2 being used, but I do not see a fatal mathematical error here.

### Application to graph products

The graph-combinatorial construction of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct. The AFP decomposition is standard for graph products and is now at least cited to CF17.

The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is supported: for a join-irreducible graph with \(|V|\ge3\) and state-zero vertex unitaries, the graph product is a diffuse full factor. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) The conclusion that such a factor is nonamenable is standard, since the diffuse amenable finite factor is hyperfinite and not full.

This final application would be fine if the earlier boundary-intersection lemma were valid.

## 5. Summary of decisive problems

The revised proof is not answer-ready for one central reason:

- The proof still lacks a rigorous valid mechanism for multiplying approximating concrete operators inside DKEP’s simultaneous normal bidual. The coefficient/fusion calculation occurs in \(\mathbb B(L^2M)\), while the limiting support product \(q_Wq_Uq_W\) occurs in \(\mathcal A_M=(\mathbb B(L^2M)^{\sharp_J})^*\). The proof does not justify that the canonical map from \(\mathbb B(L^2M)\) to \(\mathcal A_M\) preserves the products being used.

This gap affects the essential intersection lemma and therefore the entire reduction from finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary. The proof may be repairable by returning to a carefully proved ordinary-bidual support-compression argument or by proving a genuine normal-bidual sandwich theorem, but the current `answer.tex` does not contain such a proof.
