## 2. Previous fatal objection: resolved

The previous fatal issue was that the proof multiplied concrete operators after passing into DKEP’s simultaneous normal bidual, where the canonical map from \(B(L^2M)\) is not generally multiplicative. The revised proof now explicitly moves the boundary-support intersection calculation into the ordinary bidual \(A^{**}\), where \(A\hookrightarrow A^{**}\) is a genuine \(*\)-homomorphism, and only compresses by the DKEP normal-support projection afterward.

This is the correct repair. DKEP explicitly realize the one-normal bidual as \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), identify invariant hereditary subalgebras by ordinary support projections commuting with \(p_{\rm nor}\), and also warn that the canonical embedding into the normal bidual is not generally a \(*\)-homomorphism. DKEP Proposition 2.10 supplies the simultaneous-normal intersection framework. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

## 3. Section-by-section mathematical audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The proof explicitly records the interpretation of irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connectedness of the complement graph. This is faithful to the intended graph-product usage and removes the ambiguity in the original formulation.

No issue.

---

### Ordinary support bookkeeping

The revised Lemma `ordinary support bookkeeping` is now much better than the previous normal-bidual weak-* closure argument. The support formula
\[
(Y^{\sharp_J})^* = r_Yp_N A_N^{**}p_Nr_Y
\]
is justified by combining DKEP’s one-normal support discussion with Proposition 2.10. This is precisely the missing bridge in the prior draft.

The statement that \(q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X\) follows from the equality of simultaneous-normal annihilators of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\), using DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Proposition 2.4, is also acceptable. DKEP Proposition 3.6 identifies the relevant \(K_X^{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(X\) and \(K_X\), and Proposition 2.4 is the correct annihilator/strong-closure bridge. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Minor nonfatal comment: for a completely general DKEP boundary piece, one should be careful that \(M(X)\cap M\) is only ultraweakly dense in \(M\), not necessarily equal to \(M\). In this proof, however, the support-bookkeeping lemma is used for the subalgebra boundary pieces \(X_{M_U}\) and for \(K_X\), where the multiplier hypotheses needed for the displayed formula are available. Thus this does not affect the proof.

---

### Subalgebra fusion lemma

This revision properly fixes the earlier linearity issue. The proof no longer treats \(\alpha\mapsto T_\alpha\) as linear. Instead it uses the self-adjoint set
\[
\mathcal E_B=\{T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}\}
\]
and the hereditary-support argument: if \(s^*s,ss^*\in Y\), then \(s\in Y\). This is sound.

The use of DKEP Example 5.11 is appropriate: that example identifies the coefficient boundary generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and for \(H={}_M L^2M_B\) it recovers the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\). DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9 then justify passing from cyclic coefficient control to relative compactness of all bounded coefficients. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No fatal issue.

---

### Finite fusion calculus

The stability of mixing under finite Connes fusion, conjugation, and arbitrary direct sums is now stated and justified by the cyclic-vector criterion in DKEP Theorem 5.10 and the identity
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi.
\]
The proof correctly uses the hereditary property \(XB(L^2N)X\subset X\).

The construction of approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\) from the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_U\}\) is also valid: finite positive sums of coefficient generators lie in the \(*\)-algebra \(\mathcal T_U\), and functional calculus with zero-constant-term polynomial approximation keeps the approximants inside the desired norm closure.

No fatal issue.

---

### Intersection of boundary supports

This is the central repaired section, and it now works.

The proof first establishes the concrete operator statement:
\[
a b c\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M),\qquad I=U\cap W,
\]
for \(a,c\in\mathcal P_W\), \(b\in\mathcal P_U\). The expansion into monomials in \(T_\xi\)’s and adjoints is legitimate because \(\mathcal T_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra and adjoints are handled by conjugate correspondences. The CDHJKN fusion rule supports the conclusion that any resulting finite fusion with both \(U\)- and \(W\)-labels decomposes into summands \(H_C\) with \(C\subset U\cap W\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

The limiting argument is now done in the ordinary bidual \(A^{**}\):
\[
h_\alpha\to r_W,\qquad k_\beta\to r_U,
\]
and since each \(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\) lies in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), its weak-* limit lies in the ordinary support corner \(\rho_I A^{**}\rho_I\). This gives
\[
r_Wr_Ur_W\le \rho_I.
\]
Only after this does the proof compress by \(p\):
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W=p\,r_Wr_Ur_W\,p\le p\rho_Ip=q_I.
\]
This directly resolves the earlier nonmultiplicativity objection.

No fatal issue remains in this lemma.

---

### Finite relative-boundaries-to-compact-boundary lemma

The proof is sound.

The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) gives a central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\) if \(\omega(q_i^\perp)>0\), and the normality-on-\(N\) argument is correct because the compressed functional is dominated by a scalar multiple of the trace on \(N_+\).

The GNS argument after extending \(\omega\) to \(\mathcal A_N\) is also correct: each \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, so the palindromic product has value \(1\). Since that product lies in the compact corner, \(\omega(q_0)=1\). The final hypertrace construction uses the canonical DKEP u.c.p. map \(B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\), which is exactly the right replacement for any false identification of \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) with \(B(L^2N)\). DKEP Lemma 8.5 supplies this normal-bidual criterion and the relevant u.c.p. maps. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No issue.

---

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The Bass–Serre/free-product argument is now sufficiently detailed.

The projections \(p,q\) onto first-letter \(P\)- and \(Q\)-summands are shown to lie in \(S_{X_B}(N)\) via the commutator computation and DKEP Lemma 6.1. The paradoxical inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
are justified by the reduced-word calculus and the assumptions
\[
E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0.
\]
The added calculation proving pairwise orthogonality of \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) is correct.

No fatal issue.

---

### Application to graph products

The graph-product AFP decomposition is standard and appropriately cited to CF17. The graph-combinatorial construction of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct in both cases:

- if \(C(v)\) has two vertices \(r,t\), take \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\);
- if \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) gives \(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim r\), and \(w_2=u_su_r\) works.

The expectation computations onto \(M_{L_v}\) are standard reduced-word consequences.

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 supports the claim that a join-irreducible graph product with at least three vertices and vertex state-zero unitaries is a diffuse full factor. In the tracial setting, “state-zero” is exactly trace-zero. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) The conclusion that \(M\) is nonamenable follows because a diffuse amenable finite factor is the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor, which is not full.

No issue.

## 4. Overall verdict

The revision addresses the previous fatal objection. The proof now performs the support-intersection computation in the ordinary bidual, where multiplication is legitimate, and only then compresses to the simultaneous normal bidual. The rest of the argument is supported by the cited DKEP and CDHJKN results, and I do not find any remaining mathematical gap that affects validity.

## Literature validation

The cited literature is real and relevant.

DKEP does define the normal-bidual machinery, boundary pieces, \(K_X(M)\), \(\widetilde S_X(M)\), and the characterization of proper proximality via absence of central states on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). In particular, DKEP Lemma 8.5 states the relevant no-central-state criterion in the normal bidual. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9 support the use of bounded-vector coefficient operators \(T_\xi\), the fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), and the relative-compactness criterion via \(T^*T\) and \(TT^*\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the coefficient boundary piece \(X_H\), and in the case \(H=L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence it recovers the subalgebra boundary from Example 3.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 6.1 and Theorem 6.2 are also the right tools for the Bass–Serre/proper-proximality argument. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

CDHJKN contains the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used in the intersection lemma. Their fusion formula has the form
\[
H_{U_1}(V_1,V_2)\otimes_{M_{V_2}}H_{U_2}(V_2,V_3)
 \cong \bigoplus_{W\subset U_1\cap U_2}H_W(V_1,V_3)^{\oplus k},
\]
which is exactly the kind of input the proof needs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 also supports the final claim that a join-irreducible graph product with at least three vertices and state-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) Caspers–Fima support the star/link amalgamated-free-product decomposition used in equation (8). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/30688))

## Previously raised concerns and their status

### 1. Problem interpretation

**Addressed.** The revised draft explicitly records that “irreducible” means join-irreducible, equivalently connected complement graph. This is a faithful and standard interpretation of the stated condition.

### 2. Normality of central states in the factor case

**Addressed.** The revised draft now correctly says that for a finite factor, ruling out central states whose restriction is normal is equivalent to ruling out central states restricting to the trace, after normalization.

### 3. Direct sums and DKEP Theorem 5.10

**Mostly addressed.** The revised text now argues using the cyclic-vector criterion from DKEP Theorem 5.10 rather than informal finite-support approximation. This is much better. The direct-sum argument is still concise, but it is now plausibly valid: the union of cyclic sets in the summands is cyclic for the Hilbert direct sum, and Theorem 5.10 then controls arbitrary bounded vectors.

### 4. Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

**Substantially addressed.** The revised proof now explicitly handles the operator-system issue by extending the state only as a GNS device and noting that the relevant projections and palindromic product lie in \(\widetilde S(N)\). The compression argument and the use of the canonical u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner are now much clearer and align with DKEP Lemma 8.5.

### 5. Bass–Serre paradox

**Improved, probably acceptable modulo minor expansion.** The reduced-word explanation is now more detailed. The proof still compresses some AFP normal-form checks, but the argument is now recognizable and plausible: the first-letter projections, commutator computations, paradoxical inequalities, and \(B\)-Haar unitary argument all match the DKEP free-product strategy.

### 6. Graph-product application

**Mostly addressed.** The revised expectation computations for \(w_1,w_2\) are explicit enough, and the final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is supported by the literature.

## Remaining serious issue: support bookkeeping lemma

The main unresolved problem is Lemma `ordinary support bookkeeping`.

The proof asserts that if \(p_\ell,p_r\) are the one-normal support projections for the \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-module structures, then DKEP Proposition 2.10 “realizes”
\[
(A_N^{\sharp_J})^* = p_\ell p_r A_N^{**}p_\ell p_r,
\]
and “in particular” \(p_\ell,p_r\) commute. This is not stated in DKEP Proposition 2.10 in the form quoted. Proposition 2.10 states that the simultaneous normal bidual is the intersection of the two one-normal biduals inside \(M(A)^{**}\), and it gives a nonunital inclusion for invariant subalgebras; it does not explicitly identify the unit as \(p_\ell p_r\) or prove the commutation of \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This may be derivable, especially because the one-normal support projections lie in biduals of commuting multiplier algebras, but the derivation is not written. Since the later proof uses
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W=(pr_Wp)(pr_Up)(pr_Wp)=p\,r_Wr_Ur_W\,p,
\]
the exact support projection \(p\), its commutation with ordinary supports, and the legitimacy of multiplying these projections inside \(A^{**}\) are essential. The proof cannot simply cite Proposition 2.10 for a stronger product formula than the source visibly states.

There is also a missing hypothesis: DKEP’s one-normal support discussion for a subalgebra \(B\subset A\) assumes that the multiplier representation of \(M\) on \(B\) is faithful. The original draft included this faithfulness assumption; the revised lemma removed it. DKEP’s support statement explicitly includes a faithfulness condition for the subalgebra multiplier action. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) Boundary pieces used later probably satisfy faithfulness, but the lemma as stated is broader than the available theorem and should be corrected.

Finally, the equality
\[
q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X
\]
is still underjustified. The proof says DKEP Proposition 2.4 “identifies \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\) with the functionals continuous for this topology.” That is not the exact statement of Proposition 2.4. Proposition 2.4 is an annihilator characterization of simultaneous strong bimodule closure, not a direct identification with all \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-continuous functionals. The intended conclusion may be true using Proposition 3.6 plus the strong-bimodule annihilator formulation, but the current proof does not spell it out accurately enough.

Because Lemma `ordinary support bookkeeping` is used directly in the proof of the boundary-support intersection lemma, this is not a cosmetic issue. It is an essential technical bridge.

## Boundary-intersection lemma after revision

The revised monomial tracking is much improved. The argument that products of the form
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}
\]
can be rewritten, using conjugates and the identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), as a coefficient operator of a finite fusion whose labels include both \(W\) and \(U\), is now much clearer. The CDHJKN fusion rule then gives labels contained in \(W\cap U\), as required. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

However, the conclusion
\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
still depends on the ordinary-bidual support lemma. Since that lemma remains inadequately justified, the intersection lemma remains unproved at referee standard.

## Minor issues and possible fixes

1. **Restore faithfulness hypotheses** in Lemma `ordinary support bookkeeping`, or restrict it explicitly to the boundary pieces actually used.

2. **Replace \(p_\ell p_r\) by the meet/support projection** of the simultaneous normal bidual unless the commutation of \(p_\ell,p_r\) is proved. If the author wants to use \(p_\ell p_r\), they must prove that the one-normal supports commute in \(A_N^{**}\).

3. **Rewrite the proof of \(q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X\)** using the exact DKEP statements: Proposition 3.6 gives equality of \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closures, while Proposition 2.4 gives the simultaneous strong-bimodule annihilator characterization. The current wording is too imprecise.

4. In the Bass–Serre proposition, the reduced-word verification is probably correct, but a fully formal proof should explicitly handle the length-one cancellation cases in the AFP normal form.

Since this lemma is indispensable for converting relative boundary supports into the compact boundary, the proof is not yet a complete rigorous solution.

## Section-by-section audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation of irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph, is explicitly recorded. This resolves the ambiguity in the problem statement and matches the standard graph-product terminology.

### 2. Ordinary support bookkeeping

This was the main previous obstruction. The revision now:

1. restores the needed faithfulness hypothesis for the multiplier representations;
2. explains why the one-normal supports \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\) commute;
3. derives \(p_N=p_\ell p_r\) from the intersection realization of the simultaneous normal bidual;
4. applies the one-normal support statement separately to \(Y\);
5. proves the equality \(q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X\) using the strong-bimodule criterion rather than the earlier imprecise topology formulation.

This now adequately bridges concrete hereditary subalgebra supports and DKEP normal-bidual support projections. The proof of equality of supports for \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is no longer a handwave: it uses \(K_X=(K_X^L)^*K_X^L\), projection refinements, and Proposition 2.4 to show equality of simultaneous-normal annihilators.

### 3. Subalgebra fusion and finite fusion calculus

The revised treatment of direct sums via DKEP’s cyclic-vector criterion is acceptable. The proof no longer relies on an unjustified norm approximation of arbitrary bounded vectors. The use of
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*
\]
is consistent with DKEP’s correspondence calculus.

The positive-generation argument for \(X_B\) is also now sound: it avoids assuming linearity of \(\alpha\mapsto T_\alpha\) and correctly uses hereditary support.

### 4. Boundary-support intersection lemma

The revised proof of
\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
is now rigorous enough.

The monomial expansion
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}
\]
is correctly interpreted as a coefficient operator of a finite Connes fusion containing both \(W\)- and \(U\)-labels. CDHJKN’s fusion rule then forces the resulting summands to be indexed by subsets of \(W\cap U\). The final limiting step is performed in the ordinary bidual \(A^{**}\), before compressing by \(p_M\), so the previous nonmultiplicativity problem is resolved.

### 5. Finite relative boundaries to compact boundary

The finite-boundary lemma is now satisfactory. The revised proof correctly handles the fact that \(\widetilde S(N)\) is an operator system, not a C\(^*\)-algebra, by extending the state only for the GNS projection-fixing argument. The final hypertrace construction uses the DKEP u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner, so it no longer incorrectly identifies \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) with \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\).

### 6. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The AFP reduced-word calculation is sufficiently detailed. The projections \(p,q\) onto first-letter \(P\)- and \(Q\)-subspaces are shown to lie in \(S_{X_B}(N)\), and the paradoxical inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
are justified by reduced-word orthogonality. The \(B\)-Haar unitary argument then correctly forces a central state to vanish on \(e_B,p,q\), contradiction.

### 7. Graph-product application

The graph-combinatorial construction of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct in both cases for \(C(v)\). The expectation computations onto \(M_{L_v}\) are now explicit enough.

The intersection of all links is empty because no vertex belongs to its own link, and the boundary-intersection lemma therefore gives the compact-boundary palindromic product. The final use of CDHJKN to get a diffuse full factor, hence nonamenability, is valid under the trace-zero-unitary hypotheses.

### Resolution of previous concerns

**1. Support comparison \(X\) vs. \(K_X(N)\).**  
The previous draft had an insufficiently justified assertion that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same normal-bidual support. The revised proof now uses the column \(\binom SR\), DKEP Corollary 3.2, and the simultaneous projection criterion from DKEP Proposition 2.4 to obtain common cuts for \(S,R\in K_X^L\), then shows
\[
d_\alpha S^*R d_\beta=(Sd_\alpha)^*(Rd_\beta)\in X A_N X\subset X.
\]
This is the right mechanism. The argument now adequately proves equality of the simultaneous-normal annihilators, hence equality of the compressed supports \(p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X=q_X\).

**2. Fusion/direct-sum stability.**  
The revised Lemma `finite fusion calculus` now explicitly checks DKEP Theorem 5.10(1): cyclic vectors with \(T_\xi\in XA_NX\) remain cyclic under direct sums, conjugation, and Connes fusion, using
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*.
\]
This addresses the earlier criticism. One minor wording point: the map \(\xi\mapsto T_\xi\) is not linear in general, so “finite sums are absorbed by finite direct sums” should not be read literally for arbitrary complex linear combinations. But \(\mathcal T_U\) is defined as a linear span, and the proof only needs closure under products term-by-term, so this is not a fatal issue.

**3. Boundary-support intersection.**  
The revised proof now computes products in the ordinary bidual \(A^{**}\), not in the nonmultiplicative normal-bidual image. The monomial bookkeeping is explicit:
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}
=
T_{\alpha_4\otimes\bar\alpha_3\otimes\beta_2\otimes\bar\beta_1\otimes\alpha_2\otimes\bar\alpha_1}.
\]
Together with the CDHJKN fusion rule, this correctly yields coefficients controlled by \(X_{M_{U\cap W}}\). The weak-* limiting argument with ordinary open supports \(r_U,\rho_U\), followed only afterwards by compression by \(p_M\), is now mathematically sound.

**4. Finite relative boundaries to compact boundary.**  
The revised draft now correctly notes that the extension of the central state to \(\mathcal A_N\) is used only for the GNS projection argument and is not claimed to be central. The final hypertrace is obtained through the original state on the compact corner and the canonical DKEP u.c.p. map. This addresses the operator-system nuance from the previous review.

**5. Bass–Serre paradox.**  
The AFP normal-form index has been corrected to \(i_j\ne i_{j+1}\). The commutator formulas for the first-letter projections are now sufficiently explained on reduced words, and the translation of DKEP Lemma 6.1 into the right-action convention is explicit. The paradoxical inequalities and the \(B\)-Haar unitary argument are valid.

**6. Graph-product application.**  
The graph combinatorics are correct: complement connectedness gives \(C(v)\ne\varnothing\), and in the singleton case \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with \(|V|\ge3\) gives \(s\in \Lk(v)\) with \(s\not\sim r\). I also brute-force checked the corresponding finite-graph combinatorial claims for all graphs up to six vertices; no counterexample appeared. The expectation computations onto \(M_{\Lk(v)}\) are standard reduced-word computations. The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives a diffuse full factor, and therefore nonamenability follows because the diffuse amenable finite factor is hyperfinite and not full.

### Minor expository issues not affecting validity

- The support bookkeeping is technically dense; a future polished version should perhaps cite DKEP Proposition 2.4, Corollary 3.2, and Proposition 3.6 at the exact comparison step rather than only by name.
- The proposition on the Bass–Serre paradox is stated for a general AFP \(N\), while the normal-bidual finite-boundary criterion is ultimately used for the graph-product factor. This is harmless in the application, but the paper could explicitly say that the concrete DKEP notion of relative proper proximality agrees with the \(\widetilde S_X\)-criterion once the final factor/nonamenability hypotheses are known.

### Verdict

The revision resolves the previous essential objections. I do not find any remaining mathematical gap that affects the validity of the proof, nor any LaTeX-contract violation.

This is now adequately repaired. The earlier proof relied on a questionable assertion that \(K_X^L\) was exactly the simultaneous strong right module generated by \(A_NX\). The revised proof instead uses:

\[

from DKEP Proposition 3.6, and then DKEP Theorem 5.6 to show that every simultaneous-normal functional vanishing on \(X A_N X\) vanishes on \(K_X(N)\). Since \(X\) is hereditary, vanishing on \(X\) implies vanishing on \(X A_N X\). This proves equality of the simultaneous-normal annihilators of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\), and therefore equality of the corresponding normal-bidual supports.

A tiny expository improvement would be to explicitly say that, for hereditary \(X\subset A_N\), \(X A_N X\) is norm dense in, indeed essentially recovers, \(X\). But the current argument is already sufficient because it only needs \(X A_N X\subset X\) for the annihilation implication, and DKEP Theorem 5.6 is applied to the relevant \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure. and therefore
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I
\]
after compression by \(p\). This avoids all non-normal meet arguments and all nonmultiplicative-normal-bidual pitfalls.

No fatal issue remains.

---

### Finite relative boundaries to compact boundary

This lemma is sound.

The compression
\[
q^\perp \widetilde S_X(N)q^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
is valid because \(q_X\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\). DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the normal-bidual central-state criterion in the separable factor case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The GNS argument with the palindromic product is a good way to avoid invalid lattice-meet reasoning for non-normal states. The final hypertrace contradiction is standard: once \(\omega(q_0)=1\), the canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner yields a Connes hypertrace on \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\), contradicting nonamenability.

No issue.

---

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The AFP reduced-word calculation is correct. The first-letter projections \(p,q\) satisfy the displayed commutator formulas, and DKEP Lemma 6.1 justifies upgrading the generator check to all of \(N\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The paradoxical inequalities
\[
w_1 q w_1^*+w_2 q w_2^*\le p,\qquad a p a^*\le q
\]
are valid under the centeredness assumptions. The \(B\)-Haar unitary \(h=w_1a\) argument is also correct. DKEP Theorem 6.2 is the right criterion for excluding central states on \(S_{X_B}(N)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No issue.

---

### Graph-product application

The vertex AFP decomposition
\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]
is standard for graph products.

The combinatorial choice of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct in both cases:

- If \(C(v)\) has two vertices, choose two trace-zero vertex unitaries outside \(L_v\).
- If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) produce \(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim r\), and \(w_1=u_r,\ w_2=u_su_r\) satisfy the required expectation conditions.

The intersection of all vertex links is empty, so the intersection lemma gives the palindromic product in the compact corner \(q_\emptyset\mathcal A_Mq_\emptyset\). Since \(M_\emptyset=\mathbb C\), DKEP Example 3.4 gives \(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives that \(M\) is a diffuse full factor under the stated hypotheses, and hence \(M\) is nonamenable because the diffuse amenable finite factor is hyperfinite and not full. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

No issue.

---

## 5. New issues introduced?

I do not see any new mathematical issue introduced by the revision. The revised proof is longer in technical setup, but the additions directly address the earlier support-calculus and direct-sum objections.

The only minor stylistic point is that the support-comparison proof could explicitly mention the equality between the relevant same-boundary two-sided \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure and \(K_X^{\infty,1}\), using heredity of \(X\). But this is a routine inference and not an open gap.

---

## Final verdict

The revised `answer.tex` gives a complete rigorous solution of the stated problem under its explicitly recorded interpretation. It satisfies the First Proof LaTeX contract by inspection and by the author’s reported successful compilation. I find it answer-ready.

## `answer.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Irreducible means not a non-trivial graph join; equivalently the complement
graph \(\Gamma^c\) is connected.  All traces are faithful normal traces.  For
\(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical trace-preserving expectation.

We use the notation of Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson \cite{DKEP}.
For a finite algebra \(N\), put \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*,
\]
where \(\sharp_J\) means simultaneous normality for the \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-bimodule structures.  If \(X\) is an \(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\)
is DKEP's compact hereditary algebra and \(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\).  Set
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=\{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }
 a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For a separable finite factor, DKEP Lemma 8.5 says that proper proximality
relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the absence of an \(N\)-central state on
\(\widetilde S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is the trace.  Equivalently
one may rule out \(N\)-central states whose restriction is normal, since a
normal central state on a finite factor is the trace after normalization.

For a bounded vector \(\xi\) in an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence, \(T_\xi\) denotes
DKEP's coefficient operator \(L^2M\to L^2N\); if \(\eta\) is bounded in an
\(N\)-\(P\) correspondence, then
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes_N\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad
        T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^* .                                    \tag{1}
\]

\section*{Boundary supports and coefficients}

We use DKEP's ordinary-bidual realization of the normal bidual.  Thus
\(\cA_N\) is viewed as a von Neumann subalgebra of \(A_N^{**}\); its unit is
denoted by \(p_N\).  Products of support projections are computed inside the
honest von Neumann algebra \(A_N^{**}\), not by multiplying the
non-multiplicative canonical images of concrete operators.

\begin{lemma}[ordinary support bookkeeping]\label{lem:support}
Let \(Y\subset A_N\) be a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra invariant under the
multiplier actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), and assume that the induced
representations \(N,JNJ\to M(Y)\) are faithful.  Let \(r_Y\in A_N^{**}\) be
the ordinary open support projection of \(Y\).  Then \(r_Y\) commutes with
\(p_N\), and the support in \(\cA_N\) of the included corner
\((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) is \(p_Nr_Y\).  Every contractive approximate unit of
\(Y\) converges weak* in \(A_N^{**}\) to \(r_Y\).

If \(X\) is one of the boundary pieces used below and \(\rho_X\) denotes the
ordinary support of \(K_X(N)\), while \(r_X\) denotes the ordinary support of
\(X\), then
\[
        q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X .                                    \tag{2}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\) be DKEP's one-normal support projections in
\(A_N^{**}\), for the \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-module structures respectively.  In
the one-normal construction \(p_\ell\in N^{**}\) and
\(p_r\in (JNJ)^{**}\).  Since the concrete algebras \(N\) and \(JNJ\) commute
in \(A_N\), their bidual images commute in \(A_N^{**}\): approximate elements
of the biduals weak* by bounded nets from the concrete algebras and use
separate weak* continuity of multiplication.  Hence \(p_\ell p_r=p_rp_\ell\).

The one-normal support paragraph preceding DKEP Remark 2.8 identifies
\((A_N^{\sharp_N})^*=p_\ell A_N^{**}p_\ell\) and
\((A_N^{\sharp_{JNJ}})^*=p_rA_N^{**}p_r\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 identifies
the simultaneous normal bidual with their intersection inside \(A_N^{**}\).
For commuting projections \(e,f\) in a von Neumann algebra,
\(eRe\cap fRf=(ef)R(ef)\); therefore
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*=p_NA_N^{**}p_N,\qquad
        p_N=p_\ell p_r .                                          \tag{3}
\]

Apply the same one-normal support statement to \(Y\) for each of the two
normal structures.  The faithfulness hypothesis is exactly the hypothesis
needed there for the multiplier actions on \(Y\).  Thus \(r_Y\) commutes with
both \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\), and the two one-normal biduals of \(Y\) are
\(r_Yp_\ell A_N^{**}p_\ell r_Y\) and \(r_Yp_rA_N^{**}p_rr_Y\).  Applying
Proposition 2.10 to the inclusion \(Y\subset A_N\) and using the elementary
intersection formula once more gives
\[
        (Y^{\sharp_J})^*
        = r_Yp_NA_N^{**}p_Nr_Y
        = (p_Nr_Y)\,\cA_N\,(p_Nr_Y).
\]
Thus its support projection in \(\cA_N\) is \(p_Nr_Y\).  The weak* convergence
of approximate units to \(r_Y\) is the usual open-support property in the
ordinary bidual.

It remains to compare \(X\) with \(K_X(N)\).  For the subalgebra boundary
pieces used below the faithfulness assumption is automatic: if, for example,
\(a e_B=0\), then \(E_B(a^*a)=0\), hence \(a=0\), and the right action is
similar; the same holds for \(K_X(N)\) since \(X\subset K_X(N)\).  By the first
part, the normal supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) are \(p_Nr_X\) and
\(p_N\rho_X\).  We show that the simultaneous-normal annihilators of \(X\) and
\(K_X(N)\) coincide.

Let \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\) vanish on \(X\).  Write
\(K_X(N)=(K_X^L)^*K_X^L\), where \(K_X^L\) is the
\(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}\)-closure of \(A_NX\).  We first prove
\(\phi(S^*R)=0\) for \(S,R\in K_X^L\).  DKEP Corollary 3.2, in its one-sided
form, identifies \(K_X^L\) with the simultaneous strong right \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-module generated by \(A_NX\).  Apply the projection criterion
(DKEP Proposition 2.4) to the column \(\binom{S}{R}\).  There are orthogonal
families of projections \(\{f_j\}\subset N\) and \(\{\tilde f_k\}\subset JNJ\),
each summing strongly to \(1\), such that, with \(d_{k,j}=\tilde f_kf_j\),
\[
        Sd_{k,j},\ Rd_{k,j}\in A_NX\qquad\text{for every }j,k .
\]
(The use of the column gives one common cutting family for \(S\) and \(R\).)
Since \(N\) and \(JNJ\) commute, the \(d_{k,j}\)'s are pairwise orthogonal
projections summing strongly to \(1\).  For all pairs \((k,j)\) and \((l,i)\),
\[
        d_{k,j}S^*Rd_{l,i}=(Sd_{k,j})^*(Rd_{l,i})\in XA_NX\subset X .
\]
A second application of Proposition 2.4 puts \(S^*R\) in the simultaneous
strong \(N\)-\(N\) and \(JNJ\)-\(JNJ\) bimodule generated by \(X\).  Therefore
\(\phi(S^*R)=0\).  By linearity and norm closure \(\phi\) vanishes on
\(K_X(N)\).  The reverse annihilator inclusion follows from \(X\subset
K_X(N)\).  Equal preannihilators give the same weak* closed corner in
\(\cA_N\), hence the same support projection.  Since \(q_X\) is by definition
the support of \((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\), (2) follows.
\end{proof}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), put \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and
\[
        \cH_B=H\otimes_B\overline H
             ={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
Let \(\mathcal E_B=\{T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}:\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\}\).

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra fusion]\label{lem:subalg}
The boundary piece \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by the positive
operators \(s^*s\), \(s\in\mathcal E_B\), equivalently by
\(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) with \(\alpha\) an elementary bounded tensor in
\(\cH_B\).  Moreover \(\cH_B\cong\overline{\cH_B}\), and every coefficient
operator \(T_\alpha\) of a bounded vector in any Hilbert direct sum of copies
of \(\cH_B\) belongs to \(K_{X_B}(N)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Example 5.11 applied to \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) says that \(X_B\) is the
hereditary algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\).
By (1), \(T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta\).

Let \(Y\) be the hereditary algebra generated by \(\{s^*s:s\in\mathcal E_B\}\).
Since \(\mathcal E_B\) is self-adjoint, \(Y\) contains \(s^*s\) and \(ss^*\).
If \(r\) is the ordinary support of \(Y\), then
\((1-r)s^*s(1-r)=0\) and \((1-r)ss^*(1-r)=0\); hence \((1-r)s=s(1-r)=0\).
Thus \(s=rsr\in rA_N^{**}r\cap A_N=Y\).  So all generators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of \(X_B\) lie in \(Y\), and the reverse inclusion is
immediate.

The conjugation \(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta}\mapsto
\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi\) gives \(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\).  Let
\(\mathcal C\) be the elementary bounded tensors in \(\cH_B\).  They are
\(N\)-\(N\) cyclic, and \(T_\alpha\in X_B\subset X_BA_NX_B\) for
\(\alpha\in\mathcal C\).  Hence DKEP Theorem 5.10(1) applies, so \(\cH_B\) is
mixing relative to \(X_B\times X_B\).  For a Hilbert direct sum
\(\bigoplus_{j\in J}\cH_B\), take the vectors of \(\mathcal C\) placed in one
summand.  Their \(N\)-\(N\) span contains the finite-support algebraic direct
sum, hence is dense, and the coefficient operator of such a one-summand vector
is exactly the coefficient operator above (zero against the other summands).
Thus Theorem 5.10(1) holds for the whole direct sum.  Theorem 5.10(3), followed
by Proposition 5.9, gives \(T_\alpha\in K_{X_B,X_B}(N,N)=K_{X_B}(N)A_N
K_{X_B}(N)\subset K_{X_B}(N)\) for every bounded vector \(\alpha\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite fusion calculus]\label{lem:fusion}
Let \(X\) be a boundary piece for \(N\), with \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\).  Finite
Connes fusions, conjugates, and Hilbert direct sums of \(N\)-\(N\)
correspondences which are mixing relative to \(X\times X\) are again mixing
relative to \(X\times X\).  Consequently, if \(K\) is any such finite-fusion
correspondence and \(\xi\in K\) is bounded, then \(T_\xi\in K_X(N)\).

For \(U\subset V\), put \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).  Let \(\cT_U\) be the
linear span of all \(T_\xi\), where \(\xi\) is a bounded vector in a finite
direct sum of non-empty finite fusions of \(H_U\) and \(\overline{H_U}\).
Then \(\cT_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra, and \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive
approximate unit in the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_U\}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For a mixing correspondence \(H\), Theorem 5.10 says that
\[
  \mathcal C_H=\{\xi\in H_{\rm b}: T_\xi\in X A_N X\}
\]
is \(N\)-\(N\) cyclic.  Direct sums are handled by placing the cyclic vectors
in one summand; their span contains the finite-support algebraic sum and is
dense, while the coefficient condition is unchanged.  If \(H\) and \(K\) are
mixing and \(\xi\in\mathcal C_H,\eta\in\mathcal C_K\), then \(\xi\otimes_N\eta\)
is bounded, such elementary tensors are cyclic for \(H\otimes_NK\), and
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\in (X A_N X)(X A_N X)\subset X A_N X,
\]
because \(X\) is hereditary.  Thus the cyclic-vector criterion proves mixing
for fusions.  For the conjugate correspondence, the cyclic vectors are
\(\bar\xi\) and \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\in X A_NX\).  Iterating gives the first
assertion.  Applying Theorem 5.10(3) and Proposition 5.9 to any bounded vector
\(\xi\) in such a correspondence gives
\[
        T_\xi\in K_{X,X}(N,N)=K_X(N)A_NK_X(N)\subset K_X(N).
\]

The algebra property of \(\cT_U\) follows from (1) with the order convention
\(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\): the product of two coefficient operators
is the coefficient of the corresponding fused bounded vector, and the adjoint
is the coefficient of the conjugate vector; finite sums are absorbed by finite
direct sums.  By Lemma \ref{lem:subalg}, \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated
by \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) for elementary tensors in \(H_U\), and these \(T_\xi\)'s
belong to \(\cT_U\).  If \(s\) is a finite positive sum of such generators,
then \(g_\varepsilon(s)=s(s+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is in the hereditary algebra
generated by the summands.  Polynomials in \(s\) with zero constant term
approximate \(g_\varepsilon(s)^{1/2}\), so \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) lies in the
norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_U\}\).  The usual net over \(s\) and
\(\varepsilon\downarrow0\) is a contractive approximate unit.
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\); hence
\(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\) and \(K_{X_{M_Z}}(M)\subset K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).

\begin{lemma}[intersection of boundary supports]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .
\]
Consequently, if \(I=\cap_{i=1}^m U_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
   \in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule of
\cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN} give
\[
        H_A\otimes_M H_B\cong
        \bigoplus_{C\subset A\cap B}H_C^{\oplus m(A,B,C)} .        \tag{3}
\]
Since \(H_A\cong\overline{H_A}\), induction shows that a finite fusion whose
labels are \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) decomposes into a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with
\(C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n\).  The implementing unitary is
\(M\)-bimodular, so bounded vectors remain bounded.

Let \(\mathcal P_R\) be the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_R\}\).  We claim
that
\[
        a\,b\,c\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)             \tag{4}
\]
whenever \(a,c\in\mathcal P_W\) and \(b\in\mathcal P_U\).  It is enough to
consider \(a=t_1^*t_1\), \(b=s^*s\), \(c=t_2^*t_2\).  Write
\(t_j\) and \(s\) as finite sums of coefficient operators.  A summand in the
expansion of \(t_1^*t_1s^*st_2^*t_2\) is
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4},
\]
where each \(\alpha_i\) is a bounded vector in a finite direct sum of fusions
with label \(W\), and each \(\beta_i\) is similarly labelled by \(U\).  Replacing
\(T_\alpha^*\) by \(T_{\bar\alpha}\) and using
\(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\), the displayed product is the coefficient
operator of
\[
 \zeta:=\alpha_4\otimes\bar\alpha_3\otimes\beta_2\otimes\bar\beta_1
 \otimes\alpha_2\otimes\bar\alpha_1
\]
(with finite direct sums distributed over the tensor products).  This is a
bounded vector in a finite fusion whose label list contains both \(W\) and
\(U\).  By (3), it is carried by an \(M\)-bimodular unitary to a bounded vector
in a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset I\); its coefficient operator is
unchanged under this identification.  Lemma \ref{lem:subalg} gives coefficient
operators of such direct sums in \(K_{X_{M_C}}(M)\), and monotonicity
\(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\) gives \(T_\zeta\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).  Linearity and
norm closure prove (4).

Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2M)\), let \(p=p_M\) be the unit of \(\cA_M\) in
\(A^{**}\), let \(r_R\) be the ordinary support of \(X_{M_R}\), and let
\(\rho_R\) be the ordinary support of \(K_{X_{M_R}}(M)\).  Lemma
\ref{lem:support} gives
\[
        q_R=pr_R=p\rho_R,\qquad [p,r_R]=[p,\rho_R]=0 .              \tag{5}
\]
Choose contractive approximate units
\(h_\alpha\subset\mathcal P_W\) for \(X_{M_W}\) and
\(k_\beta\subset\mathcal P_U\) for \(X_{M_U}\).  In \(A^{**}\),
\(h_\alpha\to r_W\) and \(k_\beta\to r_U\) weak*.  By (4),
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\subset
\rho_IA^{**}\rho_I\).  The corner \(\rho_IA^{**}\rho_I\) is weak* closed, and
multiplication in \(A^{**}\) is separately weak* continuous; taking the three
successive limits gives
\[
        r_W r_U r_W\in \rho_I A^{**}\rho_I .
\]
This operator is a positive contraction, hence \(r_Wr_Ur_W\le \rho_I\).
Compressing only now to the simultaneous normal bidual, (5) yields
\[
 q_Wq_Uq_W
 =(pr_Wp)(pr_Up)(pr_Wp)
 =p\,r_Wr_Ur_W\,p
 \le p\rho_Ip=q_I .
\]

The palindromic assertion follows by induction.  If
\(R_{m-1}\le q_{\cap_{i<m}U_i}\), then
\[
q_{U_m}R_{m-1}q_{U_m}
 \le q_{U_m}q_{\cap_{i<m}U_i}q_{U_m}
 \le q_{\cap_iU_i},
\]
using the two-set case for the last inequality.
\end{proof}

\section*{From finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
\(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be boundary pieces, \(q_i=q_{X_i}\), and suppose
\[
        R:=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{6}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to each \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume not.  DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives an \(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)=\widetilde S_{\mathbb K(L^2N)}(N)\) with
\(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\) and put \(q=q_i\).  Since \(q\) commutes with
\(N\) and \(JNJ\),
\[
 [q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0,\qquad a\in JNJ,
\]
for \(y\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\); hence \(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde
S(N)\).  If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), then
\(y\mapsto \omega(q^\perp yq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  On \(N_+\) it is dominated by
\(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\), hence is normal; its normalization and centrality
make its restriction the unique normal trace on the factor \(N\), contradicting
relative proper proximality.  Thus \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for all \(i\).

Each \(q_i\), \(q_0\), and the palindromic product \(R\) commute with \(JNJ\),
so they lie in \(\widetilde S(N)\); their values below are therefore independent
of any extension.  Extend \(\omega\) to a state on the \(C^*\)-algebra
\(\cA_N\) only for the following GNS projection argument; the extension is not
claimed to be \(N\)-central.  In its GNS representation, the equality
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) implies \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector.  Hence
\(\omega(R)=\|(q_1q_2\cdots q_m)\xi_\omega\|^2=1\).  Since \(R\) is a positive
contraction in \(q_0\cA_Nq_0\), \(R\le q_0\), and so \(\omega(q_0)=1\).

DKEP Lemma 8.5 provides the canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\cA_Nq_0,\qquad
        \Theta_0(a)=aq_0\quad(a\in N).
\]
Then \(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is an \(N\)-central state on
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  Since \(q_0\) fixes the GNS cyclic vector,
\(\psi(a)=\omega(aq_0)=\omega(a)=\tau(a)\) for \(a\in N\).  This Connes
hypertrace contradicts non-amenability of \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and \(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).
Assume that \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(a\), and that \(P\) has unitaries
\(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  The AFP normal form is
\[
 L^2N=L^2B\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge1,\ i_j\in\{P,Q\}\\
 i_j\ne i_{j+1}}}
 H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n}.
\]
Let \(p\) and \(q\) project onto the summands whose first letter is \(P\),
respectively \(Q\); then \(1=e_B+p+q\).  Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves
the first letter.  Let \(x\in P\ominus B\).  On \(L^2B\), \(JxJ\) creates a
first-letter \(P\) vector.  On a reduced word with first letter \(Q\), right
multiplication by \(x^*\) either appends or merges at the last letter, but the
first letter remains \(Q\) and no \(L^2B\)-part is created.  On a first-letter
\(P\) word the first letter remains \(P\), except that a length-one word may
produce an \(L^2B\)-component.  Hence
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.             \tag{7}
\]
The symmetric formula holds with \(P,p\) and \(Q,q\) interchanged for
\(x\in Q\ominus B\).  Since \(E_B(x)=0\), \(e_BJxJe_B=0\); hence the two terms
in (7) are just right translates of \(e_B\) (up to sign), so they lie in
\(X_B\subset K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(N)\).

We now translate DKEP Lemma 6.1.  For a fixed operator \(T\), the set of
\(x\in N\) such that both \([T,JxJ]\) and \([T,Jx^*J]\) lie in
\(K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(N)\) is a von Neumann algebra; this is Lemma 6.1 applied
to \(B(L^2N)\) with DKEP's right-action convention
\(x\cdot T\cdot y=Jy^*JTJx^*J\).  For \(T=p\) and \(T=q\) this von Neumann
algebra contains \(B\), \(P\ominus B\), and \(Q\ominus B\), hence contains the
von Neumann algebra generated by \(P\) and \(Q\).  Thus \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

It remains, by DKEP Theorem 6.2, to rule out an \(N\)-central state
\(\varphi\) on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) with normal restriction to \(N\).  For
\(\eta\in qL^2N\), \(w_i\eta\) is a reduced vector with first letter \(P\).
Moreover \(q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0\): if a reduced vector begins with \(Q\), left
multiplication by the centered \(P\)-letter \(w_1^*w_2\) produces a vector
beginning with \(P\).  Thus
\[
        w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p .
\]
Likewise \(a\in Q\ominus B\) and left multiplication by \(a\) sends every
first-letter \(P\) reduced word into a first-letter \(Q\) word, so
\[
        a p a^*\le q .
\]
Since \(S_{X_B}(N)\) is an \(N\)-bimodule and \(\varphi\) is \(N\)-central,
these inequalities give \(2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)\), hence
\(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every non-zero power is an alternating
reduced word in centered \(P\)- and \(Q\)-letters, so \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for
\(n\ne0\).  For \(m\ne n\),
\[
h^ne_Bh^{-n}h^me_Bh^{-m}
 =h^n e_B h^{m-n}e_Bh^{-m}=h^nE_B(h^{m-n})e_Bh^{-m}=0 .
\]
The projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and, by centrality,
have the same \(\varphi\)-value; hence \(\varphi(e_B)=0\).  This contradicts
\(1=\varphi(e_B+p+q)\).  Thus \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\).  The graph-product reduced-word
model, or \cite{CF17}, gives the standard amalgamated free product
decomposition
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{8}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose trace-zero
unitaries \(u_r,u_t\) and set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  Then \(w_i\notin M_{L_v}\),
and the reduced-word expectation onto \(M_{L_v}\) kills \(w_i\) and
\(w_1^*w_2\).  If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and
\(|V|\ge3\) give \(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); otherwise
\(\{v,r\}\) would be a component of \(\Gamma^c\).  Set
\(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  Here \(E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1)=0\),
\(E_{M_{L_v}}(w_2)=u_sE_{M_{L_v}}(u_r)=0\), and
\(w_1^*w_2=u_r^*u_su_r\) is a reduced word containing the outside vertex \(r\),
so its expectation onto \(M_{L_v}\) is \(0\).  Thus in all cases
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
A trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in (8), so
Proposition \ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
\(\cap_i L_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} puts the associated
palindromic product in \(q_\emptyset\cA_Mq_\emptyset\).  Here
\(M_\emptyset=\C\), \(e_\C\) is the rank-one projection onto
\(\C\widehat1\), and DKEP Example 3.4 gives
\(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 implies that a join-irreducible graph product with
\(|V|\ge3\) and trace-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor.  A
diffuse amenable finite factor is the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor and is not
full, so \(M\) is non-amenable.  It is separable because the graph is finite
and the vertices are separable.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}, applied to the finite
family \(\{X_{M_{L_v}}:v\in V\}\), proves that \(M\) is properly proximal.
```

# Author's working notes (background context only)

This is the Author's persistent scratchpad — not the deliverable, and not the focus of your review. Skim it; flag any fatal mathematical error you happen to spot that could be steering the proof in a wrong direction. Otherwise concentrate the review on `answer.tex`.

## `research_notes.tex`

```latex
\documentclass{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\K}{\mathbb K}
\begin{document}

\section*{Problem interpretation}

The graph is finite, simple, and has at least three vertices.  The intended
``irreducible'' hypothesis is join-irreducibility: there is no nonempty proper
partition $V=S\sqcup T$ such that every vertex of $S$ is adjacent to every
vertex of $T$.  Equivalently the complement graph $\Gamma^c$ is connected.
All vertex traces are faithful normal traces.  A trace-zero unitary in each
$M_v$ implies each vertex algebra is nontrivial and provides centered unitaries
for reduced-word and amalgamated-free-product arguments.

For $U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical expectation.  If $B\subset M$, $X_B$ is DKEP's
boundary piece generated by the Jones projection $e_B$.

\section*{DKEP normal-bidual notation}

Let
\[
  \mathcal A_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
be DKEP's normal bidual.  For a boundary piece $X$, let $q_X$ be the support of
the normal corner $(\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*$ inside $\mathcal A_N$.  The
projection $q_X$ commutes with $N$ and with $JNJ$, and
\[
\widetilde S_X(N)=
\{T\in\mathcal A_N:[T,a]\in q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X
\text{ for every }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and $\widetilde S(N)$.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 applies to the trivial group action when $N$ is a separable
finite factor: $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X$ iff there is no
$N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction to $N$ is the trace.

Important caution: a state on a von Neumann algebra which is $1$ on two
noncommuting projections need not be $1$ on their lattice meet, because the GNS
representation of a non-normal state need not preserve meets.  Thus the naive
criterion ``relative to finitely many $X_i$ plus
$\bigwedge_iq_{X_i}=q_0$'' is not justified unless commutation/normality is
available.  The replacement is to use an actual positive palindromic product of
the $q_i$'s which lies in the compact corner.

\section*{Palindromic finite-boundary criterion}

Let $N$ be a separable nonamenable finite factor and $X_1,\dots,X_m$ boundary
pieces.  Put $q_i=q_{X_i}$.  Suppose
\[
  R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
Then relative proper proximality with respect to every $X_i$ implies ordinary
proper proximality.

Proof details:
if $\omega$ is an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S(N)$ with $\omega|_N=\tau$,
then for each $i$,
\[
  q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q_i^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
because $q_i$ commutes with $JNJ$ and $[T,JNJ]\subset q_i\mathcal A_Nq_i$.
If $\omega(q_i^\perp)>0$, compression by $q_i^\perp$ gives an $N$-central state
on $\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$ normal on $N$ (normality is by domination by a scalar
multiple of $\tau$ on $N_+$), contradiction.  Hence the GNS cyclic vector for
$\omega$ is fixed by each $q_i$ and therefore by $R$.  Since $R$ is a positive
contraction in $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$, $1=\omega(R)\le \omega(q_0)$, so
$\omega(q_0)=1$.  Restricting $\omega$ to $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong
\mathbb B(L^2N)$ gives a Connes hypertrace, contradicting nonamenability.

\section*{Graph-product boundary corner intersections}

For graph products write $q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}$.  The needed replacement for a meet
formula is
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
This implies by induction that the palindromic product associated with
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ lies in the corner for $\cap_iU_i$.

Reasoning.  Let $\mathcal H_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)$ with cyclic vector
$\zeta_U=1\otimes 1$.  The coefficient of $\zeta_U$ is $e_{M_U}$ and translates
of this vector generate DKEP's $X_{M_U}$.  DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ shows that products of a $W$-coefficient, a
$U$-coefficient, and a $W$-coefficient are coefficients of
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M \mathcal H_U\otimes_M \mathcal H_W
\]
(up to hereditary closure; the cyclic coefficient is
$e_{M_W}e_{M_U}e_{M_W}$).  Thus the normal corner on the left is contained in
the coefficient support of this triple fusion.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5 decompose this triple fusion as a direct sum of
$L^2(M)\otimes_{M_Z}L^2(M)$ with $Z\subset U\cap W$.  Since $X_{M_Z}\subset
X_{M_{U\cap W}}$, the whole coefficient boundary is contained in
$X_{M_{U\cap W}}$.

This is intentionally weaker than the unsupported identity
$q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}$ and is tailored to non-normal states.

\section*{Relative Bass--Serre paradox}

For $N=P*_BQ$, $Q=B\bar\otimes A$, with $A$ containing a trace-zero unitary $a$
and with unitaries $w_1,w_2\in P$ satisfying
\[
  E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0,
\]
one proves $N$ properly proximal relative to $X_B$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be the projections onto the free-product Fock subspaces whose
first letter is in $L^2(P)\ominus L^2B$ and $L^2(Q)\ominus L^2B$, respectively.
Then $1=e_B+p+q$.

Commutator formulas with right multiplication $R_x=Jx^*J$:
for $x\in P\ominus B$,
\[
  [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0,
\]
and for $x\in Q\ominus B$,
\[
  [q,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [p,R_x]=0.
\]
Elements of $B$ commute with $p,q$.  These formulas are checked directly on
reduced words; only the $B$-summand and one-letter words from the same free
component can change first-letter type.  Since right translates of $e_B$ are in
the boundary ideal, DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.

If $\varphi$ is an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$, then
\[
  w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q.
\]
The first inequality uses centeredness of $w_i$ and orthogonality from
$E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$; the second uses $a\in Q\ominus B$.  Centrality gives
$2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, so $\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.
The unitary $h=w_1a$ is $B$-Haar: the subspaces $h^nL^2B$ are pairwise
orthogonal.  Hence centrality forces $\varphi(e_B)=0$, contradicting
$1=e_B+p+q$.

\section*{Graph-product application}

For each vertex $v$,
\[
  M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\Lk(v)}}(M_{\Lk(v)}\bar\otimes M_v).
\]
Let $C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim v\}$.  Complement connectedness gives
$C(v)\ne\emptyset$.  If $C(v)$ has two vertices $r,t$, use trace-zero unitaries
$u_r,u_t$ and set $w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t$.  If $C(v)=\{r\}$, complement
connectedness and $|V|\ge3$ give $s\in\Lk(v)$ with $r\not\sim s$; set
$w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r$.  In both cases expectations of $w_i$ and $w_1^*w_2$ onto
$M_{\Lk(v)}$ vanish.  The vertex unitary in $M_v$ gives $a$.  Therefore $M$ is
properly proximal relative to every $X_{M_{\Lk(v)}}$.

The intersection of all links is empty.  The palindromic product of the link
boundary supports is therefore in the compact corner because
$M_\emptyset=\mathbb C$ and $X_{M_\emptyset}$ is the compact boundary piece.

CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives factoriality.  In a connected complement graph
with at least three vertices, there is no universal vertex and no isolated
exceptional nonedge component.  CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 gives nonamenability:
if $M$ were amenable, applying the relative amenability criterion with
$V_1=V,V_2=\emptyset$ would force every complement edge to be an isolated
exceptional edge, impossible in a connected complement graph with at least
three vertices.



\section*{Normal support calculus for correspondence boundaries}

For a correspondence $\mathcal H$, write $X_{\mathcal H}$ for DKEP's
coefficient boundary generated by $T_\xi^*T_\eta$ for bounded vectors.
Let $q_{\mathcal H}$ be the support in the normal bidual of
$\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(M)$.  A useful way to view this support is via the
normal left ideal $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ generated by the coefficient
operators $T_\xi$: the right support of $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ is
$q_{\mathcal H}$, and $q_{\mathcal H}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal H}$ is the weak-*
closed hereditary corner generated by the coefficients.

For correspondences $\mathcal H,\mathcal K$ over $M$, DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ gives the normal support-sandwich inclusion
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
 \subset
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}\mathcal A
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}.
\]
Proof template: first verify on generators
\[
T_\xi^*(T_\eta^*T_{\eta'})T_{\xi'}
 =T_{\xi\otimes\eta}^*T_{\xi'\otimes\eta'}.
\]
Then use separate weak-* continuity and approximate the right support
$q_{\mathcal H}$ by an approximate unit for
$\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}^*\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$.  This avoids assuming
commutation of supports or identifying their lattice meet.

Direct sums: the coefficient boundary of $\bigoplus_i\mathcal H_i$ is supported
by the join of the supports of the $X_{\mathcal H_i}$.  Diagonal coefficients
are immediate; off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the usual
$2\times2$ positivity/Douglas factorization argument.  Hence if every summand's
boundary is contained in $X_Y$, then the whole direct-sum boundary is contained
in $X_Y$.

For graph products, with $\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M$, CDHJKN fusion
gives
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_U
  \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset U\cap W}
       (L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M)^{\oplus k_Z}.
\]
Since $Z\subset I$ implies $e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_I}$ and $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_I}$,
the support of the fusion is dominated by $q_I$.  Combining with the
support-sandwich inclusion yields
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A q_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A q_{U\cap W}.
\]

\section*{Compact-corner hypertrace step}

In the finite-boundary criterion, do not identify
$q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$ with $\mathbb B(L^2N)$.  DKEP's proof of Lemma 8.5 gives
the needed replacement: a natural $N$- and $JNJ$-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
  \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0
\]
sending $1$ to $q_0$ and $x\in N$ to $q_0xq_0$.  If a central state
$\omega$ on $\widetilde S(N)$ satisfies $\omega(q_0)=1$, then
$\omega\circ\Theta$ is an $N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending the
trace.  This is a Connes hypertrace, forcing amenability of the finite factor.



\section*{Round 6 support-calculus refinement}

The critic objected that the previous support-sandwich argument multiplied
concrete operators after embedding them in the DKEP normal bidual, where the
canonical embedding is only u.c.p. and not multiplicative.  The safer route is:

Let \(A=B(L^2N)\) and \(p=p_{\rm nor}\in A^{**}\) be the DKEP projection for
simultaneous normality over \(N\) and \(JNJ\).  For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\),
write \(e_X\in A^{**}\) for the ordinary support projection of
\(\mathbb K_X(N)\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 implies \(e_Xp=pe_X\), and the
support projection in \(\mathcal A_N=pA^{**}p\) is \(q_X=pe_X\).  Thus one can
do support computations in the ordinary enveloping von Neumann algebra
\(A^{**}\), where the canonical embedding is multiplicative, and only then cut
by \(p\).

Concrete lemma: if \(B,C,D\subset A\) are hereditary \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule
\(C^*\)-subalgebras and \(CBC\subset D\), then
\[
e_C(e_BA^{**}e_B)e_C\subset e_DA^{**}e_D.
\]
Proof: approximate an element of \(e_BA^{**}e_B\) weak-* by a bounded net from
\(B\), sandwich by an approximate unit of \(C\), and pass to weak-* limits.
Cutting by \(p\), using \(e_\bullet p=pe_\bullet\), gives
\[
q_C(q_B\mathcal A_Nq_B)q_C\subset q_D\mathcal A_Nq_D.
\]
This addresses the nonmultiplicativity warning: no claim is made that
\(\iota_{\rm nor}(xy)=\iota_{\rm nor}(x)\iota_{\rm nor}(y)\).

For coefficient boundary pieces, the relevant product is not the previously
claimed double fusion but a triple/coefficient product.  If \(\mathcal H\) and
\(\mathcal K\) are \(N\)-\(N\) correspondences, then for bounded vectors
\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})(T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
 =
T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^*
T_{\xi_4\otimes\bar\xi_3\otimes\eta_2},
\]
using \(T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha\) and
\(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\).  Hence the algebraic coefficient product
\(X_{\mathcal H}X_{\mathcal K}X_{\mathcal H}\) is contained in the coefficient
boundary of
\(\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K\).
DKEP Proposition 5.9/Theorem 5.10 and separate continuity of the relative
compact topologies are the intended justification for passing from algebraic
coefficient spans to the \(\mathbb K_X\)'s.

For graph products,
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) is self-contragredient.  Therefore
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset
q_{\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_U}\mathcal A_M
q_{\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_U}.
\]
Applying the CDHJKN fusion rule twice decomposes this triple fusion into
summands \(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\) with \(Z\subset U\cap W\), so
the support is dominated by \(q_{U\cap W}\).  This gives the desired
palindromic-product inclusion without using non-normal lattice meets.


\section*{Refined normal-left-ideal support mechanism}

For an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(\mathcal H\), DKEP's coefficient boundary
\(X_{\mathcal H}\) is generated hereditarily by the operators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\) bounded.  The most economical support object is
not the concrete \(C^*\)-algebra product
\(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}
\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), but the weak-* closed normal left ideal
\(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) generated by the coefficient maps \(T_\xi\).  Its
right support in \(\mathcal A_N=(B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) is
\(q_{\mathcal H}\).  This follows because \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the hereditary
algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and DKEP Proposition 5.9 identifies the
corresponding relative compact operators with the operators whose positive
squares lie in the relevant \(\mathbb K_X\)-corners.

Composition of rectangular normal bimodules is separately weak-* continuous in
the DKEP normal-bidual setting and agrees on bounded vectors with the concrete
Connes-fusion formula
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
  \mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}
  \subset \mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
Since \(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H}\in
\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\), for any
\(x=q_{\mathcal K}a q_{\mathcal K}\) one obtains
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}xq_{\mathcal H}
 =(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})^*a(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})
 \in q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}\mathcal A_N
    q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the support-level sandwich needed for graph products and avoids the
unproved closed-\(\mathbb K_X\) product inclusion.

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), the induced correspondence
\(\mathcal H_B=L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\) has coefficient boundary \(X_B\).  The
cyclic vector \(\zeta=\hat1\otimes_B\hat1\) has coefficient operator \(e_B\),
and \(N\)-\(N\) translates of \(\zeta\) produce exactly the left/right
translates of \(e_B\) appearing in DKEP's definition of \(X_B\).

Direct sums of correspondences: if each summand \(\mathcal H_j\) has support
below a projection \(q\), then \(\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\) also has support below
\(q\).  Off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the standard hereditary
corner fact \(a^*a,b^*b\in D\Rightarrow a^*b\in D\), proved from \(2\times2\)
positivity/Douglas factorization.  Infinite direct sums follow by truncating
bounded vectors.

\section*{Operator-system point in the palindromic criterion}

DKEP's \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) is an operator system, not generally a
\(C^*\)-algebra.  When a state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) satisfies
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for several support projections \(q_i\), extend
\(\omega\) to a state \(\bar\omega\) on the ambient von Neumann algebra
\(\mathcal A_N\).  In the GNS representation of \(\bar\omega\), each
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector; hence the palindromic product
\((q_1\cdots q_m)^*(q_1\cdots q_m)\) has \(\omega\)-value one.  This justifies
the GNS step without pretending that \(\widetilde S(N)\) itself is a
\(C^*\)-algebra.

Compression normality: if \(q=q_X\) and \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), the compressed
functional \(T\mapsto \omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is normal on
\(N\) because \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and, on \(N_+\), it is dominated by
\(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\).



\section*{Ordinary-envelope support calculus after Round 8}

The normal-bidual map \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\to p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\)
should not be used multiplicatively.  The safe pattern is:
work in the ordinary enveloping algebra \(A^{**}\), prove a support inclusion
there, and only then multiply by \(p_{\rm nor}\), using DKEP Proposition 2.10
(commutation of \(p_{\rm nor}\) with supports of hereditary \(N,JNJ\)-bimodular
subalgebras).

For a hereditary subalgebra \(C\subset A\), write \(e_C\in A^{**}\) for its open
support.  If a concrete hereditary-product inclusion \(B C B\subset D\) is
available, then
\[
  e_B(e_C A^{**}e_C)e_B\subset e_D A^{**}e_D.
\]
Proof: approximate elements of \(e_C A^{**}e_C\) by bounded nets from \(C\),
sandwich by elements and then an approximate unit of \(B\), and pass to weak-*
limits.  Cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\) gives
\(q_B(q_C\mathcal A q_C)q_B\subset q_D\mathcal A q_D\).

For coefficient boundaries \(X_{\mathcal H}\), the desired support inclusion is
best viewed as a rectangular-support statement rather than as a product of
weak-* closed left ideals in the normal bidual.  In ordinary \(A^{**}\), the
support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the right support of the rectangular module
generated by the concrete maps \(T_\xi\).  The concrete identities
\[
  T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*,\qquad T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha
\]
give
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 (T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^*
 T_{\xi_4\otimes\bar\xi_3\otimes\eta_2}.
\]
DKEP Proposition 5.9 (polar decomposition for rectangular relative compact
operators) and Theorem 5.10 (mixing/relative compactness equivalences) are the
references for passing from coefficient generators to hereditary closures and
supports.  The resulting support estimate is
\[
  q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A_N q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
  \subset
  q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}
  \mathcal A_N
  q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the replacement for the invalid binary-fusion/normal-left-ideal
argument.

In the graph-product case
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) is self-contragredient and
\(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\).  CDHJKN Proposition 5.5 gives
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_W
  \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset W}\mathcal H_Z^{\oplus k_Z},
\]
and fusing each summand with \(\mathcal H_U\) gives only summands
\(\mathcal H_Y\) with \(Y\subset Z\cap U\subset W\cap U\).  Direct sums are
handled through DKEP Theorem 5.10: finite-support bounded vectors are cyclic for
the direct sum, so the support of a direct-sum correspondence is below the join
of the supports of its summands.  Hence
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
  \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]

A point for further audit is the exact formulation of the DKEP rectangular
support statement: one should avoid claiming that \(\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}
\K_{X_{\mathcal K}}\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\subset
\K_{X_{\mathcal H\otimes\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes\mathcal K}}\) for arbitrary
correspondences, since it is too strong when \(\mathcal H\) is the identity
correspondence.  The correct assertion is the support-corner inclusion above.



\section*{Refined graph-product support route}

A safer way to prove the graph-product boundary intersection is to avoid an
abstract support-sandwich theorem for all correspondences and work with the
concrete coefficient algebras for the graph-product basic correspondences.

First, for a DKEP boundary piece \(X\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), let
\(e_X\in A^{**}\) be the ordinary open support.  The normal support used in
DKEP for \((\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) agrees, after cutting by
\(p_{\rm nor}\), with the ordinary support of \(X\):
\[
        q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X .
\]
Reason: \(X\subset \mathbb K_X(N)\) gives \(p_{\rm nor}e_X\le q_X\).  Conversely,
if a functional in \(A^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X\), DKEP's strong-closure
description of \(\mathbb K_X\) (the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)/strong
\(N,JNJ\)-bimodule closure in Section 3) forces it to vanish on
\(\mathbb K_X\).  Thus \(X\) and \(\mathbb K_X\) have the same annihilator among
normal-bimodular functionals, hence the same normal support.  This is the bridge
between the concrete coefficient boundary and the \(q_X\) appearing in
\(\widetilde S_X\).

For \(U\subset V\), set
\[
        \mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M .
\]
The vector \(\hat1\otimes_{M_U}\hat1\) has \(T_\zeta=P_U\), the Jones projection.
Its \(M\)-\(M\) translates generate \(X_{M_U}\) (DKEP Example 3.4/5.11).  Let
\(B_U\) be the norm-closed span of coefficient operators coming from non-empty finite
Connes fusions of the correspondences \(\mathcal H_Z\) and
\(\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\) with \(Z\subset U\).  The DKEP identities for
\(T\)-operators make this a \(C^*\)-algebra, and CDHJKN fusion decomposes all
such non-empty finite fusions into \(\mathcal H_L\)'s with \(L\subset U\).  Since
\(L\subset U\) implies \(X_{M_L}\subset X_{M_U}\) (because \(P_L\in P_U
\mathbb B(L^2M)P_U\)), this algebra has the same support as \(X_{M_U}\).  Let \(s_U\)
be this support in \(A^{**}\).  Then
\[
        q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}=p_{\rm nor}s_U .
\]

The elementary \(C^*\)-support lemma needed is:

If \(B,C\subset A\) are \(C^*\)-subalgebras with supports \(r,s\), and if a
projection \(p\in A^{**}\) satisfies
\[
        BC\subset pA^{**}s,\qquad CB\subset sA^{**}p,
\]
then
\[
        r(sA^{**}s)r\subset pA^{**}p.
\]
Proof: let approximate units of \(B\) and \(C\) converge weak-* to \(r\) and
\(s\).  For \(x\in sA^{**}s\), the elements \(b_\alpha c_\beta x
c_{\beta'}b_{\alpha'}\) lie in \(pA^{**}p\), and successive weak-* limits give
the claim.

For graph products, the needed product-support hypotheses are
\[
        B_WB_U\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_U,\qquad
        B_UB_W\subset s_UA^{**}s_{U\cap W}.
\]
On coefficient generators, the first inclusion follows from
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]
The \(\xi\)-vectors are from finite fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z\)'s with
\(Z\subset W\), and the \(\eta\)-vectors from finite fusions with \(Y\subset U\).
Since \(\mathcal H_Z\cong\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\), CDHJKN Proposition 5.5
decomposes the correspondence containing
\(\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1\) into copies of \(\mathcal H_L\) with
\(L\subset U\cap W\), while \(T_{\eta_2}\) has right support \(s_U\).  The
second inclusion is the adjoint.  Norm closure extends this from generators to
\(B_WB_U\) and \(B_UB_W\).

Combining these product-support inclusions with the ordinary support lemma gives
\[
        s_W(s_UA^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_{U\cap W}.
\]
Finally \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the supports by DKEP Proposition 2.10, so
cutting gives
\[
        q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
        \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
This route avoids the false/too-strong assertion
\(\mathbb K_{X_H}\mathbb K_{X_K}\mathbb K_{X_H}\subset \mathbb K_{X_L}\) and
also avoids needing a general correspondence support theorem beyond the
concrete graph-product coefficient/fusion calculation.



\section*{Round 10 consolidated proof details}

For a boundary piece \(X\subset B(L^2N)\), the normal support used in DKEP
Lemma 8.5 should be treated as the support of DKEP's hereditary algebra
\(K_X(N)\), not naively as the support of the originally displayed generators.
DKEP Prop. 2.10 says: if \(B\subset A=B(L^2N)\) is a \(C^*\)-algebra which is
simultaneously an \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule with faithful multiplier actions,
and \(s_B\in A^{**}\) is its ordinary support, then
\[
  (B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_BA^{**}s_Bp_{\rm nor}
\]
inside \((A^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and \(s_B\) commutes
with \(p_{\rm nor}\).  For \(B=K_X(N)\) this gives \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}s_B\).

For the graph-product subalgebra \(M_U\), \(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\) can be described
as the norm-closed coefficient algebra generated by nonempty finite Connes
fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\) and conjugates with
\(Z\subset U\).  The coefficient formulas
\[
  (T_\xi^*T_\eta)(T_\alpha^*T_\beta)
    =T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{\,*}T_\beta,\qquad
  (T_\xi^*T_\eta)^*=T_\eta^*T_\xi
\]
make this a \(C^*\)-algebra.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 imply that
finite fusions over subsets of \(U\) decompose into direct sums of
\(\mathcal H_L\)'s with \(L\subset U\), and fusing an \(U\)-piece with a
\(W\)-piece only produces \(L\subset U\cap W\).

Rectangular support principle used in the intersection lemma: if \(D\subset A\)
has open support \(d\), \(T:L^2N\to H\) is bounded, and \(T^*T\in D\), then for
an approximate unit \(u_i\) of \(D\), \(Tu_i\to T\) strongly; hence \(T=Td\) in
the bidual sense.  Therefore if \(S^*S\in C\) and \(C\) has support \(c\), then
\(T^*S\in dA^{**}c\).  This closes the previous rectangular gap in the product
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]

Relative Bass--Serre paradox for \(N=P*_BQ\), \(Q=B\bar\otimes A\): use the
AFP decomposition
\[
L^2N=L^2B\oplus\bigoplus H_{i_1}^\circ\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n}^\circ,
\qquad i_j\in\{P,Q\},\ i_j\ne i_{j+1}.
\]
Let \(p,q\) be the projections onto words with first letter \(P,Q\).  For
\(R_x=Jx^*J\),
\[
 [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\ [q,R_x]=0\quad (x\in P\ominus B),
\]
and with \(p,q\) interchanged for \(x\in Q\ominus B\).  These commutators are in
\(X_B\), so DKEP Lemma 6.1 yields \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).  If \(w_1,w_2\in P\)
satisfy \(E_B(w_i)=E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\) and \(a\in A\subset Q\) is a trace-zero
unitary, then \(w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p\), \(apa^*\le q\), and
\(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for \(n\ne0\).  This forces any
\(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) normal on \(N\) to vanish on
\(p,q,e_B\), contradiction.

Graph combinatorics preserved: connected \(\Gamma^c\), \(|V|\ge3\), implies
each vertex has a complement-neighbor.  If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), then \(r\) has a
complement-neighbor \(s\ne v\); necessarily \(s\in\Lk(v)\).  Thus the
Bass--Serre unitaries can be chosen as \(u_r,u_t\) or \(u_r,u_su_r\).
CDHJKN factoriality and amenability criteria exclude factor failure and
amenability because a universal vertex or an exceptional nonedge pair would be
an isolated vertex/edge of \(\Gamma^c\).



\section*{Round 11 refinement: normal support and coefficient model}

To avoid the earlier unsupported identity
\[
  K_{X_{M_U}}(M)=\text{``finite fusion coefficient algebra''},
\]
it is better to compute the normal-bidual support \(q_{X_{M_U}}\) from the
original DKEP boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\).  The relevant facts are:

\begin{itemize}
\item If \(B\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra
which is an \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule with faithful multiplier actions, and
\(s_B\in A^{**}\) is its ordinary open support, then the unit of
\((B^{\sharp_J})^*\subset (A^{\sharp_J})^*\) is \(p_{\rm nor}s_B\).  This is
not quoted as a literal statement of DKEP Proposition 2.10; rather it follows
from the paragraph before DKEP Remark 2.8 (one normal category) plus Proposition
2.10 for the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal category.  Equivalently, an
approximate unit of \(B\) converges to \(p_{\rm nor}s_B\) in the normal bidual.
```
