\(\Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to(\mathbb K(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*
=q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\), induced by the normal-bidual quotient, with
\(\Theta_0(1)=q_0\) and \(\Theta_0(x)=xq_0\) for \(x\in N\).  If an extended
state on \(\mathcal A_N\) satisfies \(\omega(q_0)=1\), then
\(\omega\circ\Theta_0\) is a Connes hypertrace extending \(\tau\).



\section*{Ordinary bidual compression for boundary-support intersections}

The safest way to handle products of normal-bidual support projections is to
keep track of the ordinary enveloping algebra.  Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), and
realize
\[
  \mathcal A_N=(A^{\sharp_J})^*=pA^{**}p
\]
using DKEP Proposition 2.10, where \(p\) is the simultaneous support of
functionals that are normal for both \(N\) and \(JNJ\).  For a hereditary
\(N,JNJ\)-invariant \(C^*\)-subalgebra \(Y\subset A\), write \(r_Y\in A^{**}\)
for the ordinary open support projection.  DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives
\(r_Yp=pr_Y\) and
\[
  (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y).
\]
Thus the image of \(Y\) in \(\mathcal A_N\) is \(y\mapsto pyp\), but one should
not multiply such images as though \(y\mapsto pyp\) were a homomorphism.  The
useful replacement is: first prove support inclusions in \(A^{**}\), where
multiplication is the original bidual multiplication, and only then compress by
\(p\).  Since the relevant ordinary support projections commute with \(p\), the
compressed products of support projections are legitimate:
\[
  (pr_W)(pr_U)(pr_W)=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p .
\]

For graph-product subalgebra boundaries \(X_{M_U}\), let \(r_U\) be the ordinary
support of \(X_{M_U}\) and \(r_{K_U}\) the support of \(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).  The
annihilator equality between \(X_{M_U}\) and \(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\) in
\(A^{\sharp_J}\) implies \(pr_U=pr_{K_U}=q_U\), although \(r_U\) and \(r_{K_U}\)
need not be equal in \(A^{**}\).  If \(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and
\(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\) are positive contractive approximate units made from
finite-fusion coefficients, then \(h_\alpha\to r_W\) and \(k_\beta\to r_U\)
strongly* in \(A^{**}\).  The fusion rule gives
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}(M)\).  Passing to the
strong* limit in \(A^{**}\) gives
\[
  r_Wr_Ur_W\in r_{K_{U\cap W}}A^{**}r_{K_{U\cap W}},
  \qquad 0\le r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_{U\cap W}}.
\]
Compression by \(p\) then yields the normal-bidual inequality
\[
  q_Wq_Uq_W=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p\le pr_{K_{U\cap W}}p=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This avoids any use of multiplicativity of the canonical complete-order
embedding \(A\to\mathcal A_N\), which is generally false.



\section*{Coefficient products in finite fusions}

For finite-fusion arguments it is better not to rely on a fixed elementary-tensor
formula for products such as \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\,T_\alpha^*T_\beta\), because
the exact placement of conjugate correspondences depends on conventions for
left/right bounded vectors.  The invariant statement needed in the graph-product
proof is the standard one: the linear span of bounded coefficients of finite
direct sums of a class of correspondences is closed under products after the
class is enlarged to include finite Connes fusions and conjugates.  Thus a
product \(hkh\), with \(h\) a coefficient coming from finite fusions labelled by
\(W\) and \(k\) a coefficient coming from finite fusions labelled by \(U\), is a
finite sum of coefficients of finite fusions whose labels are all \(W\)'s and
\(U\)'s.  CDHJKN's fusion rule then decomposes each such term into
correspondences \(H_C\) with \(C\subset W\cap U\), which is the only label
information required.


\section*{Round 22 consolidation of technical bridge}

The support computations should be formulated using the concrete support
description in DKEP Section 2.3, not merely as an abstract consequence of
Proposition 2.10.  For a normal $M$-$C^*$-algebra $A$, DKEP introduce
$p_{\rm nor}\in M(A)^{**}$ and identify $(A^\sharp)^*$ with
$p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}$.  If $B\subset A$ is an invariant
$C^*$-subalgebra with faithful multiplier action and $r_B$ is the ordinary
support of $B^{**}$ in $A^{**}$, then $r_B$ commutes with $p_{\rm nor}$ and
\[
 (B^\sharp)^* \cong r_B p_{\rm nor} A^{**}p_{\rm nor} r_B .
\]
The simultaneous $M$ and $JMJ$ version is obtained by applying the same
construction in the two-normal category recorded in DKEP Proposition 2.10.  In
particular, for subalgebra boundaries $X_B$ and their associated $K_{X_B}$ one
may write the normal support as $p r_{X_B}$, where $r_{X_B}$ is the ordinary
open support, provided one has first checked that $X_B$ and $K_{X_B}$ have the
same annihilator in $B(L^2M)^{\sharp_J}$.  This annihilator equality follows
from DKEP Proposition 3.6 plus Proposition 2.4: $K_X^{\infty,1}$ is the
simultaneous strong bimodule closure generated by $X$, and normal functionals
in the simultaneous sharp dual vanish on this strong closure exactly when they
vanish on $X$.

For an expected inclusion $B\subset N$, the coefficient boundary of
\[
  {}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N
\]
is $X_B$.  The clean proof avoids claiming that Hilbert-space rank-one
operators generate $B(L^2B)$.  If $H={}_NL^2N_B$, DKEP Example 5.11 already
identifies the coefficient boundary of $H$ with $X_B$.  For the fused
correspondence,
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^*T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
  = T_\xi^*(T_{\bar\alpha}^*T_{\bar\beta})T_\eta ,
\]
and the middle operator lies in the allowed $e_BB(L^2N)e_B\cong B(L^2B)$
factor from Example 3.4, so all fused coefficients lie in $X_B$.  Taking
$\alpha=\beta=\widehat 1_B$ recovers the generators coming from $H$, so the
hereditary algebras agree.


For uses of DKEP Theorem 5.10 with direct sums, record the hypothesis in terms
of both left and right coefficient boundary pieces being contained in the same
boundary $X$; for the graph-product correspondences $H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M$
this is automatic because $H_U$ is self-conjugate and Lemma~\ref{lem:coeff}-type
arguments identify both sides with $X_{M_U}$.

For the boundary intersection, the limiting argument should be done in the
ordinary bidual $B(L^2M)^{**}$.  If coefficient-type approximate units
$h_\alpha\to r_W$ and $k_\beta\to r_U$ strongly* satisfy
$h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}(M)$, then the hereditary
corner supported by $r_{K_{U\cap W}}$ is strongly* closed; hence
$r_Wr_Ur_W\in r_{K_{U\cap W}}A^{**}r_{K_{U\cap W}}$.  Compressing by the
simultaneous normal support gives $q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}$.  The crucial
input for $h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}$ is CDHJKN
Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5: finite fusions of graph-product
correspondences with labels $A_1,\ldots,A_n$ decompose into direct sums of
$H_C=L^2M\otimes_{M_C}L^2M$ with
$C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n$.

In the finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary step, one only needs the canonical
ucp map supplied in DKEP Lemma 8.5 from $B(L^2N)$ into the compact normal
corner $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$, with $\Theta_0(1)=q_0$ and
$\Theta_0(x)=xq_0$ for $x\in N$.  If a central state on $\widetilde S(N)$ is
forced to take value one on $q_0$, composing with this map gives a Connes
hypertrace, contradicting nonamenability.



\section*{Refined normal-support and fusion bridge}

A safer formulation of the support bookkeeping avoids ordinary supports except
when needed for intuition.  For a hereditary \(Y\subset B(L^2N)\) which is
simultaneously invariant for \(N\) and \(JNJ\), let \(s(Y)\) be the identity of
the normal corner \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\subset (B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) given by
DKEP Proposition 2.10.  Then
\[
   (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=s(Y)\mathcal A_Ns(Y)
\]
and any contractive approximate unit of \(Y\) converges ultra-strongly* to
\(s(Y)\) in \(\mathcal A_N\).  For a boundary piece \(X\), DKEP Proposition
2.4 plus Proposition 3.6 imply that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same
annihilator in \(B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J}\): a simultaneous normal functional
vanishing on \(X\) vanishes on the simultaneous strong bimodule closure
\(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)=\overline X^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}
=\overline {K_X(N)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}\), hence on \(K_X(N)\).  Thus
\(s(X)=s(K_X(N))=q_X\).  This equality is what permits approximate units from
the original coefficient boundary \(X\) to converge to DKEP's compact-corner
support \(q_X\).

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), let
\[
   \mathcal H_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
The coefficient of \(\widehat1\otimes\widehat1\) is \(e_B\); hence \(X_B\), the
hereditary algebra generated by \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-translates of
\(e_BB(L^2N)e_B\), is hereditarily generated by positive coefficients of
\(\mathcal H_B\).  To control arbitrary bounded coefficients of the fused
correspondence, the robust argument is not to approximate arbitrary bounded
vectors by elementary tensors in operator norm.  Instead use DKEP Theorem 5.10:
for \(H={}_NL^2N_B\), Example 5.11 gives left boundary \(X_B\) and right
boundary \(B(L^2B)\); the conjugate has the reversed boundaries.  Elementary
tensors \(\xi\otimes\bar\eta\) form a cyclic set in
\(H\otimes_B\bar H\) with
\[
   T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta}=T_{\bar\eta}T_\xi
      \in X_BB(L^2N)X_B .
\]
Theorem 5.10 then gives \(T_\zeta\in K_{X_B,X_B}\) for every bounded
\(\zeta\in\mathcal H_B\), and DKEP Proposition 5.9 gives
\(T_\zeta^*T_\theta\in K_{X_B}(N)\).  Direct sums are handled by taking the
union of the summand cyclic sets; finite fusions by the identity
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) and heredity \(XB(L^2N)X\subset X\).

For products of finite-fusion coefficients, with the convention
\(T_{\bar\eta}=T_\eta^*\), if
\(c_1=T_\xi^*T_\eta\) is a coefficient of a finite fusion \(K\) and
\(c_2=T_\alpha^*T_\beta\) one of \(L\), then
\[
   c_1c_2=T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{\,*}T_\beta,
\]
viewing the two vectors in the finite direct sum
\((K\otimes\bar K\otimes L)\oplus L\).  This proves that the span of
coefficients of finite direct sums of finite fusions is a \(*\)-algebra.
Since \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by positive coefficients of
\(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), functional calculus on finite positive sums of
these coefficients gives a contractive approximate unit for \(X_{M_U}\) in the
norm closure of positive elements \(c^*c\) from that \(*\)-algebra.

In the graph-product support intersection, if \(h\) is from the positive
finite-fusion cone for \(W\) and \(k\) from that for \(U\), then \(hkh\) is a
finite sum of coefficients of fusions whose labels are all \(W\)'s and \(U\)'s.
CDHJKN's fusion rule decomposes these as direct sums of
\(H_C=L^2M\otimes_{M_C}L^2M\) with \(C\subset W\cap U\).  Since
\(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_{W\cap U}}\), the direct-sum/fusion mixing result puts
\(hkh\) in \(K_{X_{M_{W\cap U}}}(M)\).  Passing ultra-strongly to support
projections in \(\mathcal A_M\) gives
\[
   0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{W\cap U}.
\]

For the relative Bass--Serre step \(N=P*_BQ\), the projections \(p,q\) onto
first-letter \(P\)- and \(Q\)-reduced words satisfy, for \(x\in P\ominus B\),
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0,
\]
with the symmetric formula for \(x\in Q\ominus B\).  These commutators lie in
\(X_B\), so \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).  If \(w_1,w_2\in P\) have
\(E_B(w_i)=E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\), then \(w_iqL^2N\subset pL^2N\) and
\(q w_1^*w_2 q=0\), while a centered unitary \(a\in Q\ominus B\) gives
\(apa^*\le q\).  Any central state then satisfies \(2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)
\le\varphi(q)\).  The unitary \(w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar, so the conjugates of
\(e_B\) are pairwise orthogonal and force \(\varphi(e_B)=0\), contradicting
\(1=e_B+p+q\).




\section*{Caution: products must be taken in the ordinary bidual}

The canonical map \(B(L^2M)\to (B(L^2M)^{\sharp_J})^*\) should not be used as
though it were multiplicative.  In the support-intersection proof, after
establishing \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_{W\cap U}}}(M)\) for actual operators
\(h,k\in B(L^2M)\), the limiting argument should therefore take place in the
ordinary bidual \(B(L^2M)^{**}\).  Let \(p\) be DKEP's simultaneous normal
support, \(r_U\) the ordinary open support of \(X_{M_U}\), and \(r_{K_I}\) the
ordinary support of \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).  DKEP Section 2.3 and Proposition 2.10
give \(r_Up=pr_U\) and \(q_U=pr_U\) (using the equality of supports for
\(X_{M_U}\) and \(K_{X_{M_U}}\)).  Approximate units from the finite-fusion
positive cones converge strongly* to \(r_W,r_U\) in the ordinary bidual, and
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in r_{K_I}B(L^2M)^{**}r_{K_I}\).  Strong* closure
of this hereditary corner gives \(r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_I}\).  Only then compress:
\[
  q_Wq_Uq_W=pr_W\,pr_U\,pr_W=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p\le pr_{K_I}p=q_I.
\]
This avoids assuming any multiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding.


\section*{Normal-support and \(T_\xi\)-operator calculus}

For an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence \(H\), DKEP use bounded-vector operators
\(T_\xi\in B(L^2M,L^2N)\).  The crucial functorial rules are
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes_N\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*.
\]
Thus, for an \(M\)-\(M\) correspondence, operators \(T_\xi\) attached to
bounded vectors in finite fusions form a concrete multiplicative calculus:
products are represented by fusing the underlying bounded vectors, and adjoints
by conjugating the correspondence.  It is safer to use the linear span of these
operators, rather than the span of ordinary coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\),
when proving a star-algebra statement.  Ordinary positive coefficients are
still of the form \(t^*t\) with \(t=T_\xi\).

For a subalgebra boundary \(X_B\), the reliable coefficient-generation
argument uses DKEP Example 5.11.  With \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and
\(\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_B\overline H\),
\[
  T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta .
\]
Example 5.11 identifies \(X_B\) with the hereditary algebra generated by
these operators.  Therefore \(X_B\) is also hereditarily generated by the
positive operators \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\), where \(\alpha\) ranges over
bounded finite elementary sums in \(\mathcal H_B\): if
\(s=T_{\eta\otimes\bar\xi}\), then both \(s^*s\) and \(ss^*\) are of this
positive form, and a hereditary algebra containing both contains \(s\).

For the boundary-support intersection, approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\) can
be chosen from the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_U\}\), where
\(\mathcal T_U\) is the span of \(T_\xi\)'s for bounded vectors in non-empty
finite fusions of \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) and its conjugate.  If
\(h\) is built from \(\mathcal T_W\) and \(k\) from \(\mathcal T_U\), every
monomial in the expansion of \(hkh\) is a single \(T_\zeta\) for a finite
fusion whose label list includes both \(W\) and \(U\).  CDHJKN's fusion rule
then decomposes it into \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset W\cap U\); DKEP Theorem 5.10
and Proposition 5.9 put these \(T_\zeta\)'s in \(K_{X_{M_{W\cap U}}}(M)\).

For the support bookkeeping, the working principle is: if \(Y\subset
B(L^2N)\) is hereditary and invariant under the two multiplier actions, then
DKEP's simultaneous-normal bidual includes \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) as the corner
cut out by the ordinary open support \(r_Y\) compressed by the simultaneous
normal support \(p\).  Hence \(q_Y=pr_Y\).  The equality \(q_X=q_{K_X}\) follows
because \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same simultaneous strong closure
\(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), so they have the same simultaneous-normal annihilator.



\section*{Coefficient generation for subalgebra boundaries}

For an expected inclusion $B\subset N$, the safe way to generate the
subalgebra boundary is through DKEP Example 5.11, not through an attempted
closed formula for positive coefficients of translated Jones vectors.  Let
$H={}_NL^2N_B$ and $\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_B\overline H$.  Example 5.11 gives
\[
  X_B=\operatorname{her}\{T_\xi^*T_\eta:\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\}.
\]
Using the fusion identity,
\[
  T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta,
\]
so the generators of $X_B$ are ordinary coefficients $T_\alpha$ of
$\mathcal H_B$ for bounded elementary tensors $\alpha$.  If $Y$ is the
hereditary algebra generated by $T_\alpha^*T_\alpha$ for bounded finite
elementary sums $\alpha\in\mathcal H_B$, then every generator
$s=T_{\eta\otimes\bar\xi}$ of $X_B$ satisfies $s^*s\in Y$ and
$ss^*\in Y$ (the latter is the positive coefficient of
$\xi\otimes\bar\eta$), hence $s\in Y$.  Conversely the positive generators
belong to $X_B$.  Therefore $X_B=Y$.

This avoids the tempting but generally false identity
$T_{x\zeta_By}^*T_{x\zeta_By}=xJyJe_BJy^*Jx^*$ for
$\zeta_B=1\otimes_B1$.  For example, when $B=\mathbb C$ and $x,y$ are
unitaries in a noncommutative finite factor, the actual positive coefficient
of $x\otimes y$ is $e_{\mathbb C}$, while the displayed rank-one projection is
usually different.



\section*{Positive generation of the subalgebra boundary without linearity}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and
\(\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_B\overline H\).  DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the
subalgebra boundary \(X_B\) with the hereditary algebra generated by
\[
  \mathcal E_B=\{T_\xi^*T_\eta:\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\}.
\]
Using the fusion identity,
\[
  T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta,
\]
so \(\mathcal E_B\) is exactly the set of coefficient operators of elementary
bounded tensors in \(H\otimes_B\bar H\).

One must not use linearity of \(\alpha\mapsto T_\alpha\): in general
\(T_{\xi+\eta}=T_\xi+T_\eta+L_\xi^*R_\eta+L_\eta^*R_\xi\).  The correct
positive-generation statement is instead:
\(X_B\) is the hereditary algebra generated by \(s^*s\), \(s\in\mathcal E_B\).
Indeed \(\mathcal E_B\) is self-adjoint.  If \(Y\) is the hereditary algebra
generated by these positives and \(r\) is its open support in
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)^{**}\), then \(s^*s,ss^*\in Y\) imply
\((1-r)s=0=s(1-r)\), so \(s=rsr\in Y\).  Hence the hereditary algebra generated
by \(\mathcal E_B\) lies in \(Y\), and the reverse inclusion is immediate.

Thus approximate units for \(X_B\) may be constructed from finite positive sums
of \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) with \(\alpha\) elementary.  If \(\mathcal T\) is the
coefficient \(*\)-algebra generated by the relevant finite-fusion coefficients,
such a finite sum \(s=\sum t_i^*t_i\) lies in \(\mathcal T_+\); for
\(g_\varepsilon(x)=x(x+\varepsilon)^{-1}\), the element \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) is
a norm limit of \(p_n(s)^*p_n(s)\) with \(p_n(0)=0\), hence is in the norm
closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T\}\).  The net of these \(g_\varepsilon(s)\)'s
is a contractive approximate unit for \(X_B\).



\section*{Finite-boundary criterion: central state extension nuance}

In the palindromic finite-boundary criterion, the central state \(\omega\) is
originally defined on \(\widetilde S(N)\).  Since the boundary supports \(q_i\)
commute with \(JNJ\), their palindromic product
\(R=q_m\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_m\) also lies in \(\widetilde S(N)\).  An
arbitrary state extension to \(\mathcal A_N\) is useful to speak about a GNS
vector fixed by the projections \(q_i\) after \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\), yielding
\(\omega(R)=1\) and hence \(\omega(q_0)=1\) when \(R\le q_0\).  However the
extension need not be \(N\)-central.  The final hypertrace must therefore use
only values on \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\subset\widetilde S(N)\), where the original
\(\omega\) is still defined and central.  The canonical DKEP u.c.p. map
\(\Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) has range in this corner, so
\(\omega\circ\Theta_0\) is \(N\)-central by bimodularity of \(\Theta_0\) and
centrality of the original \(\omega\).



\section*{Normal-bidual support via weak-* closure}

For a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra \(Y\subset A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) invariant
under the multiplier actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), avoid comparing the
ordinary open support in \(A_N^{**}\) with DKEP's simultaneous-normal support.
Let
\[
Y^{\sharp_J}=Y^{N\sharp N}\cap Y^{JNJ\sharp JNJ},\qquad
\mathcal A_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^* .
\]
Restriction \(\rho:A_N^{\sharp_J}\to Y^{\sharp_J}\) is a quotient map by the
DKEP Hahn--Banach extension theorem for simultaneous normal functionals.
Hence \(\rho^*:(Y^{\sharp_J})^*\to\mathcal A_N\) has range
\[
(\ker\rho)^\perp
=\{\text{functionals in }\mathcal A_N \text{ annihilating every }
\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\text{ vanishing on }Y\}
=\overline{Y}^{\,\sigma(\mathcal A_N,A_N^{\sharp_J})}.
\]
This weak-* closure \(W\) is hereditary because \(YA_NY\subset Y\), \(A_N\)
is weak-* dense in \(\mathcal A_N\), and multiplication in \(\mathcal A_N\) is
separately weak-* continuous; hence \(W\mathcal A_NW\subset W\).  Thus
\(W=q_Y\mathcal A_Nq_Y\) for a projection \(q_Y\).  Multiplier invariance gives
\(nW,Wn\subset W\) for \(n\in N\cup JNJ\), and applying this to the unit \(q_Y\)
of \(W\) gives \(nq_Y=q_Yn=q_Ynq_Y\).  Any contractive approximate unit of
\(Y\) converges weak-* to the unit of \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\), hence to \(q_Y\) in
\(\mathcal A_N\).

For a boundary piece \(X\), DKEP Corollary 3.2 and Proposition 3.6 say that
\(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same simultaneous strong closure (the same
\(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure).  DKEP Proposition 2.4 identifies this with
having the same annihilator in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\).  Therefore \(X\) and
\(K_X(N)\) have the same weak-* closure in \(\mathcal A_N\), and approximate
units of \(X\) converge to the DKEP support \(q_X\) of \(K_X(N)\).  This is the
right substitute for the tempting but delicate formula \(q_X=pr_X\) involving
ordinary bidual open supports.

\section*{Intersection calculation without ordinary support projections}

Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  Use approximate units \(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and
\(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\) lying in the norm closures of
\(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_W\}\) and \(\{s^*s:s\in\mathcal T_U\}\).  The previous
support note gives \(h_\alpha\to q_W\), \(k_\beta\to q_U\) ultraweakly in
\(\mathcal A_M\).

The key algebraic assertion is: if \(a,c\) are in the \(W\)-positive closure
and \(b\) in the \(U\)-positive closure, then \(abc\in K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}(M)\).
For elementary squares,
\[
t_1^*t_1s^*st_2^*t_2
\]
expands into linear combinations of words
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}.
\]
Replace adjoints by conjugate correspondences and repeatedly use
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\).  Each word is a coefficient \(T_\zeta\)
of a bounded vector in a finite fusion whose labels include both \(W\) and
\(U\).  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 decompose such a fusion into a
direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset U\cap W\).  The finite-fusion mixing
lemma plus monotonicity gives \(T_\zeta\in K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}(M)\).  Norm
closedness handles the closures.

Thus \(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\in q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}\) for
independent indices \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\).  Taking iterated ultraweak limits
(\(\gamma\), then \(\beta\), then \(\alpha\)) and using separate normality of
multiplication in the von Neumann algebra \(\mathcal A_M\) gives
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W\in q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
As \(q_Wq_Uq_W\) is a positive contraction, this implies
\(0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}\).  This proves the palindromic finite-boundary
criterion by induction and avoids all ordinary-bidual support compression.


\section*{Ordinary-bidual support compression}

DKEP's normal bidual \(\mathcal A_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) should be
used through its ordinary-bidual realization.  Let \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
let \(p_1,p_2\in A_N^{**}\) be the normal projections for the \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-bimodule structures.  These lie in the biduals of commuting
multiplier algebras and commute; \(p_N=p_1p_2\) is the unit of the
simultaneous-normal enveloping von Neumann algebra.  If \(Y\subset A_N\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra
invariant under the \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-multiplier actions, and \(r_Y\) is its
ordinary open support in \(A_N^{**}\), then DKEP's support discussion before
Remark 2.8, together with Proposition 2.10 in the simultaneous category, gives
\[
        r_Yp_N=p_Nr_Y,\qquad (Y^{\sharp_J})^*
        \cong r_Yp_NA_N^{**}p_Nr_Y .
\]
Thus the normal support of \(Y\) is \(p_Nr_Y\).  For a boundary piece \(X\), the
normal supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) agree because DKEP Proposition 3.6 and
Proposition 2.4 identify their simultaneous-normal annihilators; hence
\[
        q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X,
\]
where \(r_X\) and \(\rho_X\) are the ordinary supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\).

This is the safe way to pass from concrete coefficient products to products of
normal support projections: do all limiting multiplication in the ordinary
bidual \(A_N^{**}\), where \(A_N\hookrightarrow A_N^{**}\) is a
\(*\)-homomorphism, and only afterwards compress by \(p_N\).  For graph-product
subalgebra boundaries, if products \(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\) with
\(h_\alpha,h_\gamma\) from an approximate unit of \(X_{M_W}\) and
\(k_\beta\) from an approximate unit of \(X_{M_U}\) lie in \(K_{X_{M_{U\cap
W}}}(M)\), then
\[
        r_Wr_Ur_W\le \rho_{U\cap W}
\]
inside \(A_M^{**}\), and therefore
\[
        q_Wq_Uq_W=p_Mr_Wr_Ur_Wp_M\le p_M\rho_{U\cap W}=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This avoids any assumption that the canonical map
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\to\mathcal A_N\) is multiplicative.

\section*{Expanded technical details for the final proof}

The DKEP simultaneous-normal bidual should be used through its ordinary
bidual model.  If \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), the one-normal supports for the
left \(N\)- and right \(JNJ\)-module structures are denoted \(p_\ell,p_r\).
DKEP Proposition 2.10 realizes
\[
 (A^{\sharp_J})^*=p_\ell p_r A^{**}p_\ell p_r
\]
inside \(A^{**}\); in particular the two supports commute.  For an invariant
hereditary subalgebra \(Y\subset A\) with ordinary open support \(r_Y\), the
one-normal support discussion preceding DKEP Remark 2.8 gives
\[
 (Y^\sharp)^*=r_Y p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}r_Y.
\]
Applying this for the two normal structures and then using Proposition 2.10
gives
\[
 (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=r_Y p_\ell p_r A^{**}p_\ell p_r r_Y,
\]
so the support in the simultaneous normal bidual is \(p_Nr_Y\), \(p_N=p_\ell
p_r\).  Approximate units of \(Y\) converge weak* to \(r_Y\) in the ordinary
bidual, and all multiplicative support-intersection limits should be taken in
\(A^{**}\) before compressing by \(p_N\).

For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\), DKEP Proposition 3.6 says that \(X\) and
\(K_X(N)\) have the same \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure.  Proposition 2.4
identifies \(A^{\sharp_J}\) with the functionals continuous for this topology.
Thus \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same preannihilator in \(A^{\sharp_J}\);
the weak* closed normal corners they generate in \((A^{\sharp_J})^*\) have the
same unit.  If \(r_X,\rho_X\) are the ordinary supports of \(X,K_X(N)\), then
\(q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X\).

The direct-sum stability of DKEP mixing is best justified through the
cyclic-vector criterion rather than by finite-support norm approximation of
arbitrary bounded vectors.  If \(\mathcal H_j\) are mixing relative to
\(X\times X\) and \(\Sigma_j\) are bounded cyclic sets satisfying Theorem 5.10,
then \(\bigcup_j\iota_j(\Sigma_j)\) is cyclic in \(\bigoplus_j\mathcal H_j\).
Cross coefficients vanish and same-summand coefficients are \(X\)-compact, so
Theorem 5.10 applies to the whole Hilbert direct sum.  The theorem then
controls every bounded vector in the direct sum; no assertion that bounded
vectors are norm limits of finite-support vectors is needed.

For the intersection lemma, if \(t_1,t_2\in\mathcal T_W\) and
\(s\in\mathcal T_U\), a summand in
\(t_1^*t_1s^*st_2^*t_2\) has form
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}.
\]
Replacing adjoints by conjugates and using
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), this is \(T_\zeta\), where \(\zeta\) is a
bounded vector in a finite fusion whose labels include both \(W\) and \(U\).
The CDHJKN fusion rule decomposes this fusion into a Hilbert direct sum of
\(H_C=L^2M\otimes_{M_C}L^2M\) with \(C\subset W\cap U\).  The \(M\)-bimodular
unitary implementing the decomposition preserves boundedness of vectors.  Since
\(C\subset I=W\cap U\) implies \(e_{M_C}\le e_{M_I}\), one has
\(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\), and the DKEP cyclic criterion plus direct-sum
stability gives \(T_\zeta\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).

In the finite-relative-boundaries argument, the projections \(q_i\), \(q_0\),
and any product of them commute with \(JNJ\), so they lie in
\(\widetilde S(N)\); extending a state from \(\widetilde S(N)\) to
\(\mathcal A_N\) is only a GNS device and does not affect their values.  If an
\(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) satisfies
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\), then in the GNS representation of any extension the
projection \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector.  Hence the palindromic product
\(R=(q_1\cdots q_m)^*(q_1\cdots q_m)\) has value \(1\).

For the AFP Bass--Serre calculation, with
\(L^2N=L^2B\oplus\bigoplus H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n}\), the
projection \(p\) onto first-letter \(P\)-words and \(q\) onto first-letter
\(Q\)-words satisfy \(1=e_B+p+q\).  For \(x\in P\ominus B\), right
multiplication by \(x^*\) sends \(L^2B\) into first-letter \(P\), preserves
first-letter \(Q\), and preserves first-letter \(P\) except for the possible
\(B\)-component arising from length-one \(P\)-words.  Therefore
\[
[p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.
\]
The symmetric statement holds for \(x\in Q\ominus B\).  DKEP Lemma 6.1 then
upgrades the generator check to all of \(N\), proving \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).



\section*{Support bookkeeping in the ordinary bidual}

Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  For the two normal structures (left \(N\) and
right \(JNJ\)) let \(p_\ell,p_r\in A^{**}\) be the one-normal support
projections from DKEP's construction.  In the one-normal case \(p_\ell\in
N^{**}\) and \(p_r\in(JNJ)^{**}\).  Since \(N\) and \(JNJ\) commute concretely
in \(A\), their bidual images commute in \(A^{**}\) (take weak-* limits of
bounded nets and use separate weak-* continuity of multiplication).  Hence
\(p=p_\ell p_r\) is a projection.  DKEP Proposition 2.10 says that the
simultaneous normal bidual is the intersection of the two one-normal biduals
inside \(A^{**}\); because \(p_\ell,p_r\) commute, this intersection is
\(pA^{**}p\).

If \(Y\subset A\) is hereditary, invariant under both multiplier actions, and
the induced representations of \(N\) and \(JNJ\) in \(M(Y)\) are faithful, then
the one-normal support discussion before DKEP Remark 2.8 applies separately to
\(Y\).  If \(r_Y\) is the ordinary open support of \(Y\), then \(r_Y\) commutes
with both \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\), and the simultaneous normal bidual of \(Y\)
is the corner
\[
   (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)A^{**}(pr_Y)\subset pA^{**}p.
\]
Thus its support inside \(\mathcal A_N=(A^{\sharp_J})^*\) is \(pr_Y\), and
ordinary approximate units of \(Y\) converge weak-* to \(r_Y\).

For DKEP boundary pieces, \(q_X\) is defined using \(K_X\), so \(q_X=p\rho_X\)
where \(\rho_X\) is the ordinary support of \(K_X\).  In the subalgebra
boundary pieces used in the graph-product proof, the multiplier actions are
faithful because \(a e_B=0\) implies \(E_B(a^*a)=0\), hence \(a=0\), and the
right action is detected on \(e_B\widehat 1\).  To compare \(X\) and \(K_X\),
use the concrete construction \(K_X=(K_X^L)^*K_X^L\), with \(K_X^L\) the
\(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}\)-closure of \(\mathbb B(L^2N)X\).  Corollary 3.2 puts
each \(S\in K_X^L\) in the strong right \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-module generated by
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)X\).  After refining projection partitions, for \(S,R\in
K_X^L\) one has \(Se,Re\in \mathbb B(L^2N)X\) on each product projection \(e\)
from \(N\) and \(JNJ\), and therefore
\[
  eS^*Rf=(Se)^*(Rf)\in X\mathbb B(L^2N)X\subset X
\]
for product projections \(e,f\).  Proposition 2.4 then puts \(S^*R\), and hence
all of \(K_X\), in the simultaneous strong \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule closure
of \(X\).  Thus every simultaneous normal functional vanishing on \(X\)
vanishes on \(K_X\), and the converse follows from \(X\subset K_X\).  Therefore
\(X\) and \(K_X\) have the same simultaneous-normal preannihilator, and their
supports in \(\mathcal A_N\) are equal:
\[
   p r_X=p\rho_X=q_X.
\]

This is the support identity needed to pass from products of concrete
approximate units in graph-product boundary pieces to palindromic products of
DKEP normal-bidual support projections.

\section*{Support comparison via simultaneous strong modules}

For a boundary piece \(X\subset A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), the equality of the
normal-bidual supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) can be proved without appealing
to an informal common refinement of unrelated partitions.  Let
\(K_X^L=\overline{A_NX}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}}\), so
\(K_X(N)=(K_X^L)^*K_X^L\).  DKEP Corollary 3.2 identifies \(K_X^L\) with the
simultaneous strong right \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-module generated by \(A_NX\).
For \(S,R\in K_X^L\), apply the projection criterion to the column
\(\binom SR\).  This gives one family of cuts \(d_{\alpha}\) (products of
projections from \(N\) and \(JNJ\), with \(\sum d_\alpha=1\)) such that
\(Sd_\alpha,Rd_\alpha\in A_NX\) for all \(\alpha\).  Then
\[
  d_\alpha S^*Rd_\beta=(Sd_\alpha)^*(Rd_\beta)\in XA_NX\subset X.
\]
The two-sided simultaneous strong-bimodule criterion puts \(S^*R\) in the
strong \(N\)-\(N\) and \(JNJ\)-\(JNJ\) bimodule generated by \(X\).  Therefore
every functional in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\) that annihilates \(X\) annihilates
\(K_X(N)\), and the converse is immediate from \(X\subset K_X(N)\).  Thus the
included normal bidual corners have the same support.

\section*{Cyclic-vector fusion calculus}

DKEP Theorem 5.10 should be applied through condition (1).  For an
\(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(H\) mixing relative to \(X\times X\), the bounded
vectors with \(T_\xi\in XA_NX\) form a cyclic set.  Direct sums use these
vectors in one summand; finite-support vectors are dense.  For a fusion
\(H\otimes_NK\), elementary tensors of cyclic vectors are cyclic and
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\in (XA_NX)(XA_NX)\subset XA_NX
\]
because \(X\) is hereditary.  Conjugation uses \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\).  Hence
finite fusions, conjugates and direct sums remain mixing, and Theorem 5.10(3)
plus Proposition 5.9 gives coefficient operators of arbitrary bounded vectors
in \(K_X(N)\).

In the graph-product intersection lemma, a monomial
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}
\]
is \(T_\zeta\) for
\[
\zeta=\alpha_4\otimes\bar\alpha_3\otimes\beta_2\otimes
\bar\beta_1\otimes\alpha_2\otimes\bar\alpha_1
\]
(up to distributing finite direct sums).  CDHJKN fusion then decomposes
\(\zeta\) into summands labelled by subsets of the intersection of all labels
appearing in the monomial.

\section*{AFP first-letter projections}

For \(N=P*_BQ\), the normal form is indexed by alternating words
\(i_j\ne i_{j+1}\).  If \(p,q\) are projections onto reduced words with first
letter \(P,Q\), then for \(x\in P\ominus B\),
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0,
\]
and symmetrically for \(x\in Q\ominus B\).  Since \(E_B(x)=0\), the displayed
terms are translates of \(e_B\) and lie in \(X_B\).  DKEP Lemma 6.1, in the
right-action convention \(x\cdot T\cdot y=Jy^*JTJx^*J\), makes the set of
\(x\)'s with \(X_B\)-compact commutators a von Neumann algebra; hence \(p,q\in
S_{X_B}(N)\).




\section*{Support comparison for \(X\) and \(K_X\)}

For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), the clean way to
identify the simultaneous-normal support of \(X\) with that of \(K_X(N)\) is to
avoid one-sided \(K_X^L\) cutting arguments.  DKEP Proposition 3.6 gives
\[
        K_X(N)\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(N)=\overline X^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}.
\]
DKEP Theorem 5.6 (with \(M=N\) and \(X=Y\)) says that if
\(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), then every \(\sharp_J\)-normal functional on
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\) vanishing on \(X\,\mathbb B(L^2N)\,X\) vanishes on \(T\).
Since \(X\) is hereditary, \(X\mathbb B(L^2N)X\subset X\).  Therefore any
simultaneous-normal functional vanishing on \(X\) also vanishes on \(K_X(N)\).
The converse annihilator inclusion is immediate from DKEP's \(X\subset K_X(N)\)
(visible in Proposition 3.6's proof and the construction of \(K_X\)).  Thus
\[
        \operatorname{Ann}_{A^{\sharp_J}}(X)
        =\operatorname{Ann}_{A^{\sharp_J}}(K_X(N)),
\]
and the corresponding weak-* corners in
\((A^{\sharp_J})^*\cong p_NA^{**}p_N\) have the same support.  Hence if \(r_X\)
and \(\rho_X\) are the ordinary open supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\),
\[
        q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X.
\]
This is the support identity needed in the boundary-intersection argument.

\section*{Direct sums in the boundary-intersection coefficient computation}

When a graph-product fusion decomposes as a direct sum of \(H_C=L^2M\otimes_{M_C}L^2M\)
with \(C\subset I\), it is safer not to expand an arbitrary vector linearly,
because \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\) is quadratic in \(\xi\).  Instead: each
\(H_C\) is mixing relative to \(X_{M_C}\) by the subalgebra-boundary lemma, and
monotonicity \(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\) makes the same cyclic vectors good for
\(X_{M_I}\).  Direct sums of correspondences mixing relative to the same
boundary piece are mixing by the DKEP Theorem 5.10 cyclic-vector criterion.
Then Proposition 5.9 gives the coefficient of the whole direct-sum vector in
\(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).

I find the revised proof mathematically complete, modulo only minor expository clarifications that do not affect validity.

## 2. Literature cross-check

The cited DKEP and CDHJKN results exist and are being used in the right parts of the proof.

DKEP define boundary pieces as hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebras and note that nonzero boundary pieces contain \(\mathbb K(L^2M)\); Example 3.4 is exactly the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Proposition 3.6 identifies \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) as both the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(X\) and of \(K_X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.6 gives the annihilator characterization used in the revision, and its condition (4) is precisely the relevant simultaneous-normal functional condition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Proposition 2.10 and the preceding support discussion justify the normal-bidual corner formalism for simultaneous normality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

DKEP Example 5.11 identifies coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences, including the subalgebra case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9 support the mixing-to-compactness arguments for bounded vectors in correspondences. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 6.1 and Theorem 6.2 support the Bass–Serre/proper-proximality part. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 8.5 is correctly invoked for the normal-bidual criterion in the factor case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

CDHJKN provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used in the boundary-intersection lemma. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 states that a join-irreducible graph product with \(|V|\ge 3\) and state-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

## 3. Previous concerns and whether they were resolved

### Previous fatal concern: support comparison \(q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X\)

This is now adequately repaired. The earlier proof relied on a questionable assertion that \(K_X^L\) was exactly the simultaneous strong right module generated by \(A_NX\). The revised proof instead uses:

\[
K_X(N)\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(N)=\overline X^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}},
\]
from DKEP Proposition 3.6, and then DKEP Theorem 5.6 to show that every simultaneous-normal functional vanishing on \(X A_N X\) vanishes on \(K_X(N)\). Since \(X\) is hereditary, vanishing on \(X\) implies vanishing on \(X A_N X\). This proves equality of the simultaneous-normal annihilators of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\), and therefore equality of the corresponding normal-bidual supports.

A tiny expository improvement would be to explicitly say that, for hereditary \(X\subset A_N\), \(X A_N X\) is norm dense in, indeed essentially recovers, \(X\). But the current argument is already sufficient because it only needs \(X A_N X\subset X\) for the annihilation implication, and DKEP Theorem 5.6 is applied to the relevant \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure. This is not a remaining gap.

### Previous concern: nonmultiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding

Resolved. The revised proof consistently performs multiplicative support computations inside the ordinary bidual \(A_N^{**}\) and only then compresses by \(p_N\). This avoids treating the canonical map \(A_N\to (A_N^{\sharp_J})^*\) as multiplicative, which DKEP explicitly warns is generally false. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### Previous concern: direct sums and nonlinearity of \(\xi\mapsto T_\xi\)

Resolved. The revised proof now states the \(*\)-algebra property of \(\mathcal T_U\) term-by-term and no longer relies on false linearity of \(\xi\mapsto T_\xi\). The direct-sum step in the boundary-intersection lemma is also now handled through DKEP’s cyclic-vector/mixing criterion rather than by expanding arbitrary direct-sum vectors linearly.

## 4. Section-by-section audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The proof explicitly records the interpretation of irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This is appropriate and satisfies the instruction for ambiguous problem statements. CDHJKN use the same join-irreducibility convention. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

### Boundary supports and coefficients

The ordinary-bidual bookkeeping is now convincing. The proof correctly uses DKEP’s support projection \(p_{\rm nor}\) formalism and Proposition 2.10 to identify simultaneous-normal biduals as corners. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The revised comparison between \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is the main successful repair. It uses DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Theorem 5.6 in the correct direction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No fatal issue remains here.

### Subalgebra fusion lemma

The identification of \(X_B\) with the hereditary algebra generated by the coefficient operators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) is supported by DKEP Example 5.11. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The positive-generation argument using \(s^*s\) and \(ss^*\) is valid for hereditary subalgebras.

The direct-sum part is acceptable: the proof uses Theorem 5.10’s cyclic-vector criterion, and then Theorem 5.10(3) plus Proposition 5.9 to obtain compactness of coefficient operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No issue.

---

### Finite fusion calculus

The stability of mixing under finite Connes fusion and conjugation follows from the coefficient identities
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*,
\]
and the hereditary property \(X A_N X\subset X\). The direct-sum argument is now stated through cyclic sets and is adequate.

The construction of a contractive approximate unit for \(X_{M_U}\) from the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_U\}\) is valid. The polynomial functional-calculus argument is standard.

No issue.

---

### Boundary-support intersection lemma

This is the central technical lemma, and the revision now gives a complete argument.

The use of CDHJKN’s bimodule decomposition and fusion rule is appropriate. Their Theorem 5.4 decomposes \(M_{V_1}L^2(M)M_{V_2}\) into summands over \(U\subset V_1\cap V_2\), and Proposition 5.5 gives the corresponding fusion rule over intersections. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The product calculation
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4}
=
T_\zeta
\]
with \(\zeta\) in a finite fusion whose labels include both \(W\) and \(U\) is correct under the stated convention. The revised proof now explicitly explains why the resulting direct sum of \(H_C\)’s with \(C\subset I=U\cap W\) gives coefficient compactness relative to \(X_{M_I}\). This resolves the previous concern about direct sums.

The limiting argument in \(A^{**}\) is also correct: the products \(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\) lie in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), hence in the ordinary support corner \(\rho_I A^{**}\rho_I\); weak* closedness and separate weak* continuity give
\[
r_Wr_Ur_W\in \rho_I A^{**}\rho_I,
\]
and therefore
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I
\]
after compression by \(p\). This avoids all non-normal meet arguments and all nonmultiplicative-normal-bidual pitfalls.

No fatal issue remains.

---

### Finite relative boundaries to compact boundary

This lemma is sound.

The compression
\[
q^\perp \widetilde S_X(N)q^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
is valid because \(q_X\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\). DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the normal-bidual central-state criterion in the separable factor case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The GNS argument with the palindromic product is a good way to avoid invalid lattice-meet reasoning for non-normal states. The final hypertrace contradiction is standard: once \(\omega(q_0)=1\), the canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner yields a Connes hypertrace on \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\), contradicting nonamenability.

No issue.

---

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The AFP reduced-word calculation is correct. The first-letter projections \(p,q\) satisfy the displayed commutator formulas, and DKEP Lemma 6.1 justifies upgrading the generator check to all of \(N\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The paradoxical inequalities
\[
w_1 q w_1^*+w_2 q w_2^*\le p,\qquad a p a^*\le q
\]
are valid under the centeredness assumptions. The \(B\)-Haar unitary \(h=w_1a\) argument is also correct. DKEP Theorem 6.2 is the right criterion for excluding central states on \(S_{X_B}(N)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

No issue.

---

### Graph-product application

The vertex AFP decomposition
\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]
is standard for graph products.

The combinatorial choice of \(w_1,w_2\) is correct in both cases:

- If \(C(v)\) has two vertices, choose two trace-zero vertex unitaries outside \(L_v\).
- If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) produce \(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim r\), and \(w_1=u_r,\ w_2=u_su_r\) satisfy the required expectation conditions.

The intersection of all vertex links is empty, so the intersection lemma gives the palindromic product in the compact corner \(q_\emptyset\mathcal A_Mq_\emptyset\). Since \(M_\emptyset=\mathbb C\), DKEP Example 3.4 gives \(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives that \(M\) is a diffuse full factor under the stated hypotheses, and hence \(M\) is nonamenable because the diffuse amenable finite factor is hyperfinite and not full. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

No issue.
